problem_description: An orbiting space station is observed to remain always vertically above the same point on Earth. Where on Earth is the observer located? Describe the orbit of the space station as completely as possible, and determine the orbital radius for such an orbit. Use the following symbols and definitions for the solution: R is the orbital radius measured from Earth's center, R_0 is Earth's radius, M is Earth's mass, m is the mass of the space station, G is the universal gravitational constant, g is the gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface, v is the orbital speed of the station, and T is the orbital period. For a geostationary orbit, take T=24 hours. For numerical evaluation, use R_0=6.37×10^6 m and g=9.8 m/s^2. In the final answer, report the orbital radius R.

A person of mass m jumps from a height h and lands without bending his knees. After contact, he decelerates over a stopping distance s. Let g denote the acceleration due to gravity, f the total force on his legs during deceleration, E_1 the mechanical energy just before contact, and E_2 the work done by the force f during the stopping distance. Given m = 80\unit{kg}, h = 1\unit{m}, and s = 1/100\unit{m}, calculate f.

Consider a uniformly rotating spherical planet of mass M and radius R. The linear speed of a point on its equator is V. Let g_p denote the gravitational acceleration magnitude at the surface at the pole, and let the effective gravitational acceleration magnitude at the equator be g_e. It is given that g_e is one half of g_p due to rotation. Let G be the universal gravitational constant. A test particle of mass m is launched from the planet's pole. Let v_{esc} denote the escape speed from the pole. Let E denote the total mechanical energy and U the gravitational potential energy measured relative to zero at infinity. Determine v_{esc} as a multiple of V.

A small mass m rests at the edge of a horizontal disk of radius R. The coefficient of static friction between the mass and the disk is μ. The magnitude of gravitational acceleration is g. The disk is rotated about its axis until the mass slides off the disk and lands on the floor a vertical distance h below the disk, where h is measured in \unit{m}. Let D denote the horizontal distance traveled from the point where the mass leaves the disk to the landing point on the floor. Find D.

The Sun is at distance R = 25000 light years from the center of the galaxy and moves approximately in a circular orbit with period T = 170000000 years. The Earth is at distance r = 8 light minutes from the Sun and moves approximately in a circular orbit with period t = 1 year. Assume the galaxy's gravitational force on the Sun can be approximated by concentrating all galactic mass M at the galactic center. Let m be the mass of the Earth, m_s be the mass of the Sun, G be the gravitational constant, v be the Earth's orbital speed, and V be the Sun's orbital speed. Using only these data, find the approximate gravitational mass M of the galaxy in units of the Sun's mass m_s. Use the conversion 1 year = 525600 minutes, so 1 ly = 525600 light minutes.

A bicyclist experiences a total resistive force due to friction and air drag given by F = a V, where F is the magnitude of the resistive force, V is the bicyclist's speed, and a = 4 \unit{N s/m}. At maximum effort, the cyclist can supply mechanical power P = 600 \unit{W}. On level ground with no wind, the maximum steady speed v_max is the speed at which the propulsive force equals the resistive force. Determine v_max. Use F for force, V for speed, a for the proportionality constant, P for power, and v_max for the maximum steady speed.

A car of weight W = 5000 \unit{lb wt} travels at a steady speed of V_0 = 60 \unit{mph} on a level road. It is then shifted into neutral at t = 0 and allowed to coast. The speed as a function of time t (in seconds) is given by the coasting law below. Find the horsepower P_{req} required to maintain a steady speed of V_{req} = 30 \unit{mph} on the same road. Assume the resistive force inferred from the coasting data equals the steady resistive force at the same speed, and that at steady speed the propulsive force equals this resistance. Let m be the car's mass related to its weight by W = m g. Let F(V) denote the magnitude of the resistive force as a function of speed V, and assume at constant speed the engine propulsive force equals F(V). Useful constants and unit relations: g = 22 \unit{mph/s}, 1 \unit{H.P.} = 550 \unit{ft.lb wt/s}, 60 \unit{mph} = 88 \unit{ft/s}. The coasting law is
$$
V(t) = \frac{60}{1 + t/60} \unit{mph}
$$

A particle of mass m moves under two forces: a central force f1 and a linear (Stokes) friction force f2. Let r(t) denote the position vector, r=|r| its magnitude, v=dr/dt the velocity vector, and t the time. The central force has magnitude f(r) and direction along the radial unit vector r̂, and the friction is proportional to the velocity with positive coefficient λ. The forces are defined by the relations below. The motion lies in a plane; adopt plane polar coordinates (r, θ) in that plane, where θ is the polar angle and r̂ is the radial unit vector. Let J(t) denote the angular momentum vector about the origin and let J0 be the initial angular momentum at t=0. Find J(t) for all t≥0.

Calculate the ratio of the mean densities of the Earth and the Sun using the following approximate data. Define rho_e as the mean density of the Earth and rho_s as the mean density of the Sun. Define the target quantity eta by eta = rho_e / rho_s. Use the following data: theta = angular diameter of the Sun seen from the Earth = 1/2 \unit{deg}, l = length of 1 \unit{deg} of latitude on the Earth's surface = 100 \unit{km}, t = one year = 3 \times 10^7 \unit{s}, g = 10 \unit{m/s^2}. Let r be the Earth–Sun distance, M_e and M_s be the masses of the Earth and the Sun, R_e and R_s be their radii, G be the gravitational constant, omega be the orbital angular speed of the Earth about the Sun with omega = 2\pi / t, and m be the mass of a small test body at the Earth's surface. Infer the Earth's radius R_e from l via the meridional relation 2\pi R_e = 360 l. Use the small-angle relation in radians for the Sun's angular diameter, theta = 2 R_s / r.

A nonlinear oscillator with mass m and coordinate x has potential energy given by $$U(x)=\frac{k x^2}{2}-\frac{m \lambda x^3}{3}$$ where k>0 is the stiffness constant and \lambda is a small coupling parameter. The equation of motion is $$m \frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=-\frac{d U(x)}{d x}$$ Seek a solution to first order in \lambda using the perturbative expansion $$x_{(1)}=x_{(0)}+\lambda x_1$$ subject to the initial condition $$x(0)=0$$ Define $$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ For the zeroth-order motion use $$x_{(0)}=A \sin(\omega t+\varphi)$$ where A is an amplitude parameter and \varphi is a phase constant. In solving for x_1, you may use a particular integral of the form $$x_1^p=B+C \cos(2 \omega t)$$ with constants B and C, and a homogeneous part $$x_1^h=D_1 \sin(\omega t)+D_2 \cos(\omega t)$$ with constants D_1 and D_2. Define $$A'=A+\lambda D_1$$ Let the velocity be defined by $$v=\frac{d x}{d t}$$ Report the first-order solution x_{(1)}(t). Note that determining A' and A requires additional information such as the amplitude or the value of v(0).

A comet of mass m moves toward the Sun from far away with initial speed v_0 relative to the Sun. The Sun has mass M and radius R. Let G be the gravitational constant. Take the Sun to be at rest and ignore all other bodies. Define the total collision cross section sigma as the area in impact-parameter space that leads to the comet striking the Sun. Let b denote the impact parameter (the initial perpendicular offset between the comet’s incoming asymptote and the Sun’s center), r the comet’s closest-approach distance to the Sun’s center (perihelion), and V the comet’s speed at r. Find sigma in terms of G, M, R, and v_0.

A particle of mass m moves in a plane under an attractive central force directed toward a fixed center O. Let r be the orbital radius for a circular orbit, θ the polar angle, and t the time, with overdots denoting time derivatives. The magnitude of the central force is f(r), depending only on r and acting radially inward. The angular momentum is conserved and defined by L = m r^2 \dot{\theta}. Consider a circular orbit of radius r with angular frequency ω satisfying m ω^2 r = f(r). Introduce small perturbations about the circular orbit by r(t) = r + \delta r(t) and θ(t) = ω t + \delta θ(t), where \delta r and \delta θ are infinitesimal. Define \Delta L to be the first-order variation of the angular momentum L induced by the perturbations. The notation [\frac{df}{dr}]_r denotes the radial derivative of f evaluated at the circular-orbit radius r. Show that the circular orbit is stable if the following condition holds at r: f(r) > - \frac{r}{3} [ \frac{df}{dr} ]_r.

Consider circular orbits just above the surfaces of Earth and Moon. A near-Earth satellite completes one revolution in about 90 \unit{min}, and a satellite orbiting just above the Moon’s surface also completes one revolution in about 90 \unit{min}. Let m be the mass of the satellite, r the orbital radius measured from the center of the central body, M the mass of the central body, \omega the orbital angular speed, T the orbital period, and G the gravitational constant. Let M_e and M_m be the masses of Earth and Moon, R_e and R_m their radii, V_e and V_m their volumes, and \rho_e and \rho_m their mean densities. What interesting statement can you derive about the Moon’s composition? State the conclusion as an equation involving \rho_e and \rho_m.

A particle of mass m moves in a plane under a central force. Use plane polar coordinates r(t), θ(t), where overdots denote derivatives with respect to time t. The force vector is F(r) r_hat, where F(r) is the signed radial component of the force, and the potential energy V(r) satisfies F(r) = - dV/dr. The observed orbit satisfies r θ = K, where K is a constant. Define u = 1/r and h = r^2 dot θ, where h is the constant specific angular momentum. Choose V(∞) = 0 as the reference level. The symbol s denotes a dummy variable of integration. Determine the potential energy V(r) as a function of r.

A charged pion, either positively charged (pi^+) or negatively charged (pi^-), moves non-relativistically with kinetic energy T toward a massive nucleus of charge +Z e and effective radius b. Treat the motion classically and neglect nuclear recoil and atomic-electron effects. A collision is said to occur if the distance of closest approach r_min satisfies the criterion below. Let m be the pion mass, e the elementary charge, Z the nuclear charge number, d the maximum impact parameter that still leads to r_min = b, r the radial coordinate, theta the polar angle, L the angular momentum, and an overdot denote time derivative. The geometric collision cross section sigma is defined by the relation below. Define the potential-energy magnitude at r = b by V. For pi^+ the potential energy at r = b is +V, and for pi^- it is -V. Show that the cross sections for pi^+ and pi^- are those given at the end, using the same V.
$$
r_{min} \le b
$$
$$
\sigma = \pi d^2
$$
$$
V = \frac{Z e^2}{b}
$$
$$
\sigma_{+} = \frac{\pi b^2\left(T - V\right)}{T}
$$
$$
\sigma_{-} = \frac{\pi b^2\left(T + V\right)}{T}
$$

Estimate the maximum radius R of a spherical asteroid from which a person could escape by a single vertical jump. Assumptions and definitions: - Before takeoff, the person lowers the center of mass by h_1 = 50 \unit{cm} and then reaches a peak h_2 = 60 \unit{cm} above normal standing height on Earth. - The total vertical displacement against Earth's gravity during the jump is h_{tot} = h_1 + h_2. - The work done by the person against gravity during the jump on Earth is W_{jump} = m g h_{tot}, and the same jump energy is available on the asteroid. - The asteroid is spherical with uniform density equal to Earth's mean density, so M/M_E = (R/R_E)^3. - Symbols: m is the person's mass, g is Earth's surface gravitational acceleration, G is the gravitational constant, M and R are the asteroid's mass and the (maximum) radius that still permits escape by jumping, and M_E and R_E are Earth's mass and radius. - Use R_E = 6400 \times 10^3 \unit{m}.

An object of unit mass m=1 moves under a central potential U(r). In plane polar coordinates (r, θ) its orbit is r=a e^{-bθ}, where a>0 and b are constants and θ is the azimuthal angle measured in the orbital plane. Define u=1/r and the conserved angular momentum per unit mass h=r^2\dot{θ}. The radial force is F_r=-dU/dr. Use Binet's formula for central forces, F_r=-h^2 u^2(d^2 u/dθ^2+u), to determine U(r) up to an overall multiplicative constant. Take U(∞)=0 to fix the additive reference level.

Pretend that the Sun is surrounded by a dust cloud extending out at least as far as the Earth's orbital radius. The Sun produces the gravitational potential energy V_g = - G M m / r, and the dust adds a small potential energy term V_d = k r^2 / 2. The Earth revolves in a nearly circular ellipse of average radius r_0. The effect of the dust may cause the ellipse to precess slowly. Find an approximate expression (to first order in k) for the apsidal precession rate and determine its sense compared to the direction of revolution. Hint: Consider small oscillations about r_0.

Definitions and notation:
- G is the gravitational constant
- M is the mass of the Sun
- m is the mass of the Earth
- r is the Earth–Sun separation
- k is a small constant associated with the dust potential term
- r_0 is the average orbital radius about which small radial oscillations occur
- L is the orbital angular momentum magnitude about the Sun
- U(r) is the effective potential energy
- x is the small radial displacement defined by x = r - r_0
- C is a constant of motion (the energy associated with small radial oscillations)
- ω_0 is the mean angular velocity of orbital revolution for the near-circular orbit
- ω_r is the angular frequency of small radial oscillations about r_0
- ω_p is the apsidal precession rate to be given as the final answer in the form ω_p = ...
- The sense of precession should be stated relative to the direction of orbital revolution

A planet of mass M_p orbits a star of mass M in a circular orbit of radius R. The star explodes, ejecting its outer envelope at a velocity much greater than the orbital motion of the planet, so that the mass loss may be considered instantaneous. The remnant of the star has mass M' with M' much greater than M_p. Neglect the hydrodynamic force exerted on the planet by the expanding shell. Let G be Newton's gravitational constant. Let r be the instantaneous separation between the star and the planet, and let F be the magnitude of the gravitational force. The pre-explosion force law and gravitational parameter are

$$
F = K r^{-2}
$$

$$
K = G M M_p
$$

Immediately after the explosion, the post-explosion gravitational parameter is

$$
K' = G M' M_p
$$

Let v denote the planet's orbital speed before the explosion. Let E and L denote the total mechanical energy and the orbital angular momentum of the planet before the explosion, and let E' and L' denote the corresponding quantities immediately after the explosion. The orbital eccentricity e is related to the energy and angular momentum by

$$
e^2 = 1 + \frac{2 E L^2}{M_p K^2}
$$

Find the eccentricity e of the planet’s orbit after the explosion.

Consider a planet of mass m moving in a nearly circular orbit of radius R around a star of mass M. Let r be the star–planet separation and θ the true anomaly. In addition to Newtonian gravitation, the planet experiences a central repulsive force proportional to r with constant A > 0, directed outward. Let G denote Newton's gravitational constant. Let F_r denote the total radial force on the planet. Define u = 1/r. Let h be the specific angular momentum defined by h = r^2 dθ/dt, where t is time. A dot over a symbol denotes a time derivative d/dt. The periastron is the point of closest approach. Let θ_1 and θ_2 be the true anomalies at two successive periastrons. Define Δθ = θ_2 − θ_1 as the angular advance between successive periastrons, Δθ_p as the precession per revolution, Δt as the time for this precession, and ω_p as its angular velocity. Let u_0 denote the constant value of u for the exactly circular orbit, and let δu be a small perturbation about u_0. Let B be a constant oscillation amplitude and a a dimensionless frequency factor defined in the solution. Compute ω_p.

A point mass m moves in a plane under a central spring force. The spring is anchored at the origin and exerts a radial Hooke force F_r = -K (r - r_0), where r is the distance from the origin and r_0 is the natural length of the spring. Gravity is neglected. Using plane polar coordinates (r(t), θ(t)), the mass initially executes uniform circular motion of radius R with angular frequency ω. At t=0 a very small radial impulse is applied, so that the subsequent motion consists of small radial oscillations about r = R while angular momentum about the origin is conserved. Find the angular frequency ω' of these small radial oscillations. You should use r for the radial coordinate, θ for the angular coordinate, m for the mass, K for the spring constant, r_0 for the natural length of the spring, R for the radius of the initial circular motion, ω for the initial angular frequency, and ω' for the angular frequency of the small radial oscillations.

Assume a solid, non-rotating Earth of uniform mass density ρ and radius R. A straight tunnel through the Earth's center connects Buffalo at r = R to Olaffub at r = -R. A small test stone of mass m moves along the tunnel with coordinate r(t), where t is time. The gravitational constant is G. The stone's simple-harmonic angular frequency inside a uniform sphere is defined by
$$
\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{4 \pi G \rho}{3}}
$$
If the stone is released from rest at r = R, the time to appear at Olaffub is
$$
T_1 = \frac{\pi}{\omega_0}
$$
Now the stone is thrown inward from r = R with initial radial velocity
$$
\dot r(0) = - v_0
$$
Find the initial speed v_0 such that the arrival time at Olaffub equals
$$
T_2 = \frac{T_1}{2}
$$
For the subsequent solution, the motion will be written in the form
$$
r(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \varphi)
$$
where A is the amplitude and \varphi is the phase determined by the initial conditions at t = 0. The period of the motion will be denoted by T, and the arrival time in the thrown case will be denoted by t_a. Use SI units: R and r in \unit{m}, t, T_1, T_2, t_a in \unit{s}, v_0 in \unit{m/s}, and \omega_0 in \unit{s^{-1}}.

A perfectly smooth horizontal disk rotates with constant angular velocity ω about a vertical axis through its center O. A person standing on the disk at radial distance R from O pushes a perfectly smooth coin (negligible size) of mass m directly toward O, imparting to it an initial speed V relative to the disk. Consider the motion as observed in the rotating frame rigidly attached to the disk. Let a right-handed Cartesian frame (x, y, z) be fixed in the disk with the z-axis along the rotation axis, the x-axis chosen opposite to the initial velocity of the coin, and the y-axis completing the right-handed triad. Let r be the position vector and v the velocity in this rotating frame. Let k be the unit vector along +z. Let i denote the imaginary unit used later for a complex representation in the disk plane. Over a short time interval 0 ≤ t ≤ Ψ_t during which (ω t)^2 is negligible, show that the trajectory seen by the person on the disk is approximately a parabola and give its equation in the form y = f(x).

Under especially favorable conditions, a well-isolated subsurface ocean current was observed to circulate counter-clockwise when viewed from directly overhead. The period of one full rotation of the current is T=14 hours. Assuming the circulation is governed by the horizontal Coriolis acceleration due to Earth's rotation, determine the geographic latitude theta and the hemisphere H in which the current was detected.

Use the following symbols and conventions for the solution:
- Adopt a local Earth-fixed coordinate system with x-axis pointing south, y-axis pointing east, and z-axis pointing vertically upward; the corresponding unit vectors are i, j, k.
- The current's horizontal velocity is v = v_x i + v_y j with speed v.
- The magnitude of Earth's angular velocity is omega = 2 pi / T_E, where T_E = 24 hours is the length of a mean solar day. The local rotation vector at latitude theta is omega = omega cos theta i + omega sin theta k.
- The Coriolis acceleration is a = -2 omega × v. Its horizontal component is a_H.
- The circulation is treated as uniform horizontal circular motion with angular speed Omega and radius r.
- Latitude theta is positive northward. The hemisphere indicator H takes values N (Northern) or S (Southern).

Consider a collection of charged particles of masses m and charges e, all having the same charge-to-mass ratio e/m. Each particle moves under a conservative central force F(r) directed toward a common force center at the origin. A uniform magnetic field B is applied. Prove Larmor's theorem: the motion of the particles in a sufficiently small magnetic field B is identical to the motion in the absence of B when viewed in a coordinate system R that rotates with a suitably chosen constant angular velocity vector omega about the force center. Determine the appropriate omega and specify quantitatively what is meant by a “small” magnetic field. Work in SI units. Definitions and notation: L is the laboratory inertial frame. R is a frame with the same origin as L that rotates with angular velocity omega relative to L. The position vector of a particle is r, with magnitude r=|r|. Velocities and accelerations measured in L are v and a. Velocities and accelerations measured in R are v' and a'. The cross product is denoted by ×. The magnitudes of vectors use absolute value bars |·|. The goal is to find omega and to state the small-field condition as an inequality.

A particle of mass m, charge q, and initial velocity v along a line (lab frame) undergoes a head-on interaction with an identical particle (same m and q) initially at rest. Work in classical mechanics, neglect radiation, and use Gaussian units so that the Coulomb potential energy between two charges q separated by a distance r is U = q^2 / r. Define r as the distance of closest approach, define v' as the common speed of each particle at the instant of closest approach (lab frame), and define v_{f,inc} and v_{f,rest} as the final velocities (lab frame) of the incident particle and the particle initially at rest, respectively. Take the positive direction to be the initial direction of motion of the incident particle. Find r, v', v_{f,inc}, and v_{f,rest}, and justify your answers.

Consider a spherically symmetric, self-gravitating collection of identical point particles, each of mass m. Every particle moves on a circular orbit about the common center at radius r with the same kinetic energy T. The only interaction is Newtonian gravity with gravitational constant G. Let M(r) denote the total mass enclosed within radius r, v be the orbital speed at radius r, rho(r) be the mass density, and n(r) be the particle number density, all depending only on r. Find n(r) such that the density remains constant in time

A satellite of mass m moves in a circular orbit of radius R with speed v about the Earth. The mass of the Earth is M and the gravitational constant is G. The satellite abruptly absorbs a small mass δm that was stationary relative to Earth before the collision. During the brief capture, neglect external torques and assume the new post-collision orbit is approximately circular. Let E and E' denote the total mechanical energy (kinetic plus gravitational potential) just before and just after the capture, respectively. Let v' be the immediate post-capture speed and R' the radius of the new circular orbit. Let the change in total energy be ΔE = E' - E. Find ΔE and R'.

An ideal rocket starts at rest in free space with no external forces. Let t denote time with units \unit{s}. Let m denote the instantaneous mass of the rocket, and let m0 denote the rocket mass at the moment of ignition. Let v denote the rocket's velocity relative to an inertial frame, and let v0 denote the constant exhaust speed of the propellant relative to the rocket with units \unit{m/s}. Let P denote the rocket's linear momentum, with P equal to m times v. Let e denote the base of the natural logarithm. Using the ideal rocket equation, determine the residual rocket mass m (expressed in terms of m0) at which P is maximized. Report the final answer as m_maxP = ... in terms of m0.

A bucket of mass M (when empty) is pulled upward from a well by a rope exerting a steady upward force P. The bucket initially contains water of mass m. The water leaks out at a steady rate so that after a time T the bucket is empty. Assume the leaked water has zero velocity relative to the bucket at the moment it leaves, air resistance is negligible, and g denotes the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity. The bucket starts from rest at t=0. Let v(t) be the upward velocity of the bucket at time t. Let M'(t) denote the total mass of the bucket and remaining water at time t. Use SI units: masses in \unit{kg}, force in \unit{N}, times in \unit{s}, velocities in \unit{m/s}, and g in \unit{m/s^2}. Find v(T), the velocity of the bucket at the instant it becomes empty.

A water droplet nucleates in a uniform, quiescent fog. As it falls under gravity, it sweeps up all fog lying in its path and retains all collected mass. Assume the droplet remains spherical, experiences no viscous drag, and buoyancy of the air is neglected. Let t denote time, R(t) the droplet radius, V(t) its downward velocity, and M(t) its mass. Let rho_1 be the density of the droplet, rho_2 be the density of the fog, and g the magnitude of gravitational acceleration with SI units \unit{m/s^2}. Define the density ratio eta=rho_1/rho_2. Overdots denote derivatives with respect to t. As t becomes large, assume the motion approaches uniform acceleration a with SI units \unit{m/s^2}, in the sense that V(t)=a t for large t. Find the asymptotic acceleration a.

A spherical dust particle falls through a water mist cloud of uniform density. The rate of accretion of mass onto the droplet is proportional to the volume of the mist cloud swept out by the droplet per unit time. The droplet starts from rest in the cloud. Let x(t) be the downward vertical position, with x=0 at t=0 at the initial position. Let R(t) be the droplet radius and M(t) the droplet mass. Let M0 and R0 be the initial mass and radius. Let v(t)=dx/dt be the velocity. Let g be the constant gravitational acceleration with SI units \unit{m/s^2}. Let ρ be the uniform mist density with SI units \unit{kg/m^3}. Let α>0 be the proportionality constant in the accretion law, with SI units \unit{kg/m^3}. Find the acceleration of the drop for large times. Define the final answer as a_large, the asymptotic value of d^2x/dt^2 for large t.

A uniform cube has side length 2a. The origin is at the center of mass, and the Cartesian axes x, y, z pass through the centers of the three pairs of opposite faces. Let I_{0} denote the moment of inertia about any axis through the center of mass and perpendicular to a face (equivalently, an axis through the center of mass and the center of one face). Let I_{corner} denote the moment of inertia about the axis through the center of mass and a corner (the body diagonal). Let I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz} be the moments of inertia about the x, y, z axes through the center of mass, and let I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx} be the corresponding products of inertia. For an axis with direction cosines lambda, mu, nu relative to x, y, z, let I denote the moment of inertia about that axis. Let i, j, k denote the unit vectors along the x, y, z axes, respectively. Let r denote the radius vector from the origin to a chosen corner of the cube; in the solution we take the corner with positive x, y, z coordinates.

A flywheel in the form of a uniformly thick disk of diameter D=4\unit{ft} has weight W=600\unit{lbf} and rotates initially at 1200\unit{rpm}. It is to be brought to rest by a constant opposing torque within t=2\unit{min}. Use the following symbol definitions in the solution: I is the moment of inertia of the flywheel about its axis, R is the radius, m is the mass, g is the gravitational acceleration, \theta(t) is the angular position, \omega(t)=\frac{d\theta}{dt} is the angular speed, \omega_0 is the initial angular speed, and \tau_{stop} is the required constant stopping torque. Take g=32.2\unit{ft/s^2}. Compute \tau_{stop}.

Model the leg as a uniform rigid rod of length l and mass m that swings about the hip as a fixed pivot, neglecting the knee joint. Let g be the gravitational acceleration, θ(t) the small angular displacement from the vertical, I the moment of inertia about the hip, ω the angular frequency of small oscillations, and ν the oscillation frequency in hertz. Using the small-angle approximation, estimate the natural swing frequency ν that sets the comfortable walking cadence, and state which characteristic of the leg it depends on.

A uniform solid bowling ball is projected along a horizontal alley with initial center-of-mass speed v_0 at t=0 so that it initially slides without rolling. The ball has mass m and radius R. The coefficient of static friction with the floor is mu_s and the coefficient of kinetic (sliding) friction is mu_d. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Ignore air resistance. While the ball is sliding, let f denote the magnitude of the kinetic friction force, a the linear acceleration of the center of mass, alpha the angular acceleration about the center, I the moment of inertia about the center, and t the time at which the ball first transitions to rolling without slipping. Let v_roll denote the linear speed at that instant. Compute v_roll in terms of v_0. Use I = \frac{2}{5} m R^2 for a uniform solid sphere and the rolling-without-slipping condition v = R omega at the transition, with omega = alpha t.

A yo-yo is modeled as two identical solid disks of radius R and thickness t, separated by a distance t, connected by a solid cylindrical shaft (spool) of radius r and length t. The yo-yo has uniform volumetric mass density ρ, total mass M, and moment of inertia I about its central axis. A massless string is wound around the shaft and the yo-yo descends vertically under gravity with acceleration g. Let x be the downward displacement of the center of mass, θ the angular displacement as the string unwinds, and F the tension in the string. Assume the string unwinds without slipping so that x = r θ and hence ddot{x} = r ddot{θ}. Find the tension F in the string during the descent.

A string is stretched between two rigid supports that are 100 cm apart. Let the string length be L and the wavelengths corresponding to the only excitable frequencies in the 100 Hz to 350 Hz range, namely 160 Hz, 240 Hz, and 320 Hz, be λ_0, λ_1, and λ_2, respectively. The string has fixed ends, so it supports standing waves with mode index n, a positive integer. Determine λ_0, λ_1, and λ_2.

An infinite string with constant tension T and linear mass density \sigma undergoes small transverse displacements y(x,t). Define the wave speed by v=\sqrt{T/\sigma}. The initial displacement and velocity are given by y(x,0)=f(x) and \frac{\partial y}{\partial t}(x,0)=g(x), respectively. Find the motion y(x,t) for all t>0. In the solution below, f_1 and f_2 are auxiliary functions, C is an integration constant, and f_1'(x)=\frac{d f_1}{dx}(x), f_2'(x)=\frac{d f_2}{dx}(x).

Consider a plane standing sound wave in air at temperature T=300 K and frequency nu=10^3 Hz. The amplitude of the excess pressure (pressure variation relative to ambient) is p_0=1 dyn/cm^2, and the ambient pressure is p_a=10^6 dyn/cm^2. Let xi(x,t) be the longitudinal displacement field of the air along the x direction, with amplitude xi_0. Define k as the wave number, omega as the angular frequency, v as the speed of sound in air, p(x,t) as the instantaneous excess pressure, B as the bulk modulus of air, and rho as the mass density of air. To relate strain to displacement, consider a small cylindrical element of air with cross-sectional area A and initial length Delta x, having initial volume V=A Delta x and volume change Delta V caused by the displacement field. Let m be the mass of that element, mu the molar mass of air, and R the universal gas constant. Use SI units in calculations. Use the following data for air: mu=29×10^{-3} kg/mol, R=8.31 J/mol/K, v=340 m/s.

An acoustical motion detector emits a continuous sound at frequency nu=50\unit{kHz}. The speed of sound in air is c=330\unit{m/s}. The detector registers a moving object if the received echo frequency differs from the emitted frequency by at least the threshold Delta nu_th=100\unit{Hz}. Assume one-dimensional motion along the line of sight and a simple reflection from the object. Let v denote the object's line-of-sight velocity relative to the detector, taken as positive when the object moves toward the detector. Let nu' be the sound frequency in the object's rest frame, and let nu'' be the echo frequency received by the detector after reflection. Find the minimum speed magnitude v_min such that the detector registers motion, and give the registration condition in the form |v|>=v_min.

A student near a railroad track hears a train's whistle as the train approaches directly and then as it recedes directly. Let nu_0 be the frequency emitted by the whistle, nu_1 the frequency heard when the train is approaching, and nu_2 the frequency heard when the train is receding. The observed frequencies are nu_1 = 250 \unit{Hz} and nu_2 = 200 \unit{Hz}. Let c = 360 \unit{m/s} be the speed of sound in air, and let v be the speed of the train relative to the air. Find v in SI units.

Given that the apparent angular diameters of the Moon and the Sun as seen from Earth are approximately equal and that the tides raised by the Moon are about twice as high as those raised by the Sun, determine the ratio of their average densities. Use the following definitions and notation: R_e, R_m, R_s are the radii of Earth, Moon, and Sun; M_e, M_m, M_s are their masses; D_m, D_s are the distances from Earth's center to the Moon and the Sun; h_m, h_s are the heights of the tides raised by the Moon and the Sun at the same point on Earth; theta is the Moon's (or Sun's) zenith distance at that point; G is the gravitational constant; g is the acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface; rho_m, rho_s are the average densities of the Moon and the Sun. Assume the tide-raising potential is dominated by the quadrupole term. The approximate equality of angular diameters implies R_m/D_m = R_s/D_s. The observed tidal ratio implies h_m/h_s = 2.

A space station is made from a large cylinder of radius R_0 and length L, filled with air. The cylinder spins about its symmetry axis with angular speed ω so that the inward (centripetal) acceleration at the rim equals the gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface g. The absolute temperature T of the air is uniform throughout the cylinder. The air behaves as an ideal gas. Let p(r) be the air pressure and ρ(r) the mass density at radial distance r from the axis. Let M be the molar mass of air, R the universal gas constant, V the gas volume, and m the gas mass. Determine the ratio p(0)/p(R_0).

An incompressible Newtonian fluid with mass density rho and dynamic viscosity eta flows in steady-state, fully developed, laminar regime through a straight circular pipe of internal radius R and length L. The pressure at the inlet end (z = 0) is p1 and at the exit (z = L) is p2, with p1 > p2. Gravity is neglected. Use cylindrical coordinates (r, phi, z) with the z-axis along the pipe. Let u be the velocity field and let v(r) denote the axial speed u_z(r). Impose the no-slip boundary condition v(R) = 0 and finiteness at the axis r = 0. Let Q denote the mass of fluid that passes through any cross-section per unit time (mass flow rate). Use SI units: rho in \unit{kg/m^3}, eta in \unit{Pa s}, R and L in \unit{m}, p1 and p2 in \unit{Pa}, and Q in \unit{kg/s}. Compute Q in terms of rho, eta, R, L, p1, and p2.

A right-handed rectangular coordinate system with axes x, y, z rotates with constant angular velocity ω about the z-axis relative to an inertial frame with axes x', y', z' and the same origin. A particle of mass m moves under a force derived from a scalar potential V(x, y, z). Using generalized coordinates q1 = x, q2 = y, q3 = z, set up Lagrange's equations of motion in the rotating coordinates. Show that these equations are identical to those for a particle in a fixed frame acted on by the force -∇V and an additional force derivable from a velocity-dependent potential U(x, y, z, \dot x, \dot y, \dot z). Find U. Notation for the solution: position vector r = (x, y, z), velocity in the rotating frame v = (\dot x, \dot y, \dot z), velocity in the inertial frame v', and constant angular velocity vector ω = (0, 0, ω).
problem_answer: $$U = - m \omega (x \dot y - \dot x y) - \frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 (x^2 + y^2)$$

problem_description: A particle of mass m moves under a central attractive force whose magnitude varies inversely as the cube of its distance r from the force center. The motion is planar and described in plane polar coordinates (r, theta) with origin at the force center. Let t denote time, and an overdot denote derivative with respect to t. Let F_r be the signed radial force component with force law F_r = -k r^{-3}, where k > 0 is a constant. Let T be the kinetic energy, V(r) the potential energy, and L = T - V the Lagrangian. Use Lagrange's equations for the generalized coordinates q_i in the set {r, theta}. The conserved angular momentum is written as b = m r^2 dot theta, where b is a constant fixed by initial conditions. To solve the orbit, use the substitution u = 1/r. Let r_0 and theta_0 be constants determined by initial conditions, so that (r_0, theta_0) is a point on the orbit. Derive the equations of motion and solve them for the orbits. Discuss how the nature of the orbits depends on the parameters m, k, and b, and present final orbit relations in the form r f(theta) = r_0 for each case

Consider a one-dimensional system with generalized coordinate q(t). The Lagrangian is the function L(t,q,\dot q) with positive constants \gamma, m, k, where overdots denote time derivatives. The Lagrangian is
$$
L = e^{\gamma t} \left( \frac{1}{2} m \dot q^2 - \frac{1}{2} k q^2 \right)
$$
Tasks:
1) Write Lagrange's equation for q(t), determine whether there are any constants of motion, and describe the motion.
2) Perform the time-dependent point transformation to the new generalized coordinate S(t) defined by
$$
S = e^{\frac{\gamma t}{2}} q
$$
Express the Lagrangian in terms of S and \dot S, write Lagrange's equation, identify any constants of motion, and describe the relationship between the two solutions.
Notation and definitions to be used:
- The trial-solution exponent is \alpha, defined through q(t) = e^{\alpha t}.
- Define b by
$$
b = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\gamma}{2} \right)^2 - \frac{k}{m} }
$$
- In the underdamped case, write b = i \beta with
$$
\beta = \sqrt{ \frac{k}{m} - \left( \frac{\gamma}{2} \right)^2 }
$$
- Define \omega by
$$
\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m} - \left( \frac{\gamma}{2} \right)^2
$$
- A, B, A', B', C, D, q_0 are integration constants determined by initial conditions.
- Let L_S denote the Lagrangian expressed in terms of S and \dot S.
- Denote any constant of motion in the S-description by K, and the final answer for it should be written in the form
$$
K = \dot S^2 + \omega^2 S^2
$$

A particle of mass m and charge e moves in an isotropic three-dimensional harmonic oscillator potential with natural angular frequency ω_0. The trapping potential is V_trap = (1/2) m ω_0^2 r^2. The particle is simultaneously subjected to uniform magnetic and electric fields of magnitudes B and E that are mutually perpendicular. Choose Cartesian axes so that the magnetic field is along the z-axis and the electric field is along the x-axis. Use SI units throughout. Find the normal-mode angular vibration frequencies. Define ω_z as the z-direction frequency and ω_+, ω_- as the two in-plane normal-mode angular frequencies. Discuss the weak-field limit eB/m ≪ ω_0 and the strong-field limit eB/m ≫ ω_0.

A small body of mass m and charge q moves without friction on the interior surface of a right circular cone of opening angle 2 alpha. A point charge -q is fixed at the apex of the cone. There is no gravity. Assume nonrelativistic motion with speed v satisfying v << c, where c is the speed of light, so that radiation is negligible. Use a Cartesian frame with the cone’s axis along z. Let the generalized coordinates be q_1 = r and q_2 = varphi, where r is the radial distance from the cone’s axis, varphi is the azimuthal angle, and z is the height coordinate. The cone constraint is z = r cot alpha, where alpha is the cone’s half-angle. The vacuum permittivity is epsilon_0 (written as \varepsilon_0 in formulas). Define J as the conserved angular momentum (conjugate to varphi) about the cone’s axis. Let r_0 be the constant radial coordinate of an equilibrium circular trajectory and dot varphi_0 be its constant angular velocity. For small radial oscillations about this trajectory, define the radial displacement xi by r = r_0 + xi with xi << r_0. Let omega be the small-oscillation angular frequency. Find omega in terms of dot varphi_0 and alpha.

A spacecraft moves at constant speed v relative to Earth along the +x direction. Let Σ (Earth frame) and Σ′ (spacecraft frame) be inertial frames. A standard clock at the Earth origin records time t, and a standard clock at the spacecraft origin records time t′. The clocks are synchronized at the event t = 0, t′ = 0, x = 0, x′ = 0. The Earth observer watches his own clock directly and the spacecraft clock through a telescope. Define Δ_t to be the reading of the Earth clock at the instant he visually sees the spacecraft clock’s display. Given β = v/c and γ = 1/√(1 − β^2), and c the speed of light, when the spacecraft clock reads 1 hour, find Δ_t.

An atomic clock is carried once around the world by a jet plane and then compared with a previously synchronized and similar clock that did not travel. Approximately how large a discrepancy does special relativity predict? Use the following definitions and assumptions: Let S be the Earth's approximately inertial frame with coordinates (x, t). Let S' be the aircraft's rest frame with coordinates (x', t'). The aircraft has constant speed v relative to S with v=1000\unit{m/s}. Let c be the speed of light with c=3\times 10^8\unit{m/s}. Define β=v/c and γ=1/\sqrt{1-β^2}. For the two events corresponding to departure and arrival of the aircraft's clock, define Δt as the time interval between these events in S and Δt' as the proper time interval measured by the aircraft's clock in S'. Define the predicted special-relativistic discrepancy as Δt_dis=Δt−Δt'. Let the Earth's radius be R=6400\unit{km}. The circumnavigation path length is 2πR and the circumnavigation time in S is T=2πR/v. Neglect gravitational effects and accelerations and use special relativity only. Report the final answer as Δt_dis in seconds.

Consider monochromatic radiation emitted at the Sun with frequency \nu_s in \unit{Hz} and received at the Earth with frequency \nu_e in \unit{Hz}. Work in a static, weak gravitational field described by the metric components

$$
g_{00}=1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}
$$

$$
g_{11}=-1
$$

$$
g_{22}=-1
$$

$$
g_{33}=-1
$$

$$
g_{\mu\neq\nu}=0
$$

Here c is the speed of light in \unit{m/s}, and \Phi(r) is the gravitational potential energy per unit mass at position r. Let r_s denote a point on the Sun where the light is emitted and r_e denote a point on the Earth where the light is received. Let P_1 be the emission point and P_2 be the reception point, both at rest in the static field. Let t_0 be the coordinate time of emission of the first crest at P_1, t be the coordinate period between successive emitted crests, and T be the coordinate propagation time from P_1 to P_2. Let \tau be the proper period at P_1 measured by a standard clock co-moving with P_1, and let \tau' be the proper period at P_2 measured by a standard clock co-moving with P_2. Let \nu be the emitted frequency measured locally at P_1 and \nu' be the received frequency measured locally at P_2. Assume the field is static so that T is constant. Using this metric, derive the gravitational redshift, expressed as the fractional frequency shift (\nu_e-\nu_s)/\nu_s, as a function of the difference of gravitational potentials at the Sun and Earth.

Two particles of equal mass m move along the +x direction in the laboratory frame K_0. Their relativistic momenta in K_0 are p_1 = 5 m c for the slower particle and p_2 = 10 m c for the faster particle. Here c is the speed of light with unit \unit{m/s}. For particle i in K_0, let v_i be its speed, \beta_i = v_i/c, \gamma_i = 1/\sqrt{1 - \beta_i^2}, and p_i its relativistic momentum with p_i = m \gamma_i v_i. Let K_1 be the rest frame of the slower particle and K_2 be the rest frame of the faster particle. Let K' be an inertial frame moving at speed v along +x relative to K_0. For any particle with x component of velocity u_x in K_0, its x component in K' is u_x' = (u_x - v)/(1 - u_x v/c^2). Define v_2' as the speed of the faster particle measured in K_1 and v_1' as the speed of the slower particle measured in K_2.

A beam of 10^6 K_l^0 mesons per second, with speed parameter defined by beta ≡ v/c = 1/\sqrt{2}, is observed to undergo coherent regeneration in a lead brick according to the reaction $$K_l^0 + \text{Brick} \to K_s^0 + \text{Brick}$$ The internal state of the brick is identical before and after the reaction, and the directions of motion of the incoming K_l^0 and outgoing K_s^0 are identical. Use the following data: $$m(K_l)=5\times 10^8 \unit{eV/c^2}$$ $$m(K_l)-m(K_s)=3.5\times 10^{-6} \unit{eV/c^2}$$ Define m_l = m(K_l), m_s = m(K_s), v as the meson speed, c as the speed of light, beta = v/c, and gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}. Let E_l and P_l denote the energy and momentum of the incoming K_l^0, and E_s and P_s denote the energy and momentum of the outgoing K_s^0, all in the lab frame. The beam rate is R = 10^6 \unit{1/s}. Let F_beam be the average force exerted by the brick on the beam, and F_brick be the average force exerted by the beam on the brick. Find the magnitude and direction of F_brick, in newtons or in dynes.

A K meson (kaon) at rest decays into a mu meson (muon) and a neutrino. Let m_K be the rest mass of the kaon and m_\mu the rest mass of the muon. Let c be the speed of light. The given rest energies are m_K c^2 = 494 \unit{MeV} and m_\mu c^2 = 106 \unit{MeV}. The neutrino is massless, i.e., its rest energy is zero. Let p_\mu and p_
u denote the magnitudes of the muon and neutrino momenta, respectively. Let E_\mu and E_
u denote their total energies. Let T_\mu and T_
u denote their kinetic energies, where T_\mu = E_\mu - m_\mu c^2 for the muon and T_
u = E_
u for the neutrino. Find T_\mu and T_
u.

An astronaut takes an ordinary flashlight, turns it on, and leaves it out in deep space, spin-stabilized about its axis so the beam direction is fixed. Assume the bulb is at the focus of a paraboloid reflector so essentially all optical power is emitted in one direction. Neglect external forces and assume the recoil speed remains nonrelativistic. Define the variables used in the solution: N is the optical power output of the flashlight in watts, t is the time interval the flashlight is on, m is the mass of the flashlight, c is the speed of light in vacuum, E is the total photon energy emitted over time t, p is the momentum delivered to the flashlight, v is the additional speed gained after time t, and \varepsilon is the energy of a single photon. For the numerical example use N=1\unit{W}, m=0.3\unit{kg}, and t=2\unit{h}.

A hypothetical flashlight emits a perfectly collimated beam of light along a straight line and can convert a significant fraction of its own rest mass into radiation. The flashlight starts at rest in an inertial frame with rest mass m0. After it is switched on, it recoils and moves freely along the same line as the beam due to conservation of momentum. At some instant its speed relative to the original rest frame is v and its rest mass at that instant is m. The speed of light is denoted by c. Let E be the total energy carried away by all photons emitted up to that instant; the total photon momentum has magnitude E/c and is directed opposite to the flashlight's velocity. Define beta = v/c and gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). Do not assume v is much smaller than c. Find m as a function of v.

Two proton beams collide head-on, each proton having kinetic energy T. Find the kinetic energy K_single of a single proton beam that, when colliding with stationary target protons, yields the same available energy for reactions. Use relativistic expressions. Definitions: m is the proton rest mass, c is the speed of light, S' is the rest frame of a target proton in the single-beam case, E' and p' are respectively the total energy and momentum of the incoming proton in S', and K_single is the kinetic energy of that incoming proton. The available energy for reactions in the fixed-target case is defined by K_single = E' - m c^2.

A charged particle with charge e and rest mass m moves in a uniform electromagnetic field that is constant in space and time. In an inertial Lorentz frame S, the field components are E = (a, 0, 0) and B = (0, 0, b), with |E| ≠ |B|. Let c denote the speed of light and τ the particle's proper time. Let s be defined by ds = c dτ. Let u^α be the particle's four-velocity with components u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4, defined by u^α = ((γ/c) u, γ), where u = (u_x, u_y, u_z) is the three-velocity and γ = 1 / sqrt(1 - u^2 / c^2) with u^2 = u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2. Let p^α = m c^2 u^α be the four-momentum. Let F be the Lorentz three-force and F^α the four-force, with F = e (E + u × B) and F^α = (γ F, (γ/c) u · F). Use the Euclidean norm for |E| and |B|. Tasks: 1) State the differential equations for the components of the four-velocity u^α(τ). 2) Show that the solutions may be expressed as superpositions of exponentials and determine the exponents. 3) Determine the condition on E and B under which all components of the four-velocity remain bounded along every trajectory

Derive the Hamiltonian for a relativistic charged particle in prescribed electromagnetic fields (Gaussian units). Definitions and notation: - Rest mass: m_0 - Charge: q - Speed of light: c - Lorentz factor: gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - v^2 / c^2) - Position coordinates: x_i for i = 1, 2, 3 - Velocities: dot x_i = dx_i / dt, collected as the velocity vector v = (dot x_1, dot x_2, dot x_3) - Scalar potential: Phi(x,t) - Vector potential: A(x,t) with components A_i - Electric field E and magnetic field H are defined by the potentials as below - The mechanical (kinetic) momentum is defined as p_mech = m_0 v / sqrt(1 - v^2 / c^2) - The canonical momentum components are defined by p_i = partial L / partial dot x_i, and p denotes the canonical momentum vector with components p_i - The Hamiltonian is denoted by cal H, and the Lagrangian by cal L. The electromagnetic fields are given by the potentials via
problem_answer: $$\mathcal H = \sqrt{ \left( p - \frac{q}{c} A \right)^2 c^2 + m_0^2 c^4 } + q \Phi$$

problem_description: A particle of rest mass m_0 moves at speed v relative to an inertial frame. Let c denote the speed of light in vacuum with units \unit{m/s}. Let E be the total relativistic energy of the particle, T its kinetic energy, and let the Lorentz factor be defined by \gamma = 1 \big/ \sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}. If the kinetic energy equals the rest energy m_0 c^2, find v in terms of c.

A particle of rest mass m has initial velocity v_0 along the x-axis at t = 0. For t > 0, it is subject to a constant force of magnitude F directed along the positive y-axis. Let the position be r(t) = (x(t), y(t)) and define the velocity components by v_x(t) = dx/dt and v_y(t) = dy/dt. Let the speed be v(t) = sqrt(v_x(t)^2 + v_y(t)^2). Let c be the speed of light. Define the Lorentz factor by gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - v(t)^2 / c^2) and define beta = v(t) / c. Also define gamma_0 = 1 / sqrt(1 - v_0^2 / c^2). Find v_x(t), v_y(t), and v(t), and show that lim_{t -> infinity} v(t) = c.

A thin but very massive insulating disc has uniform surface charge density \(\sigma\) and radius \(R\). A point charge of magnitude \(+Q\) is located on the symmetry axis at axial distance \(z\) from the disc center. Using \(\varepsilon_0\) for the vacuum permittivity, derive an expression for the force \(F\) on the charge. Here, \(F\) denotes the axial component of the force on the charge, taken as positive along the +\(z\)-axis.

A sphere of radius R carries a total charge Q uniformly distributed throughout its volume. Using Gauss's law, find the electric field vector both outside and inside the sphere. Use rho for the volume charge density, epsilon_0 for the vacuum permittivity, vec{r} for the position vector from the center, r=|vec{r}| for the radial distance, and vec{E} for the electric field.

Consider a uniformly charged solid sphere of radius R that carries a total charge Q distributed uniformly throughout its volume. Let \varepsilon_0 denote the vacuum permittivity. Let \vec r be the position vector from the center, with magnitude r=|\vec r|, and let \hat r=\vec r/r be the outward radial unit vector. Let \vec E denote the electrostatic field and \varphi the electrostatic potential, with the reference chosen so that \varphi(\infty)=0. For convenience, define \vec E_1(r) and \varphi_1(r) for points with r\leq R (inside) and \vec E_2(r) and \varphi_2(r) for points with r\geq R (outside). In radial line integrals use the dummy radial variable \rho, and take d\vec r=\hat r d\rho. Find \vec E and \varphi at all points in space.

In electrostatic equilibrium, consider a spherical conducting shell with inner radius a and outer radius b. A point charge q is fixed at the center. The outer surface of the shell carries a uniform surface charge density sigma. Let r be the radial distance from the center, e_r the outward radial unit vector, and epsilon_0 the vacuum permittivity. Let E(r) denote the electric field vector and Q_in the total charge on the inner surface. Use SI units: E has units \unit{N/C}, sigma has units \unit{C/m^2}, and a, b, r have units \unit{m}. Find E(r) for all r>0 and determine Q_in.

A grounded spherical conducting shell has inner radius R_1 and outer radius R_2. The cavity 0<r<R_1 is filled with a uniform volume space charge of density rho. The space is vacuum with permittivity epsilon_0. Let E denote the electric field vector, varphi the electric potential, r the radial distance from the center, e_r the outward radial unit vector, and W the total electrostatic energy of the system defined over the cavity volume. The grounding sets the reference varphi(R_1)=0. Find W and the potential at the center varphi(0).

A solid metal sphere of radius a is surrounded concentrically by a hollow metal sphere of inner radius b, with b>a. The region a<r<b is filled with a material whose electrical conductivity σ depends on the electric field magnitude E according to σ=K E, where K is a given constant. A steady potential difference V is maintained between the two spheres. Let I denote the total steady current flowing radially between the spheres, j the current density, r the radial coordinate measured from the center, and S=4π r^2 the area of a spherical surface of radius r. Determine I in terms of V, a, b, and K.

An isolated soap bubble is an isolated conducting sphere whose total charge remains constant. Let the initial radius be r1 = 1 cm and the initial potential be V1 = 100 V. The bubble collapses to a conducting spherical drop of radius r2 = 1 mm. Let Q be the conserved charge, ε0 the vacuum permittivity, W the electrostatic energy of a charged isolated sphere, and ΔW = W2 − W1 the change in electrostatic energy from the initial bubble (state 1) to the final drop (state 2). Find ΔW in joules.

A charge Q is placed on a capacitor of capacitance C_1. One terminal is connected to ground and the other terminal (the free plate) is insulated and not connected to anything. The separation between the plates is increased so that the capacitance becomes C_2. The capacitances satisfy
$$
C_2 < C_1
$$
Define V_1 as the electric potential of the insulated plate relative to ground before the separation, and V_2 as the electric potential of the insulated plate relative to ground after the separation. Use Q for the charge on the insulated plate, C for capacitance, and V for the potential of the insulated plate relative to ground, related by
$$
Q = C V
$$
What happens to the potential on the free plate when the plates are separated? Express V_2 in terms of V_1

A parallel-plate capacitor with circular plates of radius r is charged to an initial potential difference V and then disconnected from the charging circuit, so the magnitude of charge on each plate Q remains constant. The initial plate separation is d and it is slowly changed to d' ≠ d. Assume r ≫ d so edge effects can be neglected. Let ε0 be the vacuum permittivity. Let C and C' denote the capacitances at separations d and d', respectively. Let W and W' denote the stored electrostatic energies before and after the change, and let ΔW = W' − W. Let W_ext denote the work done by the external agent during the change. Find W_ext in terms of r, d, d', V, and ε0.

A parallel-plate capacitor has plate area A=\frac{1}{5} \unit{m^2} and initial plate separation d=\frac{1}{100} \unit{m}. It is charged to an initial voltage U=10^3 \unit{V} and then disconnected from the battery, so that the charge remains constant during subsequent changes. The plates are then pulled apart quasistatically to double the separation, so d'=2d. The vacuum permittivity is \epsilon_0=8.9\times 10^{-12} \unit{C^2/(N m^2)}. Use the capacitance formula C=\epsilon_0 A/d. Define C as the initial capacitance, C' as the final capacitance, Q as the conserved charge, \Delta W as the external work required (equal to the increase in stored electrostatic energy), and U' as the final voltage. Find the required work \Delta W and the final voltage U'.

A very long (effectively infinite) hollow metallic cylinder has inner radius r_0 and outer radius r_0+\Delta r with \Delta r \ll r_0. The cylindrical cavity r<r_0 is uniformly filled with a volume space charge density \rho_0. The metal occupies r_0<r<r_0+\Delta r and the exterior is vacuum. Use cylindrical coordinates (r, \varphi, z) with the z-axis along the cylinder axis. Let e_r denote the outward radial unit vector. Let \varepsilon_0 be the vacuum permittivity. The cylinder as a whole carries no net charge. Determine the electric field in the three regions r<r_0, r_0<r<r_0+\Delta r, and r>r_0+\Delta r. Then find the surface charge densities on the inner surface at r=r_0 and on the outer surface at r=r_0+\Delta r. Finally, find the fields and surface charges if the cylinder is grounded. Define E_1(r), E_2(r), and E_3(r) for the fields in the regions r<r_0, r>r_0+\Delta r, and r_0<r<r_0+\Delta r, respectively, and define \sigma(r_0) and \sigma(r_0+\Delta r) as the surface charge densities on the inner and outer metal surfaces, respectively

A spherical capacitor consists of two concentric conducting spheres. The inner sphere has radius b and carries total charge Q. The outer spherical conductor is initially at radius a with a > b and is grounded. It then contracts quasistatically to a final radius a'. Let epsilon_0 denote the vacuum permittivity, r the radial coordinate measured from the common center, e_r the outward radial unit vector, E the electric field vector, U(a) the electrostatic field energy when the outer radius is a, and W_el the work done by the electric force during the contraction. Find the work done W_el.

A point charge q is moved quasistatically from infinity to the center of a hollow, isolated, initially uncharged, conducting spherical shell. The shell has inner radius R and thickness t, so its outer radius is R+t. Space is vacuum with permittivity epsilon_0. The charge passes through a negligibly small hole that does not disturb the electrostatics. Let W_ext denote the work done by the external agent in bringing the charge to the center. Find W_ext in terms of q, R, t, and epsilon_0.

A long coaxial cable of length l with l much greater than b consists of an inner cylindrical conductor of radius a and a coaxial outer conductor with inner radius b. The region between conductors is uniformly filled with a homogeneous, isotropic material of permittivity ε and conductivity σ. Assume end effects are negligible. Use SI units: a, b, l in \unit{m}, ε in \unit{F/m}, σ in \unit{S/m}. Let V denote the applied voltage difference between the inner and outer conductors, r the cylindrical radial coordinate, e_r the radial unit vector, E the electric field, J the current density, I the steady current from inner to outer conductor, R the resistance between inner and outer conductors over length l, ω the surface charge density on the inner conductor, Q the total charge on the inner conductor, and C the capacitance between the conductors. Calculate R and C.

Two conductors are embedded in a uniform, linear, isotropic material of conductivity σ and dielectric constant ε. The measured electrical resistance between the two conductors is R. Let the conductors carry free charges +Q and −Q, with potential difference V between them, and let the conduction current be I. Let j denote the current density, E the electric field, and S any closed Gaussian surface enclosing only the positively charged conductor. Use Ohm's law j=σE and Gauss' law ∮ E⋅dS=Q/ε. Given σ=10^{-4} \unit{S/m}, ε=80 ε_0 with ε_0=8.85×10^{-12} \unit{F/m}, and R=10^{5} \unit{Ω}, derive an equation for the capacitance C between the two conductors and calculate its value.

Consider a long cylindrical coaxial capacitor with an inner conductor of radius a, an outer conductor of radius b, and a dielectric whose relative permittivity is K(r), varying with cylindrical radius r. The capacitor is charged to a potential difference V defined by V = φ(b) − φ(a), where φ is the electrostatic potential. Let ε0 be the vacuum permittivity. Define D(r) as the radial electric displacement field in the dielectric (positive outward), E(r) as the radial electric field component in the dielectric (positive outward), U(r) as the electromagnetic energy density in the dielectric, λ as the charge per unit length on the inner conductor, and k as a positive constant determined by the requirement that U(r) be uniform. Determine K(r) such that U(r) is independent of r, and then compute E(r) for these conditions.

Find the potential energy of a point charge in vacuum at a distance π from a semi-infinite dielectric medium. Use cylindrical coordinates (r, φ, z) with the planar interface at z=0, the dielectric occupying z<0, and vacuum occupying z>0. Place the charge q on the z-axis at (0, 0, x) with x>0. Let ε0 be the vacuum permittivity and κ be the relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium. Assume the dielectric has no free charge. Define σ_p(r) as the bound surface charge density induced on the z=0 plane. Let E_{z1}(r) and E_{z2}(r) be the normal components of the electric field just above (z=0_+) and just below (z=0_−) the interface, respectively. Let E be the z-component of the electric field at the charge location due only to the bound charges. Let F be the magnitude of the force on q due to the bound charges. Let W(x) be the potential energy of q due to this interaction when the charge is at distance x from the interface. The requested final answer should be reported as W_π, defined by W_π = W(π\unit{m}).

For steady current flow obeying Ohm's law, find the resistance R between two concentric spherical perfectly conducting shells of inner radius a and outer radius b with a<b. The region a<r<b is completely filled with a homogeneous, isotropic material of electrical conductivity σ. Use SI units throughout: a, b, r in \unit{m}, σ in \unit{S/m}, ε in \unit{F/m}, Q in \unit{C}, I in \unit{A}, V in \unit{V}, and R in \unit{Ω}. Definitions: r is the radial coordinate, e_r is the outward radial unit vector, E(r) is the electric field, E_r(r) is the radial component of E, j is the current density, j_r(r) is the radial component of j, Q is the total charge carried by the inner sphere, I is the total current from the inner to the outer conductor, V is the potential difference between the two conductors, and ε is the permittivity of the material. The vector area element on a sphere of radius r is dS and the radial line element is dr. Assumptions: time-independent steady current, Ohm's law j=σE holds in a<r<b, the material and conductors are homogeneous and isotropic, spherical symmetry applies so the surface charge on each conductor is uniform, displacement current effects are neglected, and the conductors are ideal. Determine R in closed form.

Determine the electric field inside and outside a solid sphere of radius R and permittivity \varepsilon placed in a uniform external electric field of magnitude E_0 directed along the z-axis. Let \vec{E}_0 denote the external field vector, taken as \vec{E}_0=E_0 \hat{z}. Let \varepsilon_0 denote the vacuum permittivity. Define \vec{E}_1(\vec{r}) as the electric field inside the sphere and \vec{E}_2(\vec{r}) as the electric field outside the sphere. Here \vec{r} is the position vector measured from the center of the sphere and r=|\vec{r}| is its magnitude.

A solid dielectric sphere of radius R_0 and permittivity ε is placed in a uniform static external electric field E_0 directed along the +z axis. The vacuum permittivity is ε_0. The internal electric field inside the sphere is denoted by E. The polarization inside the dielectric is denoted by p, and the total electric dipole moment of the sphere is denoted by P. The outward unit normal to the surface is n. The polar angle θ is measured from the +z direction (the direction of E_0). Show that the induced bound surface charge density σ(θ) is
$$
\sigma(\theta) = \frac{\varepsilon - \varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon + 2 \varepsilon_0} 3 \varepsilon_0 E_0 \cos \theta
$$
The sphere is then rotated rigidly with constant angular velocity ω about the +z axis (the direction of E_0). Will a magnetic field be produced? If not, explain why no magnetic field is produced. If so, describe the magnetic field lines qualitatively.

A spherical shell of radius R carries a surface charge density σ(θ)=σ_0 cos θ, where σ_0 is a constant and θ is the polar angle measured from the z-axis. Space is vacuum with permittivity ε_0, and there are no volume charges either inside or outside the shell. Let Φ_+(r,θ) and Φ_-(r,θ) denote the electrostatic potentials outside (r>R) and inside (r<R) the shell, respectively. Let E_+(r,θ) and E_-(r,θ) denote the corresponding electric fields. Let P_n denote the Legendre polynomials. Let e_r and e_θ be the spherical unit vectors and e_z be the Cartesian unit vector along the z-axis. Find Φ_+(r,θ), Φ_-(r,θ), E_+(r,θ), and E_-(r,θ) as functions of r and θ. The final answers should be written as equalities for Φ_+, Φ_-, E_+, and E_-.

Consider a sphere of radius R centered at the origin. Use spherical coordinates (r, θ, φ), where θ is the polar angle measured from the +z axis. A point charge q is located at the origin, and there are no other charges anywhere. Let ε_0 denote the vacuum permittivity. Let Φ(r,θ) be the electric potential. The boundary condition on the spherical surface r=R is Φ(R,θ)=V_0 cos θ, where V_0 is a given constant. Let Φ_-(r,θ) denote the potential in the interior region r<R, and let Φ_+(r,θ) denote the potential in the exterior region r>R. Let δ^3(r) denote the three-dimensional Dirac delta distribution at r=0. Let P_n(x) denote the Legendre polynomials. In the separation-of-variables approach used below, the interior and exterior solutions are expanded in Legendre series with coefficient sequences {A_n}_{n=0}^∞ and {B_n}_{n=0}^∞, respectively; these A_n and B_n are unknown constants to be determined by the boundary condition. Determine Φ_-(r,θ) and Φ_+(r,θ).

A perfectly conducting sphere of radius a carries a net charge q and is placed in a uniform external electric field of magnitude E_0 directed along the +z axis. Use spherical coordinates (r, θ, φ) with θ measured from +z and φ the azimuthal angle. Let ε_0 denote the vacuum permittivity. Let Φ_1 and Φ_2 be the electrostatic potentials inside (r<a) and outside (r>a) the sphere, respectively. Inside the conductor the potential is constant; denote this constant by Φ_0. Let P_n denote the Legendre polynomials. Let σ(θ) denote the surface charge density on the sphere, and define the total charge by q = ∫∫ σ a^2 sinθ dφ dθ over the spherical surface. Let Φ_dip denote the dipole-field potential term outside the sphere. Let E_{3r} and E_{3θ} denote the r and θ components, respectively, of the induced dipole field in spherical coordinates. Let P denote the magnitude of the dipole moment vector of the induced surface charge, which is parallel to +z. Three fields are present: the uniform external field, the Coulomb field due to q on the conductor, and the induced dipole field. Define their self-energies by W_1, W_2, W_3, and their pairwise interaction energies by W_{12}, W_{13}, W_{23}. Also let w_1 and w_3 denote the energy densities of the uniform field and the induced dipole field, respectively. Tasks: find Φ_1, Φ_2 for all r, find P, and identify the six energy terms W_1, W_2, W_3, W_{12}, W_{13}, W_{23}, stating which are finite, zero, or infinite. Report final answers using the symbols defined here.

An infinite straight wire of radius a carries a uniform linear charge density σ. The electric potential at a radial distance r from the wire’s axis (in the units implied by the given formula) is specified by φ(r) = (σ/(2π)) ln(1/r) + const, where const is an arbitrary reference constant. This wire is placed parallel to and at a distance b ≫ a from an infinite grounded conducting plane, whose potential is maintained at zero. Define C as the capacitance per unit length of the wire relative to the plane. Find C in terms of a and b.

Suppose that the half-space z>0 in three-dimensional space is filled with a linear dielectric material characterized by dielectric constant ε1, while the half-space z<0 is filled with a linear dielectric material characterized by dielectric constant ε2. A point charge −q is fixed at position (x,y,z)=(0,0,a) with a>0, and a point charge +q is fixed at (0,0,−a). Using Gaussian units, determine the external force required to keep the negative charge at rest. Let F denote the electric force on the negative charge −q due to the other charge and the induced image charges. Let F_ext denote the external force that must be applied to keep −q at rest, with F_ext=−F.

A perfectly conducting isolated sphere of radius a is placed in vacuum of permittivity epsilon_0. A point charge of magnitude q is located on the positive z-axis at a distance r>a from the center of the sphere. Use Cartesian coordinates with origin at the sphere center and the z-axis along the line joining the center to the external charge. Let E be the electric field and e_z the unit vector along the +z direction. The notation a_+ (respectively a_-) denotes evaluation at the point on the spherical surface approached from outside along +z (respectively along -z). Let sigma denote the induced surface charge density on the sphere, and let E_n be the outward normal component of E just outside the conductor, related by sigma=epsilon_0 E_n. If a net charge Q is given to the sphere, it distributes uniformly on the surface with surface density Q/(4 pi a^2). Define Q_min^{(+)} as the least positive value of Q such that the total surface charge density on the sphere is nonnegative everywhere when the external charge is +q. Define Q_min^{(-)} similarly for the case when the external charge is -q. You may use the method of images for an insulated sphere: for an external charge +q at z=r, the conducting sphere is replaced by image charges q_1=-(a/r)q at z=a^2/r and q_2=(a/r)q at z=0. For an external charge -q at z=r, replace q by -q in the same image configuration. Find Q_min^{(+)} and Q_min^{(-)}.

Inside a smoke precipitator, consider a long straight wire of radius R carrying a uniform linear charge density λ (charge per unit length). An uncharged spherical dielectric smoke particle of dielectric constant ε and radius a, with a < R, is located at a radial distance r from the wire axis. Work in Gaussian units. Let e_r denote the outward radial unit vector. Define E_ex(r) as the external electric field of the wire at r, E_in as the uniform electric field inside a dielectric sphere placed in a uniform external field, P as the induced electric dipole moment of the sphere in the external field, W(r) as the interaction energy of the polarized sphere with the external field, and F(r) as the force on the sphere. Assume a << R so the field is approximately uniform over the particle. Find the force of attraction just before the particle touches the wire by evaluating at r = R. Also discuss in physical terms the origin of the force

Consider the person nearest to you and approximate the person as an isolated conducting sphere with effective radius R. The room is electromagnetically shielded and in thermal equilibrium at temperature T. Let C be the capacitance of the sphere, Q be its electrical charge, U be the electrostatic energy stored on the sphere, k be Boltzmann's constant, and ε_0 be the vacuum permittivity. Using the classical equipartition theorem, estimate the root-mean-square electrical charge Q_{rms} on the person. Evaluate your result numerically for R=0.5\unit{m} and T=300\unit{K}.

Let v_d denote the drift velocity of electrons in a copper (Cu) wire of diameter d=1\unit{mm} carrying current I=10\unit{A}. Choose the correct value of v_d from the options below. Options: 10^{-5}\unit{cm/s}, 10^{-2}\unit{cm/s}, 10^{1}\unit{cm/s}, 10^{5}\unit{cm/s}

Let v_r denote the average random speed of electrons in a conductor. Choose the correct value from the options and report your answer in the form v_r=... Options:
A)
$$10^2 \unit{cm/s}$$
B)
$$10^4 \unit{cm/s}$$
C)
$$10^6 \unit{cm/s}$$
D)
$$10^8 \unit{cm/s}$$

A cylindrical soft iron rod of length L and diameter d is bent into a circular shape of radius R, leaving an air gap of width s between the nearly meeting ends. Assume s << d and d << R, and neglect fringing. A coil of N turns is wound tightly around the iron rod, and a current I flows through the coil. The relative permeability of the iron is μ_r, and μ_0 is the permeability of free space. Let B be the magnetic flux density in the gap and H be the magnetic field strength used in Ampere's circuital law. Find B in the gap.

Consider a spherical surface of radius R that carries a surface current density K_s(θ) configured to produce a uniform magnetic field of magnitude B inside the sphere, directed along the +z axis. Work in spherical coordinates (r, θ, φ), where θ is the polar angle measured from the +z axis, with unit vectors e_r, e_θ, e_φ, and let e_z be the Cartesian unit vector along +z. Let n = e_r denote the outward unit normal on the spherical surface r = R. Introduce a fictitious uniform magnetization vector M_vec of constant magnitude M, directed along +z, defined by M_vec = M e_z. Let μ_0 denote the vacuum permeability.

A uniform cylindrical outer coil in vacuum has radius r_1=1 \unit{m}, length l_1=1 \unit{m}, and number of turns N_1=100. Coaxial and centered within it is a smaller coil with radius r_2=\frac{1}{10} \unit{m}, length l_2=\frac{1}{10} \unit{m}, and number of turns N_2=10. Let \mu_0 denote the permeability of free space. Let I_1 be the current in the outer coil, producing magnetic flux density B_1 along its axis. Let S_2 be the cross-sectional area of the inner coil. Let \psi_{12} be the total flux linkage of the inner coil due to B_1. Let M be the mutual inductance between the two coils. Calculate M and give a numerical value in henries.

A closely wound search coil has N=160 turns, cross-sectional area A=4×10^{-4} \unit{m^2}, and coil resistance R=50 \unit{\Omega}. It is connected in series to a ballistic galvanometer of resistance r=30 \unit{\Omega}. The coil is rapidly rotated from a position with its plane parallel to a uniform magnetic field to one where its plane is perpendicular to the field. The galvanometer indicates a total charge q=4×10^{-5} \unit{C} passed through it during the transient. Neglect self-inductance and any other resistances. Determine the magnetic flux density B of the field. Symbols: B is the magnetic flux density, A is the coil area, N is the number of turns, R is the coil resistance, r is the galvanometer resistance, q is the total charge, i is the transient current, \varepsilon is the induced emf, \Delta \Phi is the change in total magnetic flux through the coil, and \Delta t is the short rotation time

A rectangular loop of perfectly conducting wire with side lengths a and b, mass m, and self-inductance L moves in its plane from a region of zero magnetic field into a region with a uniform magnetic field of magnitude B_0 perpendicular to the plane of the loop. Take b > a, and assume the motion is along the direction of the longer side b so that the leading and trailing edges that intersect the field boundary have length a. At time t=0, the loop is at the boundary with initial speed v_0, zero current I(0)=0, and displacement s(0)=0. Let v(t) denote the speed along the direction of motion and s(t) the displacement along that direction measured from the position at t=0. Describe v(t) and s(t) as functions of time.

A solid copper penny (uniform conducting disk) of radius r_0 and thickness h is placed on its circular edge in a uniform vertical magnetic field of magnitude B. The penny is given a slight push so that it begins to tip and fall under gravity. Let theta be the angle between the plane of the penny and the vertical axis, with initial angle theta_0 at the start of the fall and final angle pi/2 at the end. Assume that eddy currents are induced in the penny and that, at each instant during the fall, the magnetic torque balances the gravitational torque so that the gravitational potential energy is converted predominantly into heat. Let sigma be the electrical conductivity of copper, rho its mass density, and g the gravitational acceleration. Use SI units throughout. For a numerical estimate, use B = 2 \unit{T}, sigma = 6 \times 10^7 \unit{S/m}, rho = 8.9 \times 10^3 \unit{kg/m^3}, g = 9.8 \unit{m/s^2}, r_0 = 0.01 \unit{m}, h arbitrary but cancelling, and theta_0 = 0.1 \unit{rad}. Let T be the total time for the penny to fall from theta_0 to pi/2. Estimate T.

Show that the force between two magnetic dipoles varies as the inverse fourth power of the distance between their centers, for any relative orientation in space. Assume the dipoles are small compared with their separation and the medium is vacuum.
