Definitions: m_1 and m_2 are the magnetic dipole moment vectors, \vec{r} is the displacement vector from the center of dipole m_2 to the center of dipole m_1, r is the magnitude of \vec{r}, \mu_0 is the vacuum permeability, \varphi_m is the magnetic scalar potential due to m_2, \vec{B} is the magnetic flux density produced by m_2 at the location of m_1, and \vec{F}_m is the force on dipole m_1. The gradient operator \nabla acts with respect to the observation point at the location of m_1.

Consider an inertial frame S. The uniform electromagnetic fields are specified in Gaussian-cgs units by E = 3 A e_x and B = 5 A e_z, where A is a positive constant and e_x, e_y, e_z are the Cartesian unit vectors. An ion of rest mass m_0 and charge q is released from rest at position (x,y,z) = (0,b,0) at time t = 0. Let c denote the speed of light in vacuum. Let t_ret denote the time that elapses until the ion first returns to the y-axis, i.e., until x(t_ret) = 0 with z(t) constrained to remain zero by the initial conditions. Find t_ret.

Four identical capacitors of single-capacitance C_0=1\unit{\mu F} are connected in parallel, charged to voltage V=200\unit{V}, and then discharged through a fine copper wire of length L=5\unit{mm}. The wire has resistance per unit length r=4\unit{\Omega/m} and mass per unit length \mu=0.045\unit{g/m}. Take the melting point of copper as T_m=1356\unit{^\circ C}, the specific heat of copper as c=0.091\unit{cal/(g^\circ C)}, and the initial temperature as T_0=25\unit{^\circ C}. Neglect latent heat of fusion and any heat losses, and assume all stored electrical energy is converted to thermal energy in the wire. Define the following quantities to be used in the solution: total capacitance C, stored energy E, wire resistance R, wire mass m, temperature rise \Delta T=T_m-T_0, and the sensible heat required Q=c\,m\,\Delta T. Decide whether the wire melts by comparing E and Q. Report the final decision using an indicator variable M, where M=1 if the wire melts and M=0 otherwise.

A solenoid with N=100 uniformly spaced turns has diameter D=2 \unit{cm} and length l=10 \unit{cm}. Assume edge effects are negligible. Use \mu_0=4\pi\times 10^{-7} \unit{T m/A}. Let r be the solenoid radius (r=D/2), A its cross-sectional area, I the current through the windings, n the turn density (n=N/l), B the magnetic field inside the solenoid (assumed uniform), \Phi the magnetic flux through one turn (\Phi=B A), \psi the total flux linkage for the coil (\psi=N \Phi), and L the inductance to be found.

A circular conducting loop is placed between the pole faces of an electromagnet with its plane parallel to the pole faces. The loop has radius a, total resistance R, and self-inductance L. The magnetic field is initially zero. Starting at time t=0, the magnet is turned on so that a uniform magnetic field of constant final magnitude B, directed normal to the loop, fills the area of the loop for t→∞. Assume B is uniform across the loop area at all times during the turn-on. Find the total electric charge q that flows past any fixed point on the loop during the entire turn-on process. Variable definitions and SI units: a is the loop radius in \unit{m}, R is the total loop resistance in \unit{\Omega}, L is the self-inductance in \unit{H}, B is the final magnetic field magnitude in \unit{T}, t is time in \unit{s}, \phi is the magnetic flux through the loop in \unit{Wb}, \varepsilon is the induced emf in \unit{V}, i is the current in \unit{A}, and q is the total charge that passes a point on the loop in \unit{C}. The loop area is \pi a^2, and positive q is taken along the direction of the induced current

A long air-core solenoid has length l = 1/2\unit{m}, cross-sectional area S = 1\unit{cm^2}, and N = 1000 turns. Neglect end effects and use \mu_0 = 4\pi\times 10^{-7}\unit{H/m}. A secondary winding wrapped around the center of the solenoid has N' = 100 turns. The solenoid winding is connected to a load resistance R = 10^3\unit{\Omega}. A constant current I = 1\unit{A} flows in the secondary winding and is suddenly stopped at time t = 0^+. Define: n = N/l as the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid, B as the magnetic field inside the solenoid, \phi as the magnetic flux through one turn of the solenoid, \psi as the total flux linkage of the solenoid winding, \psi' as the total flux linkage of the secondary winding, L as the self-inductance of the solenoid, M as the mutual inductance between the windings, i(t) as the induced current in the solenoid after the interruption, q as the total charge that flows through the resistance during the transient, and t as time. Find L, M, and q.

You have n identical storage cells, each with open-circuit voltage V and internal resistance R_i. The cells are grouped into sets of k series-connected cells each, where k is a positive integer that divides n. The n/k sets are then connected in parallel across a load resistance R. Let V_eq denote the open-circuit voltage of the combined source seen by the load, R_eq the equivalent internal resistance of the combined source, and P_max the maximum power delivered to the load. Find the value of k that maximizes the power delivered to R, and find the corresponding maximum power.

A compound microscope has an objective of focal length f_1 = 1/2 cm and an eyepiece of focal length f_2 = 2 cm. The distance between the two thin lenses is d = 22 cm. The observer focuses so that the final image is at infinity. The normal near point of the eye is N = 15 cm. Use the thin lens equation for each lens. Define p_1 and q_1 as the object and image distances for the objective, and p_2 and q_2 for the eyepiece. Let y be the object height and y' the image height formed by the objective. Let alpha be the angle subtended at the unaided eye by the object at distance N, and alpha' the angle subtended at the eye by the final image formed by the microscope. Let the magnifying power be m = alpha'/alpha. Find the required object distance p_1 from the objective and the magnifying power m. Give numerical answers within 10%. Derive all necessary relations starting from the lens equation.

A thin lens of focal length f forms an image of the Sun on its focal plane. Assume: (1) the Sun is a Lambertian source with uniform radiance (brightness) L measured in \unit{W/cm^2/sr}, (2) the Sun’s projected emitting area (its apparent disk) is S at distance R from the lens, (3) the lens has clear aperture area A, (4) the image of the solar disk on the focal plane has area S', and (5) there is no attenuation in the atmosphere or in the lens. Prove that the image radiance L' on the focal plane is approximately equal to the Sun’s surface radiance L, i.e., L' \approx L. You may use that the solid angle subtended by the source at the lens is S/R^2 and that the solid angle subtended by the lens as seen from the focal plane is A/f^2. Define Φ as the optical power collected by the lens from the Sun and Φ' as the optical power carried through the image region on the focal plane toward the lens.

A flat white sheet on Earth is illuminated by the Sun when it is overhead. A thin lens of radius r and focal length f is used to form an image of the Sun on the sheet. Only light passing through the lens reaches the image. Let B be the Sun's brightness (luminous radiance), modeled as Lambertian. Let sigma' be the image area on the sheet; for comparison, use the same area sigma' on the sheet when illuminated directly by the Sun without the lens. Let Phi be the luminous flux reaching area sigma' directly from the Sun, and let Phi' be the luminous flux on the same area sigma' in the lens-formed image. Let d Omega be the solid angle subtended by the Sun at Earth and let alpha be the Sun's angular aperture used in d Omega = pi alpha^2. From Earth, take alpha ≈ 0.01 rad. Let u' be the semi-angular aperture of the lens as seen from the image point, so that sin u' = r/f (in air). Tasks: 1) Find the flux gain G = Phi'/Phi on the image area. 2) For a given lens radius r_1, find the condition on f such that there is no increase in flux on the image area, i.e., G ≤ 1. Use r, r_1, and f in units of meters and alpha in units of radians.

The width of a certain spectral line at wavelength λ = 500 nm is Δλ = 2 × 10^{1} nm. Let l_c denote the coherence length of the light and let Δ_max denote the largest optical path difference for which interference fringes produced by this light are clearly visible. Assume that fringes remain clearly visible when the optical path difference does not exceed one quarter of the coherence length. Approximately what is Δ_max?

Find the thickness d of a uniform, parallel-sided soap film that produces constructive second-order interference in the reflected light. The light has wavelength in air λ = 7000\unit{\AA}. The film has refractive index n = 1.33. A parallel beam is incident from air (refractive index ≈ 1) at an angle to the normal θ_0 = 30\unit{^\circ}. Let Δ denote the optical path difference between the two reflected rays, and let θ be the angle of refraction inside the film. Account for the single phase reversal upon reflection at the upper air-to-film surface. Report the required film thickness as d.

White light is incident normally from air on a plane-parallel thin film. The film has refractive index n = 3/2 and thickness t = 5000 \unit{A}. Let \lambda denote the wavelength in air. Let k be a nonnegative integer (interference order). Let OPD denote the optical path difference between the two principal reflected rays. The visible spectrum is taken to be 4000–7000 \unit{A}. Let \lambda_{max,i} denote the wavelengths in air within 4000–7000 \unit{A} for which the reflected intensity is a maximum, indexed by increasing k. Determine all such \lambda_{max,i}.

A TiO2 film of index n1 = 2.5 is placed on glass of index n2 = 1.5 to increase the reflection in the visible. The ambient is air with index n0 = 1. Choose a suitable visible wavelength λ0 and determine the film thickness d in microns and the resulting reflectivity R_{λ0}. Use n0 for the refractive index of air, n1 for the refractive index of the TiO2 film, n2 for the refractive index of the glass, d for the film thickness, λ0 for the chosen wavelength in vacuum, k for an integer interference order, OPD for the optical path difference between the two main reflected rays at normal incidence, and R_{λ0} for the reflectivity at λ0.

Monochromatic light of wavelength λ is normally incident on a screen with a circular aperture of radius R. Let z denote the distance from the screen measured along the axis normal to the screen through the center of the aperture, into the region behind the screen. The on-axis light intensity I(z) vanishes at certain discrete points along z. In the regime λ/R ≪ 1, find the most distant point from the screen at which the on-axis intensity vanishes. Define z_max as the greatest z for which I(z)=0.

A plane wave of microwaves with wavelength lambda = 1 cm is incident on an opaque screen containing a circular aperture of adjustable radius. A detector with a very small sensitive area is located on the axis of the aperture at distance r_0 = 1 m behind it. As the aperture radius is increased from zero, determine the aperture radius at which the detector response first reaches a maximum and the aperture radius at which it reaches its second minimum after the first maximum. At the latter aperture radius, determine the on-axis positions of the successive maxima and minima. Use rho_m for the radius of the m-th Fresnel half-period zone corresponding to an on-axis point at distance r_0, and use r_k for the on-axis distance at which the fixed aperture radius equals rho_k. The integers m and k are positive and start from 1.

Light from a monochromatic point source of wavelength $\lambda$ is focused to a point image by a Fresnel half-period zone plate having 100 open odd half-period zones $(1,3,5,\ldots,199)$ with all even zones opaque. Compare the image-point intensity with that at the same point for the zone plate removed, and for a lens of the same focal length and diameter corresponding to 200 half-period zones of the zone plate. Assume the diameter of the opening is small compared to the distance from the source and the image. Definitions for use in the solution: Let $A_i$ denote the complex amplitude contribution at the image point from the $i$th half-period zone when that zone is open. Let $A$ be the resultant amplitude at the image point with the zone plate in place, and let $I$ be the corresponding intensity. Let $A'$ be the resultant amplitude at the image point when a lens of the same focal length and the same overall diameter (corresponding to 200 half-period zones) replaces the zone plate, and let $I'$ be the corresponding intensity.

A transmission-type diffraction grating has line density n_g = 250 lines per millimeter. The grating is illuminated at normal incidence to the grooves. Using the grating equation d sin theta = k lambda, where d = 1/n_g is the groove spacing, theta is the diffraction angle measured from the normal, k is the diffraction order (a positive integer), and lambda is the vacuum wavelength, determine all wavelengths within the visible range 4000 \unit{\AA} to 7000 \unit{\AA} that appear at the diffraction angle theta = 30 \unit{deg}. Report the wavelengths as lambda_i together with their diffraction orders k, and state the corresponding perceived colors.

A reflecting telescope has an objective diameter D=8 cm, an objective focal length f=3/2 m, and is used with an eyepiece of magnification M=80. Assume monochromatic light of wavelength λ=6000 Å. Take the unaided human eye’s resolving limit to be θ_eye=1 arcminute. Use the Rayleigh criterion for a circular aperture Δθ1=1.22 λ/D for the telescope and define the eye-limited resolution at the eyepiece as Δθ2=θ_eye/M. Let Δθ be the resolvable angular separation in arcseconds, defined by Δθ=max(Δθ1,Δθ2). Use 1 Å=10^{-10} m and 1 rad=206265 arcsec.

Consider diffraction of monochromatic light through a single slit of width D (taken as the telescope's objective diameter). A telescope has objective diameter D equal to 1/2 meter. The light wavelength is λ equal to 5×10^{-7} meters. The Earth–Moon distance is L equal to 3.8×10^{8} meters. Using the single-slit diffraction criterion for angular resolution θ_1 = λ/D, estimate the smallest resolvable lunar crater diameter d_min, where d_min = L θ_1. Use E_k for kinetic energy, E_p for potential energy, E for total energy if needed (not used here), M for the mass of the central body, m for the mass of the satellite, and R for the radius of the orbit (not used here).

Let s denote the separation between the two headlights, D the diameter of the eye's pupil, λ the mean wavelength of the light, θ the angular resolving limit of the eye, and L the maximum distance at which the two headlights can just be resolved. Given s = 1.3 m, D = 4 mm, and λ = 550 nm, estimate L using the Rayleigh criterion for a circular aperture.

The Space Telescope, as currently planned, will have a mirror diameter D=2\unit{m} and will orbit above the Earth's atmosphere. Two stars are at a common distance R=10^{22}\unit{cm} from the Earth. Using the Rayleigh criterion in order-of-magnitude form, take the angular resolution to be \theta\approx\frac{\lambda}{D}, where \lambda is the observing wavelength and D is the telescope diameter. Take \lambda=600\unit{nm} as a representative visible wavelength. Let d denote the minimal resolvable linear separation between the two stars at distance R. Estimate d.

A parabolic mirror with diameter 10\unit{cm} and focal length 500\unit{cm} is used to photograph stars. Assume the relative aperture is small so that geometric aberrations are negligible and the resolution is limited by diffraction. Define D as the mirror diameter, f as the focal length, and \lambda as the imaging wavelength. For visible light, take \lambda = 500\unit{nm} (equivalently 5000\unit{\AA}). Let \theta be the off-axis field angle of a star relative to the optical axis. Let d_{on} denote the diameter in the focal plane of the on-axis star’s diffraction-limited image (Airy disk), and let d_{off} denote the corresponding diameter for a star at field angle \theta. Discuss the dominant limitations of resolution on axis and off axis, and compute d_{on} for the given parameters.

Four perfect linear polarizing plates are stacked so that the transmission axis of each plate is turned 30 degrees clockwise with respect to the preceding plate. An unpolarized light beam with incident intensity I_0 is sent onto the first plate. Let I_k denote the intensity after the k-th plate for k=1,2,3,4. Let theta be the fixed relative angle between successive transmission axes, with theta=30 degrees. Let T be the transmitted fraction defined by T=I_4/I_0, which is the required answer. For reference, Malus' law states that for a linearly polarized beam with incident intensity I_in impinging on a polarizer whose transmission axis makes an angle theta with the incident polarization direction, the transmitted intensity is I_out, where I_out=I_in cos^2 theta.

A He–Ne laser operating at vacuum wavelength λ0=6328\unit{\AA} has a resonant cavity with plane end mirrors separated by l=\frac{1}{2}\unit{m}. Calculate the frequency separation between the axial modes Δf of this laser. Then, given that the spontaneous-emission linewidth of the Ne 6328\unit{\AA} transition is Δλ=0.016\unit{\AA}, estimate the number of axial frequencies N_ax that can oscillate simultaneously. Use c for the speed of light in vacuum, n for the integer axial mode index, f_n for the nth axial mode frequency, Δf for the axial mode spacing, and Δf' for the frequency bandwidth corresponding to Δλ via Δf'=c\frac{Δλ}{λ0^{2}}. Use 1\unit{\AA}=10^{-10}\unit{m}. Express numerical results in SI units.

Calculate the intensity reflectance R, defined as the ratio of the reflected intensity to the incident intensity, for monochromatic light incident normally from air onto the surface of a deep body of water. Let n_1 be the refractive index of air and n_2 be the refractive index of water. Use n_1=1 and n_2=1.33. Let I_r denote the reflected intensity and I_i denote the incident intensity. Neglect absorption and assume non-magnetic dielectric media

An aquarium is being filled with water of refractive index n>1. A motionless fish looks vertically upward through the rising water surface at a stationary monochromatic plane-wave light source located above the water. The water moves upward with speed V relative to the fish. Let c be the speed of light in vacuum, let nu be the source frequency in vacuum, let u be the speed of light in the moving water relative to the fish, let lambda_{water} be the wavelength of the light in water when the water is at rest, and let nu' be the frequency measured by the fish. Using Fresnel's drag law for light in a medium moving at speed V relative to the observer, determine whether the fish sees a blue shift, a red shift, or no change in frequency. Report the final answer by comparing nu' and nu

Estimate the luminous efficiency η_L (visible watt per input watt) for any two of the following light sources: gas light, incandescent bulb, light emitting diode, fluorescent tube, mercury arc, sodium arc, laser. Definitions: Let η_L denote luminous efficiency, defined by the ratio of the radiant power emitted in the visible range to the total input power. Let P_vis be the radiant power in the visible range and P_in be the total electrical input power. The visible range is taken as wavelengths λ from 400 to 760 in nanometers. Let dP/dλ denote the spectral radiant power density as a function of wavelength λ. Subscripts on η_L indicate the source, e.g., η_{L,gas} for gas light.

Estimate the external electric field magnitude required to pull an electron out of a hydrogen-like atom in a time comparable to half an orbital period. Let Z be the nuclear charge number, e the magnitude of the electron charge, E the magnitude of the external electric field, a the average orbital radius, and a_0 the Bohr radius. Use the data: the ionization energy is 13.6 Z^2 in units of electronvolts, the average orbital radius is a=a_0/Z, and the Bohr radius is a_0=0.53×10^{-8} in units of centimeters.

The range of the potential between two hydrogen atoms is approximately 4 angstroms. For a gas in thermal equilibrium, obtain a numerical estimate of the temperature below which the atom-atom scattering is essentially s-wave. Use the following definitions: a is the interaction length between hydrogen atoms, taken as a=4 angstroms, k is the de Broglie wave number, p is the momentum, E_k is the kinetic energy, m is the mass of the hydrogen atom, k_B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature, and \hbar is the reduced Planck constant. Use 4 angstroms = 4×10^{-10} meters when evaluating numerically.

Let \tau denote the typical lifetime of an excited atom. Choose the correct order of magnitude for \tau from the options below.

$$\tau=10^{-1}\unit{s}$$

$$\tau=10^{-8}\unit{s}$$

$$\tau=10^{-13}\unit{s}$$

$$\tau=10^{-23}\unit{s}$$

An atom can exist in two states: a ground state with rest mass M and an excited state with rest mass M + Δ. In the laboratory frame where the atom is initially at rest, a photon is absorbed to induce the transition to the excited state. Let ν be the required photon frequency, p be the momentum of the atom after absorption, c be the speed of light in vacuum, and h be Planck's constant. Find ν.

An optical spectral line emitted by hydrogen atoms in a flame at room temperature is observed in the visible range with line-center frequency denoted by 
u_0. Estimate the Doppler width \Delta 
u due to thermal motion and identify the closest option from the set {10^{6}, 10^{9}, 10^{13}, 10^{16}} \unit{Hz}. Definitions and data: k is Boltzmann's constant, T is the absolute temperature, m is the mass of a hydrogen atom, c is the speed of light, v_{rms} is the root-mean-square speed, and  eta=v_{rms}/c. Use the nonrelativistic Doppler estimate \Delta 
u \approx 
u_0  eta with 
u_0 \approx 10^{14} \unit{Hz}. Take k=8.6	imes 10^{-5} \unit{eV/K}, T=300 \unit{K}, and mc^{2}\approx 10^{9} \unit{eV}.

Estimate (order of magnitude) the full Doppler width Δλ of an emission line with wavelength λ = 5000 \unit{Å} emitted by argon at temperature T = 300 \unit{K}. Use A = 40 for the mass number and Z = 18 for the atomic number. Let m be the mass of the argon atom, c the speed of light, k the Boltzmann constant, v the thermal speed, and \overline{v^{2}} the mean square thermal speed. Define β = v/c and let Δλ denote the full Doppler width. You may approximate the atomic rest energy by m c^{2} ≈ A × 938 \unit{MeV}. Report the final answer as Δλ.

In low-energy electron–atom scattering, estimate the typical scattering cross section. Let sigma denote the typical scattering cross section (area), and let a denote the linear dimension of an atom. Choose the value closest to sigma from the options below:
$$10^{-16} \unit{cm^2}$$
$$10^{-24} \unit{cm^2}$$
$$10^{-32} \unit{cm^2}$$
$$10^{-40} \unit{cm^2}$$

An electron of mass m is confined to the interior of a hollow spherical cavity of radius R with impenetrable walls. In its ground state (zero angular momentum), find P(R), the pressure exerted by the electron on the cavity walls. Use the following definitions: E is the electron's energy, P is the pressure on the wall, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, r is the radial coordinate, \Psi is the wave function, \chi(r) is the radial function defined by \Psi=\frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}}\frac{\chi(r)}{r}, k is the wave number, and A is a constant. The impenetrable wall imposes the boundary condition that the wave function vanishes at r=R.

A spin-1/2 particle with magnetic moment mu = mu_0 s is placed in a uniform magnetic field B pointing along the x axis. Work in the (s^2, s_x) representation, i.e., the eigenbasis of s_x. Let s = (s_x, s_y, s_z) denote the spin operators, with s_k = (1/2) sigma_k where sigma_k are the Pauli matrices in this basis. Let the time-dependent spinor be psi(t) = (a(t), b(t))^T in this basis. At t = 0 the particle is found to have s_z = +1/2. Here mu_0 is a constant, hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and i is the imaginary unit. Find the probabilities at time t of measuring s_y = ±1/2.

The ground state of the realistic helium atom is nondegenerate. However, consider a hypothetical helium atom in which the two electrons are replaced by two identical spin-1 particles of negative charge. Neglect spin-dependent forces. For this hypothetical atom, what is the degeneracy of the ground state? Give your reasoning. Definitions for clarity: Let S denote the total spin quantum number of the two-particle system. For a given S, let g_S denote the spin multiplicity, where g_S = 2S + 1. Let g_{gs} denote the total degeneracy of the ground state to be found.

Compute the hyperfine energy splitting E_hf in the ground state of atomic hydrogen using Planck's relation ΔE = h Δν_hf, where h is Planck's constant, Δν_hf is the hyperfine transition frequency, and ΔE is the corresponding energy difference. Take Δν_hf = 1.42 × 10^9 s^{-1} (experimental value for atomic hydrogen) and h = 4.14 × 10^{-15} eV s. From the options 10^{-7} eV, 10^{-5} eV, 10^{-3} eV, 10^{-1} eV, select the closest value to E_hf and report it as E_choice. Use E_hf for the hyperfine energy splitting, ΔE for the energy difference, h for Planck's constant, Δν_hf for the transition frequency, and E_choice for the selected option.

Choose the closest option for the spin–orbit splitting energy of the hydrogen 2p state from {10^{-6}, 10^{-4}, 10^{-2}, 10^{0}} eV. Let n be the principal quantum number, l the orbital angular momentum quantum number, s the electron spin quantum number, and j the total electronic angular momentum. For the hydrogen 2p state use n=2, l=1, s=1/2, with fine-structure levels labeled by j_1=3/2 and j_2=1/2. Let R be the Rydberg constant, h Planck's constant, c the speed of light, and alpha the fine-structure constant. Use h c R = 13.6 eV and alpha = 1/137. Let E_split denote the spin–orbit splitting energy between the j_1 and j_2 levels to be computed, and let E_option denote the selected option that best matches E_split.

We may generalize the semiclassical Bohr–Sommerfeld relation for a closed real-space orbit C by replacing p with p - eA/c in the integrand. In zero field the rule is $$ \oint p\cdot dr = \left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)2\pi\hbar $$ In an electromagnetic field we use $$ \oint \left(p-\frac{e}{c}A\right)\cdot dr = \left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)2\pi\hbar $$ Use this and the equation of motion for the linear momentum p in a uniform magnetic field B to derive a quantized condition on the magnetic flux Φ through any surface Σ bounded by C. For electrons in a solid this condition can be restated in terms of the k-space orbit area S. Let A_r denote the real-space area enclosed by C in a plane perpendicular to B, and let S denote the area enclosed by the corresponding k-space orbit. You may use the relations Δp = ħ Δk and Δp = (e/c) B Δr for small in-plane displacements of magnitudes Δr and Δk. Ignore spin effects. Symbols: p is the electron linear momentum, r is the position vector, A is the vector potential, B is the magnetic field, C is the closed real-space orbit, Σ is any surface bounded by C, dS is the oriented area element on Σ with vector form dS, Φ is the magnetic flux through Σ, A_r is the real-space area enclosed by C, k is the crystal wavevector, S is the k-space orbit area, n is a nonnegative integer, e is the magnitude of the electron charge, c is the speed of light, and ħ is the reduced Planck constant.

Consider a very small uniform-density sphere of charge in an electrostatic potential V(r). To leading order in its very small radius r_0, its potential energy is given by the truncation of the multipole expansion below. Use this to treat the finite-size correction as a small perturbation to the Coulomb potential of the hydrogen atom and compute the resulting Lamb shifts for the 1s and 2p levels. Let ΔE_{1s} and ΔE_{2p} denote the first-order energy shifts of the 1s and 2p levels, respectively. Use the unperturbed hydrogenic wave functions provided and express your results in terms of r_0 and fundamental constants. The unperturbed potential energy of the electron in the hydrogen atom is V(r) = - e^2 / r with e > 0 the magnitude of the electron charge. The unperturbed wave functions are normalized with spherical harmonics Y_l^m chosen so that Y_0^0 = 1, and m ∈ {-1,0,1} for l = 1. The Bohr radius is a_B = ħ^2 / (m_e e^2), where m_e is the electron mass and ħ is the reduced Planck constant.

Problem. Derive an expression for the hyperfine energy splitting of an atomic level produced by the magnetic dipole hyperfine interaction between the nuclear magnetic moment and the magnetic field of a single electron. Work to first order in perturbation theory for a hydrogenic one-electron atom with nuclear charge Z. Use the unperturbed atomic state |l s j I F M_F>, where l is the electron orbital angular momentum quantum number, s is the electron spin quantum number, j is the total electronic angular momentum quantum number, I is the nuclear spin quantum number, F is the total atomic angular momentum quantum number, and M_F is its z-component. Define all quantities as follows: r is the electron position vector relative to the nucleus, v is the electron velocity, r' is the displacement from the electron to a generic field point, A is the vector potential, B_e is the magnetic field from the electron’s orbital motion, B_s is the magnetic field from the electron’s spin magnetic moment, H_{lI} and H_{sI} are the corresponding interaction Hamiltonians, and H_{hf} is the total hyperfine Hamiltonian. The electron mass is m, the vacuum permeability is mu_0, the elementary charge is e, and the reduced Planck constant is hbar. The Bohr magneton is mu_B = e hbar/(2 m). The nuclear magnetic moment is m_N = g_N mu_N I/hbar, where mu_N is the nuclear magneton and g_N is the nuclear Landé g-factor. The Bohr radius is a_0 and n is the principal quantum number. The operators l, s, I, and j are vector operators for orbital, spin, nuclear, and total electronic angular momenta, respectively, and F is the total atomic angular momentum operator with F = I + j. The Dirac delta is denoted by delta(r). The gradient with respect to r is denoted by nabla, and that with respect to r' is denoted by nabla'. Use the identity nabla^2(1/r) = -4 pi delta(r). Express your final answers for the hyperfine energy corrections Delta E in terms of the angular momentum quantum numbers and, where needed, the hydrogenic expectation values.
problem_answer: $$\Delta E = \frac{2 \mu_0 g_N \mu_N \mu_B}{3 \pi} \cdot \frac{Z^3}{a_0^3 n^3} \cdot \left[ F(F+1) - I(I+1) - s(s+1) \right]$$

problem_description: What is the energy of the photon emitted in the transition from the n=3 to n=2 level of the μ− mesic atom of carbon? Express the answer in terms of the γ energy for the electronic transition from n=2 to n=1 of hydrogen. Use the following definitions: Z is the atomic number of carbon with Z=6, m_μ is the muon mass, m_e is the electron mass with m_μ/m_e=210, E_n(H) is the energy of the nth bound state of the hydrogen atom (electronic, Z=1), E_n(μC) is the energy of the nth bound state of the μ− mesic carbon atom, E_γ^{(μC,3→2)} is the photon energy for the μ− mesic carbon transition n=3→2, and E_γ^{(H,2→1)} is the photon energy for the hydrogen electronic transition n=2→1.

A mu- meson (negatively charged muon) of mass M = 210 m_e, where m_e is the electron mass, is captured into a circular orbit around a proton. Its initial orbital radius R is approximately the Bohr radius of an electron bound to a proton. Using classical arguments, including the Larmor formula for the power radiated by a nonrelativistic accelerating charge, estimate the time t it takes the mu- meson to radiate enough energy to reach its ground state. Use Gaussian electromagnetic units for intermediate formulas. Definitions: e is the elementary charge magnitude, c is the speed of light, ℏ is the reduced Planck constant, r is the instantaneous orbital radius of the mu- around the proton, K(r) is the kinetic energy, E(r) is the total mechanical energy, F_Coul is the magnitude of the Coulomb force, a is the centripetal acceleration magnitude, P is the radiated power, t is time, R is the initial orbital radius, and r_0 is the ground-state orbital radius of the muonic hydrogen atom. In intermediate steps, m denotes m_e.

Given are the 2p binding energies for two elements. For niobium with atomic number Z = 41, the measured 2p binding energies are 2370 \unit{eV} for the 2P_{1/2} level and 2465 \unit{eV} for the 2P_{3/2} level. For lead with atomic number Z = 82, the measured 2p binding energies are 13035 \unit{eV} for the 2P_{1/2} level and 15200 \unit{eV} for the 2P_{3/2} level. The observed trends are that the average 2p binding energy is roughly proportional to (Z - a)^2, and the fine-structure splitting between the 2P_{1/2} and 2P_{3/2} levels is roughly proportional to (Z - a)^4. Explain these scalings using a central-potential model with effective screening and spin-orbit coupling, and determine reasonable screening constants. Use the following definitions and conventions: E is the energy of the 2p electron, B is the binding energy with B = -E, R h c is the Rydberg energy in electron-volts, \alpha is the fine-structure constant, j is the total angular momentum quantum number for the 2p levels (j = 1/2 or j = 3/2), a_1 is the effective screening constant entering the central-field term that controls the average binding energy, and a_2 is the effective screening constant entering the fine-structure term that controls the splitting. State clearly how the data for niobium and lead determine a_1 and a_2, and report numerical values for a_1 and a_2 for each element. Note that according to Hund's rule the 2P_{3/2} level lies lower in energy than the 2P_{1/2} level, so it has the larger binding energy. In your final numerical expressions, use \unit{} for units. The final answers should be written in the explicit forms a_1^{\mathrm{Nb}} = \dots, a_2^{\mathrm{Nb}} = \dots, a_1^{\mathrm{Pb}} = \dots, a_2^{\mathrm{Pb}} = \dots

Sketch a derivation of the Landé g-factor g, i.e., the factor determining the effective magnetic moment of an atom in weak magnetic fields. Let P_L, P_S, and P_J denote the total orbital, total spin, and total angular momentum operators of the electrons, respectively, all expressed in units of ħ, so that their squared magnitudes have eigenvalues L(L+1), S(S+1), and J(J+1). The corresponding magnetic moments are defined by mu_L = - mu_B P_L and mu_S = - 2 mu_B P_S, where mu_B is the Bohr magneton. The total magnetic moment is taken to be mu_J = - g mu_B P_J, which defines the Landé g-factor g. Derive g in terms of the quantum numbers L, S, and J.

Discuss qualitatively the Stark shift due to a constant external electric field of the n = 2 energy levels of hydrogen. Neglect spin. Include the observed zero-field splitting W between the 2s and 2p states, where E_{2s} and E_{2p} are the unperturbed 2s and 2p energies. Let e denote the positive elementary charge magnitude, a_0 the Bohr radius, r the electron position operator, and E the constant external electric field vector with magnitude E_0 = |E|. Use the hydrogenic basis kets |n l m>, and focus on the four n = 2 states |200>, |210>, |211>, |21 -1>. Define H' as the perturbation Hamiltonian due to the electric field. Define u as the dipole matrix element u = <210|H'|200>. Let E_+ and E_- denote the two perturbed energies within the subspace spanned by |200> and |210>. Use the following datum for the zero-field splitting:

Approximate the molecule 1H35Cl as a rigid dumbbell with internuclear separation r = 1.29×10^{-10} \unit{m}. Use m_H = 1 amu and m_Cl = 35 amu with 1 amu = 1.67×10^{-27} \unit{kg}, and Planck's constant h = 6.6×10^{-34} \unit{J s}. Let I be the moment of inertia, \mu the reduced mass, c the speed of light, J the rotational quantum number, \nu the frequency of the J → J − 1 transition, and \Delta \nu the frequency separation between adjacent rotational lines to be reported as the final answer. Let \hbar denote the reduced Planck constant, defined as h divided by 2\pi. The rotational constant B is defined by B = \hbar^2/(2 I h c).

Consider the homonuclear diatomic molecule {}^{14}N_{2}. Each nitrogen nucleus has nuclear spin I=1, so the two nuclei are identical bosons and the total molecular wave function must be symmetric under exchange of the nuclei. Let J denote the rotational quantum number. Under exchange of the nuclei, the rotational wave function acquires the parity factor P_J. Define P_J by the equation below, and note that even J corresponds to a symmetric rotational state while odd J corresponds to an antisymmetric rotational state. For two identical nuclei of spin I, let g_S and g_A denote, respectively, the numbers of nuclear spin states that are symmetric and antisymmetric under exchange. For rotational Raman scattering in a homonuclear diatomic, the selection rule is stated below, and the nuclear spin symmetry does not change during the transition. Define I_even and I_odd as the intensities of spectral lines arising from even-J and odd-J rotational manifolds, respectively. Define the ratio of intensities of adjacent rotational lines as R_int, where R_int=I_even/I_odd. Report the final answer as R_int

Estimate the lowest neutron kinetic energy at which a free neutron, in an inelastic collision with a molecule of gaseous oxygen (O2), can lose energy by exciting molecular rotation. Use the rigid-rotor model with the following definitions and data: - Let r denote the O–O bond length. - Let m denote the mass of a single oxygen atom and let μ = m/2 be the reduced mass of the molecule. - Let I = μ r^2 be the moment of inertia of the molecule about its center of mass. - The rotational energy eigenvalues are E_J = h^2/(8π^2 I) J(J+1) with J = 0, 1, 2, .... - Let E_min be the minimum rotational excitation energy required for the transition J = 0 → 1. - Let E_{n,min} be the minimum neutron kinetic energy in the lab frame; neglect molecular recoil so that E_{n,min} = E_min to lowest order. Use the numerical data r = 1.2 \unit{Å}, m ≈ 16 u with u the atomic mass unit and u c^2 = 931.5 \unit{MeV}, and \hbar c = 197.327 \unit{eV nm}. Express the final numerical answer for E_{n,min} in \unit{eV}.

A {}^{14}N nucleus has nuclear spin I=1. Consider a diatomic nitrogen molecule N_{2} that can rotate but does not vibrate at ordinary temperatures, and ignore electronic motion. Ortho denotes the symmetric nuclear-spin state, and para denotes the antisymmetric nuclear-spin state. Let N_{ortho} and N_{para} denote the equilibrium populations of ortho- and para-N_{2}, respectively. Define the relative abundance ratio R_{p/o}=N_{para}/N_{ortho}. To present the final abundance as an a:b form, define A_{p:o} to denote the para:ortho abundance in a:b notation. The rotational quantum number is J, the moment of inertia is H, the reduced Planck constant is \hbar, the Boltzmann constant is k, and the absolute temperature is T. The rotational energies are E_J and the rigid-rotor approximation is used. Define Z_{even} and Z_{odd} as the rotational partition-function contributions from even-J and odd-J rotational levels, respectively. In the approximations below, introduce continuous variables x and y to replace discrete indices when sums are approximated by integrals. Determine R_{p/o} and A_{p:o} at ordinary temperatures, and describe the behavior of R_{p/o} as T is lowered towards 0 \unit{K}

Consider a deformed nucleus modeled as a uniformly charged, axially symmetric ellipsoid with semimajor axis a and semiminor axis b, where the long axis is 10\% longer than the short axis. Let the total nuclear charge be Q. Let the nuclear radius scale as R = r_0 A^{1/3} with r_0 = \frac{6}{5}\unit{fm}, and choose a representative mass number A \approx 125. Let the first Bohr radius be a_0 = 0.53 \times 10^{-8}\unit{cm}. Estimate the relative error in the electric potential V at distance r = a_0 if the nucleus is treated as a point charge. You may set a = (1 + \varepsilon) R and b = (1 - \varepsilon/2) R with small deformation parameter \varepsilon, and use the quadrupole correction V_q = M Q / r^3 with M = \frac{2}{5}(a^2 - b^2). Provide your final answer as the relative error \Delta V = V_q / V_p.

A nucleus with nuclear g-factor g and mass m_N undergoes Larmor precession in the Earth's magnetic field B. In Gaussian-cgs units, the precession angular frequency is given by omega = g e B / (2 m_N c), where e is the elementary charge and c is the speed of light. Use the order-of-magnitude values g = 1, e = 4.8 × 10^{-10} esu, c = 3 × 10^{10} cm/s, B ≈ 0.5 Gs, and m_N ≈ 10^{-23} g for a light nucleus. Estimate omega and select the closest option among 10^{-1}, 10^{1}, 10^{3}, 10^{5} s^{-1}. State the final answer as omega in s^{-1}.

The nucleus ^{27}_{14}Si decays to its mirror nucleus ^{27}_{13}Al by positron emission. The decay is ^{27}_{14}Si -> ^{27}_{13}Al + beta^+ + nu, where beta^+ denotes the positron and nu denotes the neutrino. The maximum total positron energy E_{pos,max}, defined as kinetic energy plus m_e c^2, is 3.48 MeV. Neglect the recoil energy of the daughter nucleus. Assume that the mass-energy difference between the initial and final nuclei is entirely due to the Coulomb energy. Model each nucleus as a uniformly charged sphere with total charge Z e and radius R, where Z is the atomic number and e is the elementary charge. Assume the nuclear radius satisfies R = r_0 A^{1/3}, where A is the mass number and r_0 is a constant to be estimated. Let m_e be the electron mass, c the speed of light, and hbar the reduced Planck constant. Define E_e as the Coulomb energy difference between the two nuclei, computed from the uniform-sphere model. For numerical evaluation, use e^2 = hbar c / 137 and hbar c = 197 \unit{MeV fm}.

Given the binding energies B(^{90}Zr)=783.916\unit{MeV} and B(^{90}Y)=782.410\unit{MeV}, estimate the excitation energy E_{exc} of the lowest T'=6 isospin state in ^{90}Zr. Use the Coulomb displacement energy between isobaric analog states and the neutron–proton mass difference. Take A=90 for both nuclei, Z=39 for ^{90}Y, and the nuclear radius R=1.2 A^{1/3}\unit{fm}. Constants and symbols: e is the elementary charge, \alpha is the fine-structure constant, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, c is the speed of light, m_n and m_p are the neutron and proton masses, and B denotes nuclear binding energy.

Both nuclei ^{14}_7 N and ^{12}_6 C have ground-state isospin T=0. The lowest T=1 state has an excitation energy E_exc(^{14}N)=2.3 \unit{MeV} in ^{14}_7 N and E_exc(^{12}C)\approx 15.0 \unit{MeV} in ^{12}_6 C. Explain why there is such a marked difference. Also indicate the basis on which an isospin value T is ascribed to such nuclear states by considering the other members of the T=1 isospin triplets and explaining their relationships via systematic nuclear properties. Definitions and conventions used below (all symbols appear in the solution): - T is the total isospin and T_3 is its third component with T_3=(Z-N)/2 for a nucleus with proton number Z and neutron number N - For a fixed mass number A, an isospin T=1 triplet has members with T_3=+1, 0, -1, corresponding to three isobars differing by one unit in Z - M(Z,A) denotes the nuclear mass of the nuclide with proton number Z and mass number A - The mass excess is \Delta=M-A, tabulated in \unit{MeV/c^2} for the nuclides used below - The excitation energy of the lowest T=1 state in nucleus X is defined as E_exc(X)=[M(X,T=1)-M(X,T=0)] c^2 and should be reported in \unit{MeV} - The Coulomb displacement energy between neighboring isobars is defined as \Delta E=[M(Z,A)-M(Z-1,A)] c^2 and is estimated in \unit{MeV} - The nuclear radius is R=R_0 A^{1/3}, with R_0 the radius parameter - e is the elementary charge, h is Planck’s constant, c is the speed of light, m_n and m_p are the neutron and proton masses, and \alpha=\frac{e^2}{h c}=\frac{1}{137} - h c=197 \unit{MeV fm} - J^p denotes the spin-parity quantum numbers of the state - An asterisk, as in ^{A}Z^*, denotes an excited isobaric analog state of the same A Final answers should be written as E_exc(^{14}N) and E_exc(^{12}C), in \unit{MeV}. The following mass-excess values \Delta=M-A (in \unit{MeV/c^2}) are used:
Nuclide\Delta = M - A (MeV/c^2)
^{12}_5 B13.370
^{14}_7 N2.864
^{12}_6 C3.020

Hard sphere scattering. Consider classical elastic scattering of point particles from an infinitely massive, fixed sphere of radius R. Particles approach along the -z direction. Let theta be the polar angle of the surface point of contact (measured from the +z axis). Let alpha be the scattering (deflection) angle between the incident and emergent directions. Let b be the impact parameter, defined as the perpendicular distance from the incident trajectory to the sphere's center. Let dσ/dΩ denote the differential scattering cross section, with dσ = 2 pi b db the area element in impact-parameter space and dΩ = 2 pi sin alpha d alpha the azimuthally averaged solid-angle element. Show that dσ/dΩ is independent of alpha, i.e., the scattering is isotropic.

Neutrons with kinetic energy E = 1000 eV are incident on a carbon target. The inelastic scattering cross section is σ_in = 400 × 10^{-24} cm^2. At this energy, assume only s-wave scattering contributes. Define the s-wave phase shift δ and the s-wave S-matrix element S = e^{2 i δ}. Let m_n be the neutron mass, m_c the carbon nucleus mass, and μ = m_n m_c / (m_c + m_n) the reduced mass. Let k be the center-of-mass wave number and λ = 1/k the corresponding reduced wavelength. Use ħ for the reduced Planck constant and c for the speed of light. Take m_c ≈ 12 m_n, ħ c = 197 MeV fm, and m_n c^2 = 940 MeV. Find upper and lower limits on the elastic scattering cross section σ_e.

Draw the qualitative curve of mean binding energy per nucleon as a function of nuclear mass number. Use values in \unit{MeV} as accurately as possible, and indicate the location of the maximum. From the form of this curve, explain why nuclear fission of a heavy nucleus releases energy and estimate the energy released per fission of uranium-235. Finally, state which force is principally responsible for the behavior of the curve in the upper mass region. Definitions and notation: Let A be the nuclear mass number, B the total nuclear binding energy, and b=B/A the mean binding energy per nucleon (measured in \unit{MeV/nucleon}). Let A_{max} denote the value of A at which b is maximal along the valley of stability. For the fission estimate, let A_U=235 for ^{235}U, let b_{parent} be the mean binding energy per nucleon of ^{235}U, and let b_{prod} be the mean binding energy per nucleon of the typical fission products (take two fragments near A\approx 117 as a representative case). Let E_{release} be the energy released per fission event, to be reported as E_{release}=\dots\unit{MeV}. The final answers should be written in the forms A_{max}=\dots and E_{release}=\dots\unit{MeV}.

Figure 2.5 shows a plot of the average binding energy per nucleon as a function of the mass number A. Let epsilon(A) denote the average binding energy per nucleon for a nucleus with mass number A, so E is defined by the relation
$$
E = \varepsilon(A)
$$
Consider the fission of a parent nucleus characterized by mass number A_0, proton number Z_0, mass M_0, and total binding energy B_0 into two fragments characterized by mass numbers A_1 and A_2, proton numbers Z_1 and Z_2, masses M_1 and M_2, and total binding energies B_1 and B_2, respectively. Let c denote the speed of light, m_p the proton mass, and m_n the neutron mass. The energy released Q is defined by
$$
Q = M_0 c^2 - M_1 c^2 - M_2 c^2
$$
Express Q in terms of \varepsilon(A) and A. Then estimate Q for symmetric fission with A_0 = 240, where symmetric means A_1 = A_2 = 120. Use values read from Figure 2.5: \varepsilon(120) \approx 8.5 \unit{MeV} and \varepsilon(240) \approx 7.6 \unit{MeV}.

Assume a uranium nucleus breaks up spontaneously into two roughly equal parts. Estimate the reduction in electrostatic energy of the nuclei, and state how this relates to the total change in energy. Use the uniform charge distribution model with nuclear radius R(A)=1.2×10^{-13} A^{1/3} cm. Define Z_0=92, A_0=236 for the initial uranium nucleus, with initial radius R_0=R(A_0). After fission into two equal fragments, each fragment has Z=Z_0/2 and A=A_0/2, with radius R=R(A). Let Q=Ze be the charge of a fragment, where e is the magnitude of the elementary charge. Use Gaussian units for electrostatics so that the electrostatic self-energy of a uniformly charged sphere is E_self=(3/5) Q^2/R. For numerical evaluation, take the fine-structure constant α=e^2/(ħ c)≈1/137 and ħ c≈1.97×10^{-11} MeV cm. Let ΔE denote the reduction in electrostatic energy, defined as initial minus final.

Estimate (order of magnitude) the ratio of the energy released when 1\unit{g} of uranium undergoes fission to the energy released when 1\unit{g} of TNT explodes. Define E_TNT as the energy released by the explosion of 1\unit{g} of TNT, E_fiss as the energy released by the fission of 1\unit{g} of uranium, and let the final answer be reported as r=E_fiss/E_TNT. Use U-238 with mass number A=238 for the uranium sample. Let N_A be Avogadro's number and epsilon_fiss be the energy released per fission event of a uranium nucleus. Let N_U denote the number of uranium nuclei in 1\unit{g} of U-238. Energies may be expressed in \unit{eV} or \unit{MeV}, written with \unit{} units.
Let t_{1/2} denote the half-life of uranium-235 (U-235), measured in years. Choose the closest value for t_{1/2} from the following options: 10^3 years, 10^6 years, 10^9 years, 10^12 years.

A nuclear reactor operates at constant thermal power P=100\unit{MW}. Let N denote the number of fission events per second in the core, and let E_f be the energy released per fission. Assume E_f=200\unit{MeV}.

Select the correct order of magnitude for N from the set {10^6, 10^{12}, 10^{18}, 10^{24}, 10^{30}}.

Discuss thermonuclear reactions. Provide concrete examples of reactions important in the Sun, in the H bomb (uncontrolled thermonuclear reaction), and in controlled fusion attempts. Estimate, in electron volts per reaction, the energy release for each example. Also describe the most important characteristic of the nuclear force relevant to these reactions. Notation and definitions to be used in the solution: p denotes a proton, d denotes a deuteron (2H), t denotes a triton (3H), n denotes a neutron, 3He and 4He denote helium-3 and helium-4, respectively, 6Li denotes lithium-6, e^{+} denotes a positron, ν_e denotes an electron neutrino, and γ denotes a gamma ray. The quantity Q denotes the energy released per reaction. M(X) denotes the atomic mass of nuclide X, and c denotes the speed of light. Energies are expressed in eV (specifically MeV) and masses in amu, with units written using \unit{}.

A deuteron of rest mass M and binding energy B is disintegrated into a neutron of rest mass m_n and a proton of rest mass m_p by a gamma ray of energy E_{\gamma}. Let c denote the speed of light. Assume B \ll M c^2. The binding energy satisfies B = ( m_n + m_p - M ) c^2. Define \Delta = E_{\gamma} - B and let \Delta_{min} be the minimum value of \Delta for which the reaction can occur, i.e., the threshold at which the final nucleons are at rest in their center-of-mass frame. Find \Delta_{min} to lowest order in B / ( M c^2 ).

Consider two nucleons with position vectors x_1 and x_2 in ordinary space, intrinsic spin operators J^(1) and J^(2), and isospin operators I^(1) and I^(2). Neglect all velocity- or momentum-dependent terms. Define the relative coordinate x = x_1 - x_2 and r = |x|. The two-body potential is V(x_1, x_2, J^(1), J^(2), I^(1), I^(2)). Derive the most general form of V that is consistent with the following symmetries and conservation laws: spatial translations, spatial rotations, spatial parity, isospin rotations, hermiticity (probability conservation), and time reversal. For each symmetry, state the transformation law and the corresponding restriction on V. Use the spatial rotation operator R, the isospin rotation operator R_I, and the time-reversal operator U. Treat x as a polar vector and J^(i) and I^(i) as axial vectors in their respective spaces. Use the Kronecker delta δ_ij and the Levi-Civita symbol ε_ijk as needed. Conclude with the final structured form of V in terms of rotational and isospin invariants and scalar coefficient functions of r. The final answer should be written as V(x_1, x_2, J^(1), J^(2), I^(1), I^(2)) equals a sum of terms built from the allowed invariants

To some approximation, a medium-weight nucleus can be regarded as a flat-bottomed potential with rigid walls. To simplify this picture still further, model the nucleus as a cubical box of side length a equal to the nuclear diameter. Consider a nucleus of iron-56, which has 28 protons and 28 neutrons. Assume the nonrelativistic single-particle Schrödinger equation in a three-dimensional infinite square well, with zero potential inside the box and infinite potential outside, and neglect interactions beyond the Pauli principle. Use a nuclear diameter a=10^{-12}\unit{cm}. Let m be the nucleon mass, \hbar the reduced Planck constant, and c the speed of light. Let E denote the single-particle total energy eigenvalue, which equals the kinetic energy inside the box because V=0 there. Let E_{\max} denote the kinetic energy of the highest-energy (most energetic) nucleon in the filled ground-state configuration of the nucleus. Estimate E_{\max}.

A recent model for collective nuclear states treats them in terms of interacting bosons. Consider states that are fully symmetric superpositions of S bosons and D bosons. Each S boson has spin 0, and each D boson has spin 2. Let N_s denote the number of S bosons and N_d denote the number of D bosons. Let E_s be the single-boson energy of each S boson and E_d be the single-boson energy of each D boson. There is a residual interaction of constant strength alpha between each distinct pair of D bosons, contributing alpha per D–D pair. Let J denote the total spin of a state, and let J_{N_d,k} denote the k-th allowed value of J for a given N_d, ordered from smallest to largest. Determine the allowed total spins for N_d = 0, 1, 2, 3. Then, for all configurations with fixed total boson number N_s + N_d = 3, determine the total energy E of each configuration.

A simplified model of the complex nuclear interaction is the pairing force in a single j orbit acting in the two-identical-particle space. The two-particle basis states are defined by
$$
\phi_m = (-1)^{j-m} | j m \rangle | j - m \rangle
$$
for the magnetic projections
$$
m = \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{3}{2}, \dots, j
$$
In this basis the pairing Hamiltonian is
$$
H = - g M
$$
where g > 0 is the pairing strength and M is the matrix with all entries equal to 1. If the basis has dimension n, then
$$
M_{ij} = 1
$$
for i,j = 1,\dots,n, and for a single j orbit the dimension is
$$
n = j + \tfrac{1}{2}
$$
Here H is the two-particle Hamiltonian, j is the single-particle total angular momentum, m is its projection, J is the total spin of a two-particle state, and E denotes an energy eigenvalue. Let \(\Psi^{(N=2)}\) denote a normalized two-particle eigenstate. This interaction has a single outstanding eigenstate. Determine its spin and energy, to be reported as
$$
J_{\mathrm{special}}
$$
$$
E_{\mathrm{special}}
$$
Determine also the spins and energies of the remaining two-particle eigenstates, to be reported as
$$
\{J_{\mathrm{rest}}\}
$$
$$
E_{\mathrm{rest}}
$$

Natural gold $^{197}_{79}Au$ is unstable against $\alpha$-decay with $\alpha$-particle kinetic energy $E_{\alpha}=3.3\unit{MeV}$. Use the Geiger–Nuttall law to estimate the half-life $T_{1/2}$ of $^{197}_{79}Au$ and explain why ordinary gold does not noticeably heat itself. Let $\lambda$ be the decay constant in $\unit{1/s}$, $T_{1/2}$ the half-life in $\unit{s}$, $E_{\alpha}$ the $\alpha$-particle energy in $\unit{MeV}$, $Z$ the atomic number, and $C,D$ empirical constants that depend on $Z$. For a rough estimate, use $C\approx52$ and $D\approx140\unit{MeV^{1/2}}$ (values for lead, $Z=82$).

A sphere of the isotope 239Pu with mass m_Pu = 120.1 g is immersed in liquid nitrogen of sufficient volume to stop all emitted alpha particles. The measured boil-off corresponds to a constant thermal power P = 0.231 W, which equals the energy release rate from radioactive decays. The kinetic energy of each emitted alpha particle is E_alpha = 5.144 MeV. Include the recoil energy of the daughter nucleus 235U in the energy per decay. Assume the decay 239Pu -> 235U + alpha, with mass numbers A_Pu = 239, A_U = 235, and A_alpha = 4. Use the following constants and conversions: 1 MeV = 1.60206 × 10^{-13} J, 1 atomic mass unit u = 1.66 × 10^{-24} g, and 1 year = 3.156 × 10^{7} s. Variables used in the solution: M_alpha and M_U are the masses of the alpha particle and the 235U recoil nucleus; p_alpha and p_U are the magnitudes of their momenta; E_u is the recoil kinetic energy of 235U; E is the total energy deposited per decay in the liquid; E_J is the energy per decay expressed in joules; N is the number of 239Pu nuclei in the sample; dN/dt is the decay rate (activity); lambda is the decay constant; T_{1/2} is the half-life; T_{1/2,round} is the half-life rounded to the nearest hundred years. Approximate nuclear masses by mass numbers where needed, i.e., M_alpha/M_U ≈ A_alpha/A_U.

Both ^{161}Ho and ^{163}Ho decay by allowed electron capture to Dy isotopes. The quantity Q_{EC} is defined as the mass difference between the final ground state of the nucleus plus atomic electrons and the initial ground state of the nucleus plus atomic electrons. The Dy orbital electron binding energies are listed in the table below. The capture rate for 3p_{1/2} electrons in ^{161}Ho is about 5% of the 3s capture rate. Using this information, calculate the 3p_{1/2}-to-3s relative capture rate in ^{163}Ho. Then, assuming Q_{EC} remains the same but the neutrino has a mass m_ u = 50 \unit{eV} instead of being massless, determine how much the 3p_{1/2} and 3s capture rates change for both ^{161}Ho and ^{163}Ho. Use the following definitions and symbols consistently in your work: A is a constant prefactor, M_{if} is the transition matrix element, ho(E) is the density of final states, E_ u is the neutrino energy, p_ u is the neutrino momentum, B_s and B_p are the Dy binding energies of the captured 3s and 3p_{1/2} electrons, respectively, and \lambda is the decay constant for a given capture channel. For the neutrino-mass part, define the fractional change as (\lambda_0 - \lambda)/\lambda_0, where \lambda_0 is the decay constant for a massless neutrino. Report the relative rate in ^{163}Ho as R_{163} = \lambda(3p_{1/2})/\lambda(3s), and report the fractional changes for each isotope and orbital as (\lambda_0 - \lambda)/\lambda_0.
OrbitalBinding Energy (keV)
1s54
2s9
2p1/28.6
3s2.0
3p1/21.8

An element of low atomic number Z can undergo an allowed positron beta decay. Let p_0 be the maximum possible momentum of the positron, with p_0 << m_e c, where m_e is the positron mass and c is the speed of light. Let Gamma_{beta} be the total beta-decay rate. An alternative process is K-capture, in which the nucleus captures a K-shell electron and undergoes the same nuclear transition with emission of a neutrino; let Gamma_K be the decay rate for this process. Treat the wave function of the K-shell electron as hydrogenic and ignore the electron binding energy. Define the total available decay energy (Q-value) as E. Let E_0 be the maximum positron kinetic energy and take E_0 approx E under the given nonrelativistic assumption. Let E_e be the positron kinetic energy and E_{nu} the neutrino energy, and let p and p_{nu} be the corresponding momenta. Neglect nuclear recoil throughout. Use u_i and u_f to denote the initial and final nuclear wave functions, respectively. Use psi_i and psi_f for the initial and final total wave functions. The transition is treated in Fermi theory with an interaction Hamiltonian H of constant strength g. The positron and neutrino one-particle wave functions are denoted phi_{beta} and phi_{nu}, respectively, and are taken as plane waves normalized in a volume V. Their wave vectors are k_{beta} and k_{nu}. The spatial coordinate is r and the spatial volume element is d tau. Define the nuclear matrix element M_{fi} by the relation ∫ psi_i u_f^* e^{- i (k_{beta} + k_{nu}) · r} d tau = M_{fi}. Let hbar be the reduced Planck constant and e the elementary charge. The hydrogenic K-shell electron wave function is denoted phi_K. Let d n / d E denote the density of final states per unit energy. Let omega denote the total beta-decay probability per unit time at fixed decay energy E. Define I(p) by omega = ∫ I(p) d p, so that I(p) is the differential spectrum in positron momentum. For convenience define B = g^2 |M_{fi}|^2 / (2 pi^3 hbar^7 c^3). Compute the ratio Gamma_K / Gamma_{beta}.

A nucleus of rest mass M is initially at rest in an excited state whose energy is \Delta E above the ground state of the nucleus. The nucleus emits a gamma-ray photon of energy h 
u and makes a transition to its ground state. Explain why the gamma-ray energy h 
u is not equal to the energy-level difference \Delta E and determine the fractional change f. Assume \Delta E < M c^2. Constants and variables: c is the speed of light, h is Planck's constant, 
u is the photon frequency, E is the total relativistic energy of the recoiling nucleus after emission, p is the magnitude of its recoil momentum, E_\gamma is the photon energy, and p_\gamma is the photon momentum. The fractional change is defined as f = (h 
u - \Delta E)/\Delta E.

A hypothetical particle A has a ground-state rest mass m and an excited state A* whose excitation energy is ΔE, so the excited-state rest mass is m + ΔE/c^2. The particle is initially at rest and can be excited by absorbing a single γ-ray photon. Let c denote the speed of light, and let E_γ be the photon energy required to excite A to A*. Assume that ΔE/c^2 is not small compared to m. For intermediate steps one may use p_γ for the photon momentum and p for the recoil momentum of A after absorption. Find the resonant photon energy E_γ.

A foil of 7Li with mass 0.05 \unit{g} is irradiated with thermal neutrons. The 7Li captures neutrons with microscopic capture cross section \sigma = 37 millibarns, with 1 \unit{b} = 10^{-24} \unit{cm^2} and 1 \unit{mb} = 10^{-3} \unit{b}. This forms 8Li, which undergoes \beta^- decay with half-life T_{1/2} = 0.85 \unit{s}. The foil is exposed to a steady neutron flux \phi = 3 \times 10^{12} \unit{s^{-1} cm^{-2}}. Let N_1(t) be the number of 7Li atoms and N_2(t) be the number of 8Li atoms in the foil at time t. Let \lambda be the 8Li decay constant, related to the half-life by \lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}. Let A be the \beta activity, i.e., the number of \beta decays per second. Use Avogadro's number N_A = 6.023 \times 10^{23} \unit{mol^{-1}}. Assume the foil is thin so the flux is uniform and self-shielding is negligible. Find the equilibrium activity A when the foil is exposed to the given flux.

Let E_{nuc} denote a typical nuclear excitation energy measured in mega-electron-volts. Among the following orders of magnitude, which is most typical for E_{nuc}?
$$10^{-2} \unit{MeV}$$
$$10^{1} \unit{MeV}$$
$$10^{3} \unit{MeV}$$
$$10^{5} \unit{MeV}$$

A detector is coated with a thin layer of naturally fissioning heavy nuclei. The detector area is 2\unit{cm^2}. The mean life of the fissioning isotope is given by 
$$
\tau = \frac{1}{3} \times 10^9 \unit{yr}
$$
with the conversion 
$$
1\unit{yr} = 3 \times 10^7 \unit{s}
$$
In the absence of an external neutron flux, the number of fissions detected per second is 
$$
R_{\text{nat}} = 20 \unit{s^{-1}}
$$
The detector is then placed in a uniform neutron flux 
$$
\phi = 10^{11} \unit{cm^{-2} s^{-1}}
$$
and the total number of fissions detected per second becomes 
$$
R_{\text{tot}} = 120 \unit{s^{-1}}
$$
Define the following variables for the solution: N(t) is the number of heavy nuclei at time t, N_0 = N(0) is the initial number of nuclei, \lambda = 1/\tau is the decay constant, \phi is the neutron flux as given above, and \sigma is the neutron-induced fission cross section to be determined. Assume the induced fission rate is 
$$
R_{\text{ind}} = \sigma N \phi
$$
and the natural fission rate is 
$$
R_{\text{nat}} = \lambda N
$$
Find the cross section \sigma.

Let \sigma denote the total scattering cross-section for low-energy neutron–nucleus scattering, measured in \unit{cm^2}. Let r denote the radius of the sphere of action of nuclear forces, measured in \unit{cm}. Choose the typical value of \sigma from the following options: 10^{-16} \unit{cm^2}, 10^{-24} \unit{cm^2}, 10^{-32} \unit{cm^2}, 10^{-40} \unit{cm^2}.
Let \tau_\mu denote the mean proper lifetime of the muon. Choose the correct order of magnitude (in seconds) for \tau_\mu from the following options: 10^9, 10^2, 10^{-2}, 10^{-6}.

Describe the properties of the three pions π+, π0, and π−, and discuss in detail the experiments that determine their spin J, parity P, and isospin I. Conventions and definitions: Use J_X for the spin of particle X, P(X) for its intrinsic parity, I(X) for its isospin, C(X) for the charge-conjugation parity of neutral states, and G(X) for G-parity, defined as charge conjugation followed by a rotation by π about the second isospin axis. Strong interactions conserve isospin and G-parity. Let σ denote a total cross section and dσ/dΩ a differential cross section. The deuteron, proton, and neutron are denoted by d, p, and n, respectively. In center-of-mass kinematics, p_π and p_p are the magnitudes of the center-of-mass momenta of the pion and proton, respectively; J_p and J_d are the proton and deuteron spins. The principle of detailed balance relates forward and inverse reaction cross sections when the transition matrix element is the same. In the decay π0 → 2γ, let ε1 and ε2 be the polarization vectors of the two real photons and k the photon three-momentum in the π0 rest frame; the two-photon final state is exchange symmetric. The symbol γ denotes a real photon. Let l be the orbital angular momentum of the relative motion of two final-state nucleons and J the total angular momentum. Use the isospin basis |I, I3⟩, where I3 is the third component of isospin. The symbols e^+ and e^- denote the positron and electron, respectively. The term s-wave denotes l = 0. Masses should be quoted in \unit{MeV}. Report the final identified quantum numbers as J_{\pi^+}, J_{\pi^-}, J_{\pi^0}, P(\pi^+), P(\pi^-), P(\pi^0), and I(\pi).

The electrically neutral baryon Σ^0(1915) has mass 1915 \unit{MeV/c^2}, isospin I=1, and third component I_3=0. Let Γ_{K^- p}, Γ_{\bar K^0 n}, Γ_{\pi^- p}, and Γ_{\pi^+ \pi^-} denote the partial decay widths for the decays Σ^0(1915) → K^- p, Σ^0(1915) → \bar K^0 n, Σ^0(1915) → \pi^- p, and Σ^0(1915) → \pi^+ \pi^-, respectively. Assume that the masses of the nucleons, K^-, and \pi^- are such that all these decays are kinematically allowed, and disregard the small mass splittings within any isospin multiplet. Use the isospin convention that N=(p,n) is an isospin doublet, (\pi^+,\pi^0,\pi^-) is an isospin triplet, K=(K^+,K^0) is an isospin doublet, and \bar K=(K^-,\bar K^0) is the corresponding antiparticle doublet. The strong-interaction Hamiltonian H is taken to be isospin invariant. States are denoted by |I,I_3\rangle. The change in the third component of isospin between initial and final states is denoted by ΔI_3. The two-particle symbol Ψ(XY) denotes the isospin product state of particles X and Y, and Ψ(Σ^0) denotes the isospin state of the initial Σ^0(1915). Define the target ratios below and determine their values

Consider the hyperon nonleptonic weak decays. Assume these ΔS=1 weak decays satisfy the ΔI=1/2 rule. Use Clebsch–Gordan tables to find the values of x, y, z as defined below. Here A denotes the transition amplitude, I is the total isospin, I_3 is its third component, and H_w is the weak Hamiltonian for these nonleptonic transitions. To implement the ΔI=1/2 rule in the isospin-coupling method used in the solution, introduce an auxiliary isodoublet a with isospin I_a=1/2 and third component I_{3,a}=-1/2.

$$\Lambda^0 \to p\,\pi^-$$

$$\Lambda^0 \to n\,\pi^0$$

$$\Sigma^- \to n\,\pi^-$$

$$\Sigma^+ \to p\,\pi^0$$

$$\Sigma^+ \to n\,\pi^+$$

$$\Xi^- \to \Lambda^0\,\pi^-$$

$$\Xi^0 \to \Lambda^0\,\pi^0$$

Define the ratios x, y, z by

$$x=\frac{A(\Lambda^0\to p\,\pi^-)}{A(\Lambda^0\to n\,\pi^0)}$$

$$y=\frac{A(\Sigma^+\to \pi^+ n)-A(\Sigma^-\to \pi^- n)}{A(\Sigma^+\to \pi^0 p)}$$

$$z=\frac{A(\Xi^0\to \Lambda^0\,\pi^0)}{A(\Xi^-\to \Lambda^0\,\pi^-) }$$

An experiment in a gold mine in South Dakota has been carried out to detect solar neutrinos using the reaction ν + Cl^{37} → Ar^{37} + e^{-}. The detector contains approximately 4 × 10^5 liters of tetrachloroethylene (CCl4). Estimate how many atoms of Ar^{37} would be produced per day. List your assumptions. How can you improve the experiment?

Definitions and symbols:
- ν is a solar neutrino
- Cl^{37} and Ar^{37} are chlorine-37 and argon-37 nuclei, respectively
- e^{-} is the electron
- M_{Ar} and M_{Cl} are the atomic masses of Ar^{37} and Cl^{37}
- c is the speed of light
- E_{th} is the threshold neutrino energy for the reaction ν + Cl^{37} → Ar^{37} + e^{-}
- E_{ν} is the neutrino energy
- ρ is the density of CCl4
- A is the molecular weight of CCl4
- N_0 is Avogadro's number
- n is the number density of Cl nuclei in CCl4
- V is the detector volume
- \bar{σ} is the average interaction cross section per Cl nucleus
- F is the solar neutrino flux above threshold
- t is the exposure time
- N_{ν} is the expected number of detected neutrino interactions per day (equal to the number of Ar^{37} atoms produced per day)
- N_{Ar,day} is the number of Ar^{37} atoms produced per day

Neglect CP violation. Work in natural units with \hbar=1 and c=1, so m_L and m_S denote the rest energies that enter the time evolution phases. The flavor states |K^0> and |\bar K^0> are related to the long- and short-lived eigenstates |K_L> and |K_S> by
$$
|K^0>=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|K_L>+|K_S>)
$$
$$
|\bar K^0>=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|K_L>-|K_S>)
$$
The lifetimes are defined by
$$
\tau_L=\frac{1}{\gamma_L}
$$
$$
\tau_S=\frac{1}{\gamma_S}
$$
with distinct rest energies
$$
m_L\neq m_S
$$
At time t=0 a meson is produced in the state
$$
|\psi(0)>=|K^0>
$$
Let H be the effective Hamiltonian that is diagonal in the basis \{|K_L>,|K_S>\} and generates time evolution. The mass eigenstates evolve as
$$
|K_L(t)>=e^{-i m_L t} e^{-\gamma_L t/2} |K_L>
$$
$$
|K_S(t)>=e^{-i m_S t} e^{-\gamma_S t/2} |K_S>
$$
Define the time-evolved state
$$
|\Psi(t)>=e^{-i H t}|\psi(0)>
$$
Let P_0(t) be the probability to find the system in the state |K^0> at time t and let \bar P_0(t) be the probability to find the system in the state |\bar K^0> at time t. Find P_0(t)-\bar P_0(t) in terms of \gamma_L, \gamma_S, m_L, and m_S

In e^+ e^- annihilation experiments, a narrow resonance (with width smaller than the intrinsic energy spread of the two beams) has been observed at the center-of-mass energy E_{CM} = 9.5 \unit{GeV} for both channels. The reactions are
$$
e^+ e^- \to \mu^+ \mu^-
$$
$$
e^+ e^- \to \text{hadrons}
$$
Let E denote the center-of-mass energy variable that appears in the cross sections \sigma_{\mu\mu}(E) and \sigma_h(E). Let M be the mass of the resonance and J its spin; for this resonance we take
$$
M = E_{CM}
$$
$$
E_{CM} = 9.5 \unit{GeV}
$$
$$
J = 1
$$
Let \Gamma be the total width, and \Gamma_{ee}, \Gamma_{\mu\mu}, \Gamma_h, \Gamma_{\tau\tau} be the partial widths for decays into e^+ e^-, \mu^+ \mu^-, hadrons, and \tau^+ \tau^- respectively. Assume lepton universality and neglect small phase-space differences so that
$$
\Gamma_{ee} = \Gamma_{\mu\mu}
$$
$$
\Gamma_{\mu\mu} = \Gamma_{\tau\tau}
$$
The experimentally measured integrated cross sections are
$$
\int \sigma_{\mu\mu}(E) dE = 8.5\times 10^{-33} \unit{cm^2 MeV}
$$
$$
\int \sigma_h(E) dE = 3.3\times 10^{-31} \unit{cm^2 MeV}
$$
Use the Breit–Wigner resonance formula to determine the partial widths \Gamma_{\mu\mu} and \Gamma_h for the \mu\mu and hadronic decays of the resonance. Report the final answers for \Gamma_{\mu\mu} and \Gamma_h in \unit{MeV}. You may use the conversion constant
$$
\hbar c = 197\times 10^{-13} \unit{MeV\,cm}
$$

The Princeton synchrotron (PPA) has recently been used to accelerate highly charged nitrogen ions. The ring has fixed bending radius rho and a maximum magnetic field B_m. For a particle of charge magnitude |q| moving in the same ring, the magnetic rigidity limit gives the maximum attainable momentum p_m through p_m = |q| rho B_m. The PPA can produce protons of nominal total energy E_p = 3\unit{GeV}. Use the proton rest energy m_p c^2 = 0.938\unit{GeV}. Work in natural units with c = 1 for energy–momentum relations, and report momenta in \unit{GeV/c} and energies in \unit{GeV}. A fully stripped nitrogen-14 ion has charge magnitude |q|_N = 6 e, where e is the elementary charge, and rest mass m_N = 14 m_p. Define p_p as the proton momentum at the magnetic limit, p_N as the nitrogen momentum at the same limit, E_N as the nitrogen total energy at that limit, and T_max as the maximum kinetic energy of the ^{14}N^{6+} ions. Find T_max.

A muon has rest mass m such that m c^2 ≈ 100 \unit{MeV} and proper lifetime at rest \tau_0 ≈ 2 \unit{\mu s}. Assume the Earth’s magnetic field could, in principle, bend the muon into a circular path around the Earth of radius R ≈ 6.4\times 10^6 \unit{m}. Use the speed of light c ≈ 3\times 10^8 \unit{m/s}, the elementary charge e ≈ 1.6\times 10^{-19} \unit{C}, and the conversion 1 \unit{MeV} = 1.6\times 10^{-13} \unit{J}. Also use 1 \unit{G} = 10^{-4} \unit{T}. Let v be the muon speed, define \beta = v/c and \gamma = (1 - \beta^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}. Let p denote the relativistic momentum magnitude, E the total energy, and B the magnetic flux density. Define E_{min} as the minimum total energy such that the time-dilated lifetime is at least the travel time for one circumnavigation, p_{min} as the corresponding minimum momentum, and B_{min} as the minimum magnetic field needed to keep the muon in a circular orbit of radius R. A "fair chance" means the time-dilated lifetime \gamma \tau_0 is at least the travel time 2\pi R/v. Compute E_{min} and B_{min}, and decide whether the Earth’s surface field (about 0.25 to 0.65 \unit{G}) is sufficient to keep the muon in orbit.

In a high-energy collision analyzed in natural units where c=1, an incident proton collides with a proton at rest to produce, in addition to the two protons, a new particle of rest mass M. Let m_p denote the proton rest mass. In the fixed-target case, let E_p and p_p denote the total energy and the magnitude of the three-momentum of the incident (moving) proton in the laboratory frame. Let S denote the invariant mass squared of the initial two-proton system. At threshold (minimum incident energy for the process to occur), all final-state particles are produced at rest in the center-of-momentum frame. Find the minimum E_p required in the fixed-target case. Then consider a collider configuration in which two protons approach one another with equal speeds, each with energy E_p and momentum magnitude p_p, and find the corresponding threshold energy per proton.

Determine the threshold kinetic energy T_p of a proton beam needed to produce the reaction p + p -> p + p + \pi^0 on a stationary proton target in the laboratory frame. Use natural units with c=1 so that rest masses are expressed as energies. Let m_p denote the proton rest energy and m_\pi the neutral pion rest energy. Use m_p = 938 \unit{MeV} and m_\pi = 135 \unit{MeV}. Let E_p denote the total energy of the incident proton at threshold.

Determine the threshold energy E_{\gamma,\mathrm{th}} for a gamma ray to create an electron–positron pair in an interaction with an electron at rest. Consider the reaction gamma + e^- -> e^- + e^+ + e^-. Use natural units with c=1, so energies and masses are measured in MeV. Let m_e denote the electron rest mass energy, E_\gamma the incident gamma-ray energy, p_\gamma its momentum magnitude, and S the invariant mass squared of the system. Use m_e=0.511 MeV.

Consider a beam of negative pions impinging on a fixed proton target at rest in the laboratory frame. Determine the threshold pion energy required for K^- production. Use natural units with c=1 so that energies, masses, and momenta are all in GeV. Define the following quantities: m_p for the proton mass, m_n for the neutron mass, m_K for the kaon mass, m_pi for the pion mass, E_pi for the pion energy at threshold in the lab frame, p_pi for the corresponding pion momentum in the lab frame, and S for the invariant mass squared of the total four-momentum, defined by S=(ΣE)^2-(Σp)^2, where ΣE is the total energy and Σp is the magnitude of the total three-momentum. The third component of isospin is denoted by I_z, and strangeness is conserved. The final answer should be reported as E_pi.

A particle of rest mass m has kinetic energy T' equal to twice its rest energy. It collides with a particle of equal rest mass m that is at rest. The two combine into a single new particle of rest mass M. Use natural units with c=1 so that the rest energy of a particle of rest mass m equals m. Let E denote the total energy of the incident particle, p its momentum magnitude, and S the invariant mass squared of the two-particle system. Compute M in terms of m.

In the CERN colliding-beam storage ring, two protons, each of total energy E_b = 30\unit{GeV}, collide head-on. Consider instead a fixed-target collision in which a proton of laboratory total energy E and momentum magnitude P strikes a stationary proton. Let m_p denote the proton rest mass energy and let s denote the invariant mass squared of the two-proton system. Find E such that the fixed-target collision has the same s as the collider case. Use m_p \approx 0.938\unit{GeV}. Report the required E in \unit{GeV}.

Calculate the fractional change in the kinetic energy of an \alpha-particle when it is scattered through 180^\circ by an O^{16} nucleus initially at rest. Assume nonrelativistic, elastic scattering. Use E for the initial kinetic energy of the \alpha-particle, p for its initial momentum magnitude, m_{\alpha} for its mass, E' and p' for the kinetic energy and momentum magnitude after scattering, M for the mass of the O^{16} nucleus, and P_0 for the magnitude of the recoil momentum of the O^{16} nucleus. Take M \approx 4 m_{\alpha}. Define the fractional change to be \chi = (E' - E)/E.

A beam of positively charged pions pi^+ with laboratory-frame kinetic energy T produces some positively charged muons mu^+ that travel backward in the laboratory frame. The mu^+ are produced via the two-body decay reaction pi^+ -> mu^+ + nu, where nu is a (massless) neutrino. Use natural units with c=1 so that all masses are expressed in energy units. Let T be the laboratory-frame kinetic energy T_pi of the incident pion, and let E_pi be its total laboratory-frame energy. Denote quantities evaluated in the center-of-mass frame of the decay (which is the rest frame of the parent pion) by an overbar. Let beta denote speed in units of c, so beta_pi is the laboratory-frame speed of the pion and bar beta_mu is the center-of-mass speed of the muon.
