The rest energies are given by the following numerical values: $$m_{\pi} c^2 = 139.57 \unit{MeV}$$ $$m_{\mu} c^2 = 105.66 \unit{MeV}$$ $$m_{\nu} c^2 = 0.0 \unit{MeV}$$ For what range of T is backward emission of some mu^+ in the laboratory frame possible?

Reaction on a stationary proton target in the laboratory frame: K^- + p -> π^0 + Λ^0. The proton target is at rest initially. Use natural units with c = 1 so that energies, momenta, and masses share the same units. Let m_{π^0}, m_{K^-}, m_p, and m_{Λ^0} denote the rest masses of the π^0, K^-, proton, and Λ^0, respectively. Let E_K be the total energy of the incident K^- in the lab frame, and p_K its momentum magnitude. Let E_π and p_π be the total energy and momentum magnitude of the outgoing π^0 in the lab frame. The Λ^0 is produced at rest in the lab, so its momentum magnitude is p_{Λ^0} = 0. Find the special value of E_K such that Λ^0 is produced at rest, and express your answer in terms of m_{π^0}, m_{K^-}, m_p, and m_{Λ^0}.

The Q-value (energy released) of the reaction He-3(n,p) that produces tritium is given as Q=0.770 MeV. The maximum kinetic energy of the beta particle (electron) emitted in tritium beta decay is E_max=0.018 MeV. Using the conversion 1 amu = 931 MeV, calculate the mass difference between a neutron and a hydrogen atom (protium), defined as M(n) - M(^1H), and report it in amu. Definitions: M(X) denotes the atomic mass-energy equivalent of species X expressed in MeV via mc^2, n is the neutron, p is the proton, ^1H is the hydrogen atom (protium), Q is the Q-value of the reaction ^3He(n,p)^3H, and E_max is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron in the decay ^3H -> ^3He + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e.

An X-ray photon with initial frequency nu_0 collides with an electron at rest and is scattered through an angle theta. The electron Compton wavelength is lambda_c. Use c for the speed of light, h for Planck's constant, m for the electron rest mass, lambda_0 for the incident wavelength, lambda' for the scattered wavelength, and nu' for the scattered frequency. Given nu_0=3×10^{19} Hz, theta=90°, lambda_c=2.4×10^{-12} m, and c=3×10^8 m/s. Find nu'.

Consider Compton scattering of photons that collide head-on with moving electrons, and observe back-scattered photons at scattering angle θ=180°. Let the incident photon energy be E_γ=hν=2\unit{eV}, where h is Planck's constant and ν is the photon frequency. The incident electron has kinetic energy T_e=1\unit{GeV}. Work in natural units with c=1, so the photon energy equals its momentum magnitude. Define the electron rest mass energy m_e=0.511\unit{MeV}. Denote the total energy and momentum magnitude of the incident electron by E_e and p_e, and those of the incident photon by E_γ and p_γ. After the collision, denote the corresponding quantities by E_e', p_e', E_γ', p_γ'. For photons E_γ=p_γ and E_γ'=p_γ'. Find the energy of the back-scattered photons, E_γ'.

A negative Xi^- particle decays into a Lambda^0 and a pi^- as follows: $$\Xi^- \to \Lambda^0 + \pi^-$$ The Xi^- moves in the laboratory frame along the positive x direction with momentum defined as $$p_\Xi=2\unit{GeV/c}$$ In the Xi^- rest frame (denoted by an overbar on any quantity), the Lambda^0 is emitted at a polar angle of 30^\circ relative to the initial Xi^- direction. Using natural units with c=1, so that energies and rest masses are given in GeV and momenta in GeV/c, find the laboratory-frame momenta and angles of the Lambda^0 and the pi^- with respect to the positive x axis after the decay. Use the following rest energies: $$M_\Xi c^2=1.3\unit{GeV}$$ $$M_\Lambda c^2=1.1\unit{GeV}$$ $$M_\pi c^2=0.14\unit{GeV}$$ Definitions and notation to be used in the solution: m_\Xi, m_\Lambda, and m_\pi denote the rest masses expressed in GeV via m=M c^2. E denotes total energy in GeV and p denotes the magnitude of the three-momentum in GeV/c. The Lorentz factors for the initial Xi^- in the lab are beta_\Xi=v_\Xi/c and gamma_\Xi=1/\sqrt{1-\beta_\Xi^2}. A bar over a symbol indicates the Xi^- rest frame. The unknowns to determine are p_\Lambda, \theta_\Lambda, p_\pi, and \theta_\pi in the laboratory frame.

A K-meson (denoted K) of rest energy 494 MeV decays at rest into a muon (denoted mu) of rest energy 106 MeV and a neutrino of zero rest energy. Work in natural units with c=1. Define m_K=494 MeV and m_mu=106 MeV as the rest energies of K and mu, respectively. Let E_mu and E_nu denote the total energies of the muon and neutrino. Let p_mu and p_nu denote the magnitudes of their three-momenta. Let T_mu and T_nu denote their kinetic energies, with T=E-m for massive particles and T=E for massless particles. Find T_mu and T_nu in the K rest frame.

A neutral long-lived kaon K_L^0 with rest mass M decays in flight into two charged pions pi^+ and pi^- with rest mass m. The rest energies are M c^2 = 498 \unit{MeV} and m c^2 = 140 \unit{MeV}. The kaon's laboratory momentum magnitude p satisfies p/(M c) = 1. Use natural units c = 1 in intermediate formulas; numerical answers must be reported with units. Definitions: E_K is the kaon laboratory-frame energy, beta_c = v_K/c is the kaon speed parameter, and gamma_c = 1/sqrt(1 - beta_c^2) is the corresponding Lorentz factor. In the kaon rest frame, \bar{E} and \bar{p} are respectively the energy and momentum magnitude of a decay pion, and \bar{\theta} is its emission angle relative to the kaon's laboratory flight direction. In the laboratory, p_t and p_l are respectively the transverse and longitudinal components of a decay pion's momentum. Determine p_{t,max}, the maximum possible transverse momentum, and p_{l,max}, the maximum possible longitudinal momentum, of a decay pion in the laboratory.

The proper (rest-frame) mean lifetime of a charged pion is tau_0=2.6\times 10^{-8}\unit{s}. A monoenergetic beam of charged pions travels a distance l=10\unit{m} in the laboratory frame, during which 10\% of the pions decay, so N(l)=\frac{9}{10}N_0, where N_0 is the initial number of pions and N(l) is the number remaining after distance l. Let v be the pion speed, define \beta=\frac{v}{c} with 0<\beta<1, and let c=3\times 10^8\unit{m/s} be the speed of light. Define the Lorentz factor \gamma=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^2}}. Let t be the flight time over distance l, \tau the mean lifetime in the lab frame, and \lambda=\frac{1}{\tau} the decay constant in the lab frame. Let m be the charged pion rest mass with numerical value m=0.14\unit{GeV/c^2}. Use p for the pion momentum and T for the pion kinetic energy, to be reported in \unit{GeV/c} and \unit{GeV}, respectively.

A particle of mass M=3\unit{GeV/c^2} moves along the x-axis in the laboratory with momentum p=4\unit{GeV/c}. It decays into two photons. In the parent-particle rest frame (denoted by overbars), the emission is isotropic: for the photon polar angle \theta^* measured from the x-axis in that frame, we have $$\frac{dP}{d\cos\theta^*}=\frac{1}{2}$$ Overbars, as in \bar E_i and \bar p_i for i=1,2, denote the energy and three-momentum of photon i in the parent-particle rest frame. Let p_x denote the x-component of a photon's momentum in the laboratory frame. Let \gamma be the Lorentz factor of the parent particle in the laboratory and \beta its speed in units of c in the laboratory. Define (p_x)_{max} and (p_x)_{min} as the maximum and minimum possible values of p_x, respectively. Determine (p_x)_{max} and (p_x)_{min}. Then find the probability density dP/dp_x as a function of p_x, and specify the domain on which it is valid.

A pi^0 meson decays isotropically into two photons in its rest frame. Consider a laboratory where a pi^0 moves along the z axis with momentum magnitude p_pi=280\unit{MeV/c}. The pion rest energy is m_pi c^2=140\unit{MeV}. Let theta be the laboratory polar angle of an emitted photon measured with respect to the pi^0 flight direction, and let phi be the corresponding laboratory azimuthal angle. Let theta^* and phi^* be the corresponding photon angles in the pion rest frame. Define w^*(cos theta^*,phi^*) as the rest-frame probability density per unit solid angle d\Omega^*=d(cos theta^*) d phi^* for photons produced in a single pi^0 decay, counting both photons, normalized by \int w^* d\Omega^*=2. Define the azimuth-integrated rest-frame distribution W^*(cos theta^*) by W^*(cos theta^*)=\int_0^{2\pi} w^*(cos theta^*,phi^*) d phi^*. Define the laboratory azimuth-integrated distribution W(cos theta) by \int_{-1}^{1} W(cos theta) d\cos\theta=2, so that W(cos theta) d\cos\theta is the probability to find a photon between cos theta and cos theta+d\cos\theta in the laboratory. Let k and E be the laboratory photon momentum magnitude and energy, and let k^* and E^* be their values in the pion rest frame. Let gamma_0 and beta_0 be the Lorentz factor and speed (in units of c) of the moving pi^0. Work in natural units c=1 in the derivation, while using \unit{} for the given input values. Find the explicit expression for W(cos theta) and give the final answer as W(cos theta)=...

Consider the two-body decay K^0 -> pi^+ + pi^-. The transition matrix element is specified as T_{if} = [G / sqrt(8 E_K E_+ E_-)] [P_K (P_+ + P_-)] / m_K. Here E_K, E_+, and E_- are the relativistic energies of K^0, pi^+, and pi^- respectively, and P_K, P_+, and P_- are the corresponding four-momenta. The symbol P_A P_B denotes the Lorentz-invariant scalar product. The constants are: m_K for the K-meson mass, mu for the pion mass, G for the coupling constant, hbar for the reduced Planck constant, and c for the speed of light. In the rest frame of the decaying K^0, let p_1 and p_2 be the magnitudes of the three-momenta of pi^+ and pi^- respectively, E_1 and E_2 their energies (with E_1 = E_+ and E_2 = E_-), and Omega the solid angle. Let E denote the total final-state energy E = E_1 + E_2. The total transition probability per unit time is W, the density of final states is rho(E), and the lifetime measured in the K^0 rest frame is tau, with tau = 1/W. Assume the decay is isotropic. Show that the lifetime of the K^0 meson in its rest frame is tau = [ G^2 / (8 pi hbar^4 c) sqrt(m_K^2/4 - mu^2) ]^{-1}.

Estimate the attenuation of a monoenergetic beam of 50\unit{keV} X-rays passing through a 1\unit{cm}-thick layer of human soft tissue. Assume the tissue density equals that of water and use the mass attenuation coefficient at 50\unit{keV}. Define I_0 as the incident intensity, I as the transmitted intensity after thickness x, ρ as the tissue mass density, μ_m as the mass attenuation coefficient, A as the areal density A=ρx, and f_att as the attenuation fraction f_att=1-I/I_0. Use ρ≈1\unit{g/cm^3} and μ_m≈0.221\unit{cm^2/g}. Compute f_att for x=1\unit{cm}.

Given: The mean free path l of fast neutrons in lead (Pb) is l=5\unit{cm}. Lead has density \rho=10\unit{g/cm^3} and molar mass approximated by A=200\unit{g/mol} (atomic mass number ~200). Let N_0=6.022\times 10^{23}\unit{mol^{-1}} be Avogadro's constant. Let n denote the number density of Pb atoms and \sigma the total neutron–Pb interaction cross section. Express the final answer for \sigma both in \unit{cm^2} and in barns b, where 1\unit{b}=10^{-24}\unit{cm^2}.

A sheet of cadmium (Cd) is placed in a beam of slow neutrons to reduce the transmitted intensity to one twentieth of its original value. Let t be the Cd sheet thickness, I_0 the initial beam intensity, and I(t) the transmitted intensity after passing through thickness t. Let n be the number density of Cd atoms, given by n=ρN_A/A, where ρ is the mass density of Cd, A is the atomic weight of Cd, and N_A is Avogadro's constant. Let σ be the microscopic absorption cross section of Cd. Use the unit conversion 1 barn = 10^{-24} cm^2. Given data are A=112 g/mol, ρ=8.7×10^3 kg/m^3, σ=2500 barn, and N_A=6.022×10^{23} mol^{-1}. Find t such that the transmitted intensity condition holds

Mean free path for 3 MeV electron-neutrinos in matter. Let X denote the mean free path expressed as mass column density, defined by the relation below. Here ρ is the mass density of the medium, ℓ is the geometric mean free path length in the medium, σ is the neutrino–matter interaction cross section, and n is the atomic number density of the medium. Report the final answer as X_ans=... using one of the options provided.

$$X=\rho \ell$$

Option A
$$X=10 \unit{g/cm^2}$$

Option B
$$X=10^7 \unit{g/cm^2}$$

Option C
$$X=10^{17} \unit{g/cm^2}$$

Option D
$$X=10^{27} \unit{g/cm^2}$$

Let \alpha denote the fine-structure constant, defined by \alpha = \frac{e^2}{\hbar c}, where e is the elementary charge, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and c is the speed of light. The experimentally determined value is \alpha = \frac{1}{137.03604(11)}. Determine the number of significant figures to which \alpha is known.

A detector observes counts from a radioactive source that follow a Poisson process. Let r be the average count rate and T be the observation time. Let X be the number of counts recorded in time T. In this problem, r = 4 per second and T = 1 second, so the mean count in one second is λ = rT. Find the probability P(X=8). In the general Poisson formula, k denotes a nonnegative integer.

In a cloud chamber filled with air at atmospheric pressure, 5 \unit{MeV} alpha particles make tracks about L = 4 \unit{cm} long. Using the nuclear radius model R = r_0 A^{1/3} with r_0 = 1.2 \unit{fm} and A = 14.7 for the average air nucleus, and taking the density of air to be \rho = 1.293 \unit{kg/m^3} and Avogadro's number N_A = 6.023 \times 10^{23} \unit{mol^{-1}}, estimate how many tracks must be observed to have a good chance of finding one with a distinct sharp bend resulting from a nuclear encounter. Let \sigma denote the nuclear geometric cross section, n the number density of nuclei, and \lambda the mean free path for such encounters. Define N_{obs} as the required number of observed tracks such that the expected number of encounters in N_{obs} tracks of length L is of order one. Assume that a distinct sharp bend corresponds to a single large-angle scattering event with probability per unit path length equal to n \sigma.

A radioactive source emits two types of radiation, A and B. A counter distinguishes between them. In a given counting interval, the number of detected A-type events is N_A=1000 and the number of detected B-type events is N_B=2000. Assume the two counting processes are independent and follow Poisson statistics. Let r denote the measured ratio r=N_A/N_B. Let Δr denote the standard (1σ) statistical uncertainty of r, and let ΔN_A and ΔN_B denote the standard deviations of N_A and N_B, respectively. Compute Δr.

A proton synchrotron at Batavia accelerates protons to total energy E = 500 GeV. Let R be the bending radius in a uniform dipole magnetic field B. The proton has rest mass m, charge magnitude e, speed v, and moves relativistically with the following definitions. Use c for the speed of light. Assume a typical guide field B = 1 T and that at this energy the motion is ultrarelativistic with beta approximately 1. Choose the closest order of magnitude for R from {10^2 m, 10^3 m, 10^4 m, 10^5 m}. Report the selected option as R_ans = ...

$$
\beta = \frac{v}{c}
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^2}}
$$

$$
p = m \gamma \beta c
$$

$$
R = \frac{p}{e B}
$$

$$
E = m \gamma c^2
$$

$$
B = 1 \unit{T}
$$

Space charge in a charged particle beam produces a potential difference between the beam axis and its surface. A 10\unit{mA} beam of 50\unit{keV} protons travels along the axis of an evacuated beam pipe with speed v = 3 \times 10^{6}\unit{m/s}. The beam has a circular cross section of diameter 1\unit{cm}. Assume the current density is uniform over the beam diameter. Let I be the beam current, v the particle speed, R the beam radius, r the radial coordinate within the beam, j the uniform current density, \rho the charge density, E the electric field magnitude at radius r, V the electric potential, \varepsilon_0 the vacuum permittivity, S the beam cross-sectional disk, dS the outward differential area vector on S, and l the length of a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface. Define the desired potential difference as \Delta V = V(0) - V(R). Compute \Delta V.

Estimate the ground-level differential cosmic-ray flux per unit area, per unit time, and per unit solid angle. Let Φ_total denote the total flux, Φ_hard the hard component, and Φ_soft the soft component, all measured in counts per unit area per unit time per unit solid angle. Among the following approximate rates per unit area and solid angle, select the closest to the true value at ground level: 1/\unit{year}, 1/\unit{min}, 1/\unit{ms}, 1/\unit{\mu s}, per \unit{cm^{2} sr}. Report the final choice as ans, where ans is the selected rate expressed in the form ans = 1 \unit{cm^{-2} min^{-1} sr^{-1}}.

Define \phi as the particle flux per pulse (number of particles delivered per single pulse) in a giant proton accelerator. Select the typical value of \phi from the following options:

$$10^{4}\unit{pulse^{-1}}$$
$$10^{8}\unit{pulse^{-1}}$$
$$10^{13}\unit{pulse^{-1}}$$
$$10^{18}\unit{pulse^{-1}}$$

Define the constant-volume heat capacity C_v and, using a first-principles estimate based on the Dulong–Petit law, calculate the numerical value for a copper penny in your pocket. Use and define the following quantities: Q is the heat added to the penny, T is its temperature, and C_v is evaluated at constant volume. Let m be the mass of the penny with m=32 \unit{g}. Let M_{Cu} be the molar mass of copper with M_{Cu}=64 \unit{g/mol}. Let n be the amount of substance with n=m/M_{Cu}. Let R be the universal gas constant. Let C_{v,m} be the molar constant-volume heat capacity of a monatomic solid, given by C_{v,m}=3R. Compute the total constant-volume heat capacity C_v of the penny. Although the prompt mentions calories, keep the final numerical result in the units obtained in the solution.

Select the best estimate of the specific heat capacity of granite from the options 0.02, 0.2, 20, 2000 cal/g·K. Assume granite can be approximated by its main component calcium carbonate CaCO3. Let C denote the specific heat capacity of granite in units of \unit{cal/g.K}. Let R denote the universal gas constant in units of \unit{cal/mol.K}. Let M denote the molar mass in units of \unit{g/mol}. Use the Dulong–Petit estimate that the molar heat capacity of a solid is approximately 3R. Use M=100\unit{g/mol} for CaCO3.

One mole of a real gas obeys the van der Waals equation of state. The symbols used are: V for molar volume, p for pressure, T for absolute temperature, R for the universal gas constant, and a and b for the van der Waals constants. The molar internal energy is denoted by u, with c a constant independent of T and V. The molar heat capacities at constant volume and constant pressure are denoted by C_v and C_p, respectively. The gas obeys the van der Waals equation of state
$$(p+\frac{a}{V^2})(V-b)=RT$$
and the molar internal energy is
$$u=cT-\frac{a}{V}$$
Calculate C_v and C_p.

A homogeneous isotropic solid object has mass M, density rho, and coefficient of linear thermal expansion alpha. The object is at pressure p. Let Q be the heat added to the system, U its internal energy, V its volume, and T its temperature. The heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume are defined by C_p = (dQ/dT)_p and C_v = (dQ/dT)_v, respectively. For isotropic solids, the volumetric expansion coefficient beta satisfies beta = (1/V)(dV/dT) and beta = 3 alpha. Use V = M/rho. Show that the relation between C_p and C_v at pressure p is C_p - C_v = 3 alpha M p / rho.

For a diatomic ideal gas near room temperature, determine the fraction of the heat supplied that is available for external work in two cases: (i) an isobaric (constant-pressure) expansion and (ii) an isothermal (constant-temperature) expansion. Let n be the number of moles, p the pressure during the isobaric process, V_1 and V_2 the initial and final volumes, T_1 and T_2 the initial and final temperatures, and R the ideal gas constant. Define ΔT = T_2 - T_1. Let W be the work done by the gas on the surroundings and Q the heat supplied to the gas. Let ΔU be the change in internal energy of the gas. Let C_v be the constant-volume heat capacity of the entire sample and let C_p = C_v + n R be the constant-pressure heat capacity of the entire sample. For a diatomic ideal gas near room temperature, take C_v = \frac{5}{2} n R and C_p = \frac{7}{2} n R. Use the ideal gas law p V = n R T. Report the answers as φ_p = W/Q for the isobaric process and φ_T = W/Q for the isothermal process.

An ideal gas is contained in a large jar of volume V_0. Fitted to the jar is a vertical glass tube of cross-sectional area A in which a metal ball of mass M fits snugly and can move without friction. The external atmospheric pressure is p_0. Let g denote the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity. If the ball is displaced slightly from its static equilibrium position, it executes simple harmonic motion. The gas changes are quasistatic and adiabatic with ratio of specific heats gamma. Let V denote the equilibrium volume of the gas during the small oscillations (for a large jar, V may be taken approximately equal to V_0), and let p denote the instantaneous gas pressure. Let x be the small displacement of the ball from equilibrium chosen so that an increase in x increases the gas volume, so that the volume change satisfies dV = A x. Let F be the net restoring force on the ball due to the pressure change, k the effective spring constant, omega the angular frequency, and f the oscillation frequency. At static equilibrium the internal gas pressure is p = p_0 + M g / A. For the adiabatic motion, p V^gamma = K, where K is a positive constant for the gas during the motion. Find f in terms of A, V, M, p_0, g, and gamma.

Two systems with constant heat capacities C_1 and C_2 interact thermally and reach a common final equilibrium temperature T_f. The initial temperature of system 1 is T_1, and the initial temperature of system 2 is T_2. Assume the combined systems are isolated so that the total energy remains constant. Find the initial temperature T_2 in terms of C_1, C_2, T_1, and T_f.

A spherical black body of radius r at absolute temperature T is placed in a large environment at uniform absolute temperature T_0. It is surrounded by a thin concentric spherical shell of radius R that is black on both sides. The space between the spheres is evacuated, and thermal conduction is negligible. Let sigma denote the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. Define the rate of cooling as the net radiative power loss of the black body. Let Q be the radiative power loss of the black body without the shell, Q' be the radiative power loss of the black body with the shell in place, Q'' be the radiative power loss of the shell, and T_1 be the uniform absolute temperature of the shell at steady state. Show that the reduction factor f = Q' / Q has the form a R^2 / (R^2 + b r^2), and find the numerical coefficients a and b

The solar constant (radiant flux at the surface of the earth) is about 0.1 \unit{W/cm^2}. Assume the Sun is a black body with temperature T. Let \sigma be the Stefan–Boltzmann constant, J the radiant flux density at the surface of the Sun, r_S the radius of the Sun, and r_{SE} the distance between the Sun and the Earth. Use r_S=7.0\times10^5 \unit{km} and r_{SE}=1.5\times10^8 \unit{km}. Find T in \unit{K}.

Estimate the equilibrium surface temperature T_N of Neptune by neglecting any internal heat sources. State clearly the radiative assumptions about Neptune's surface and atmosphere. Use the following symbols and data. Definitions: R_S is the Sun's radius, R_N is Neptune's radius, R_SE is the mean Sun–Earth distance, R_SN is the mean Sun–Neptune distance, T_S is the Sun's surface temperature, T_N is Neptune's equilibrium surface temperature, J_E is the solar flux at Earth's orbit (solar constant), σ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant, P_abs is the total solar power absorbed by Neptune, and P_emit is the total thermal power emitted by Neptune. Assumptions: Bond albedo A = 0 (Neptune absorbs all incident solar radiation), emissivity ε = 1 (blackbody emission), and efficient heat redistribution so that emission is over the full surface 4 π R_N^2. Given data: R_S = 7 × 10^5 \unit{km}; R_N = 2.2 × 10^4 \unit{km}; R_SE = 1.5 × 10^8 \unit{km}; R_SN = 4.5 × 10^9 \unit{km}; T_S = 6000 \unit{K}; J_E = 1.4 × 10^3 \unit{W/m^2}; σ = 5.7 × 10^{-8} \unit{W/m^2 K^4}

A steam turbine operates between a hot intake at 400\unit{^\circ C} and a cold exhaust at 150\unit{^\circ C}. For a given heat input Q, determine the maximum possible net work W_{max} the turbine can produce. Under what condition is this maximum achieved?

Definitions for use in the solution:
- Q_1 is the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir at absolute temperature T_1
- Q_2 is the heat rejected to the cold reservoir at absolute temperature T_2
- W is the net work per cycle, with W = Q_1 - Q_2
- Q is the given heat input, so Q_1 = Q
- T_1 and T_2 denote the absolute temperatures corresponding to the intake and exhaust, respectively

A reservoir behind a high-power dam of height about 110\unit{m} can exhibit a temperature difference of up to 10\unit{^\circ C} between surface and bottom. Compare the maximum mechanical energy obtainable from the thermal energy of one gram of water using an ideal (Carnot) heat engine with the mechanical energy obtainable by allowing the same gram of water to fall through the dam height using conventional turbines. Use the following definitions and data: Let T_{high} be the hot-reservoir temperature and T_{low} the cold-reservoir temperature, with T_{low} = T_{high} - 10\unit{K}, and take T_{high} = 300\unit{K} (room temperature) for numerical evaluation. Let C_v be the specific heat of water per gram, with C_v = 1\unit{cal/(g^\circ C)}. Let Q be the heat extracted from 1\unit{g} of water when cooled from T_{high} to T_{low}. Let \eta denote the ideal (Carnot) efficiency. Let W denote the maximum work from the thermal gradient for 1\unit{g} of water. Let W' denote the mechanical work from gravitational potential when 1\unit{g} of water falls through height h. For the gravitational estimate use m = 1\unit{g}, g = 980\unit{cm/s^2}, and take h = 100\unit{m} = 10^4\unit{cm} for numerical calculation. Compute W and W' and then state whether W' < W or W' > W under ideal assumptions.

A building with indoor air at temperature T (in \unit{K}) is heated by an ideal heat pump that uses the outdoor atmosphere at temperature T_0 (in \unit{K}) as the heat source. The pump consumes power W (in \unit{W}). The building loses heat to the outside at a rate alpha (T - T_0) (in \unit{W}), where alpha (in \unit{W/K}) is a thermal loss coefficient. Let T_e denote the equilibrium indoor temperature (in \unit{K}). Find T_e.

Calculate the entropy change ΔS for heating a gram-atomic weight of silver at constant volume from T1=0°C to T2=30°C. Over this temperature range, the molar heat capacity at constant volume may be treated as constant with Cv=5.85 cal/(K mol). Use Kelvin for temperature in the formulas. Definitions: n is the amount of substance in moles; for a gram-atomic weight, n=1 mol. T1 and T2 are the initial and final absolute temperatures in Kelvin. Cv is the molar heat capacity at constant volume.

Compute the entropy difference ΔS between two states of nitrogen at pressure p=1 atm: State A is 1 g of nitrogen gas at 20 °C, and State B is 1 g of liquid nitrogen at its boiling point −196 °C. Assume the nitrogen vapor behaves as an ideal gas with temperature-independent molar heat capacity at constant pressure Cp=7.0 cal/(mol K). The latent heat of vaporization per unit mass is L_g=47.6 cal/g at the boiling point. Use the molar mass M=28 g/mol. Let m=1 g and n=m/M be the number of moles. Let T1 be the absolute temperature corresponding to 20 °C and T2 be the absolute temperature corresponding to −196 °C. Define L=M L_g as the molar latent heat. Define ΔS′ as the entropy change for isobaric heating of the vapor from T2 to T1, ΔS″ as the entropy change for isothermal, isobaric vaporization at T2, and ΔS=ΔS′+ΔS″. Report the final answer as ΔS in units of cal/K.

A thermally conducting, uniform, and homogeneous bar of length L, cross-sectional area A, density ρ, and specific heat at constant pressure c_p is brought to a nonuniform temperature distribution by contact at one end with a hot reservoir at temperature T_H and at the other end with a cold reservoir at temperature T_c. After steady state is established, the bar is removed from the reservoirs, thermally insulated, and kept at constant pressure. Let x denote the coordinate along the bar measured from the cold end. Define the total heat capacity at constant pressure C_p = c_p ρ A L and the final uniform temperature T_f after insulation. Show that the change in entropy of the bar ΔS satisfies

A mixture of n_1 = 1/10 mole of helium with heat-capacity ratio gamma_1 = C_{p1}/C_{v1} = 5/3 and n_2 = 1/5 mole of nitrogen with heat-capacity ratio gamma_2 = C_{p2}/C_{v2} = 7/5 is modeled as an ideal mixture of two ideal gases. The mixture is initially at temperature T_i = 300\unit{K} and volume V_i = 4\unit{L}. The gas is compressed slowly and adiabatically (reversible and adiabatic). Let p_i, V_i, T_i denote the initial pressure, volume, and temperature, and p_f, V_f, T_f the corresponding final values. Let R be the ideal-gas constant. Let C_{v1}, C_{p1} be the molar heat capacities at constant volume and pressure for helium, and C_{v2}, C_{p2} those for nitrogen. Let \Delta S_1 and \Delta S_2 denote the entropy changes of the helium and nitrogen components, respectively. Show that the changes of temperature and pressure during the compression can be described by an intermediate exponent g defined via T_f = T_i (V_i/V_f)^g for the mixture, and determine g and the effective heat-capacity ratio gamma of the mixture in terms of C_{v1}, C_{p1}, C_{v2}, C_{p2}. Then compute the magnitudes of the changes when the volume is reduced by 1%, i.e., when V_f = (99/100) V_i. Report exact expressions and numerical approximations for T_f and p_f.

Two finite, identical, solid bodies each have a constant total heat capacity per body C. Their initial temperatures are T_1 and T_2, respectively. They are used as heat sources to drive a heat engine that operates until both bodies reach a common final temperature T_f. Find the maximum total work W_max obtainable from the system. Use SI units: C in \unit{J/K}, T_1, T_2, T_f in \unit{K}, and W, W_max in \unit{J}. Define W as the total work extracted and \Delta S as the total entropy change of the two-body system.

Consider a simple gas. Let c_p and c_v denote the specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively, defined per unit mass with SI units of J/(kg K). Let C_p and C_v denote the molar heat capacities with SI units of J/(mol K). Let S be the entropy, T the temperature, p the pressure, V the volume, m the mass of the gas sample, n the number of moles, M the molar mass, R the universal gas constant, α the coefficient of thermal expansion, and κ_T the isothermal compressibility. Tasks: 1) Find the approximate numerical value of c_p - c_v for simple gases and explain the physical reason why c_p differs from c_v. 2) Calculate c_p - c_v for an ideal gas, expressing the result both per unit mass and per mole.

A paramagnetic system is placed in a uniform external magnetic field H and is thermally insulated from the surroundings, so the process is adiabatic with constant entropy S. The system is quasi-statically demagnetized from an initial field H_i to a final field H_f=0 while remaining in internal equilibrium. The internal energy is U, the entropy is S, the temperature is T, and the induced magnetization is M. The equation of state for the magnetization is M=a H/T, and the heat capacity at constant H is c_H=b/T^2, where a and b are positive constants. Use dU=T dS+H dM for this magnetic system. Determine how the temperature T changes as H is reduced to zero, and express T(H) and the final temperature T_f in terms of the initial temperature T_i and field H_i. Then, find the initial field strength H_i required so that the final temperature is half of the initial temperature, i.e., T_f=T_i/2. All logarithms are natural logarithms, and the final answers should be written using the variables H_i, H_f, T_i, T_f, a, and b as defined above.

A substance has the equation of state relating pressure p, volume V, and temperature T with a constant A. Its internal energy U depends on constants B, n, a reference volume V_0, and a function f(T) of temperature only. Let S denote the entropy. Determine the constants B and n such that S is a state function consistent with the first law of thermodynamics. The equation of state is
$$p=\frac{A T^3}{V}$$
The internal energy is
$$U=B T^n \ln\frac{V}{V_0}+f(T)$$
Here p is the pressure, V is the volume, T is the temperature, A is a constant, U is the internal energy, B and n are constants to be determined, V_0 is a constant reference volume, f(T) depends only on T, and S is the entropy.

A thermodynamic system has Gibbs free energy

$$
G(p,T)=R T \ln \frac{a p}{(R T)^{5/2}}
$$

where p is the pressure, T is the absolute temperature, and a and R are constants. Let S denote the entropy and C_p the specific heat at constant pressure. Find C_p.

Consider a simple compressible substance held at pressure p and absolute temperature T. Let V be the volume and S be the entropy. Define the heat emitted from the system during a reversible process as Q_{em}, with differential dQ_{em} = - T dS. Show that, at constant temperature T, the following holds: (∂Q_{em}/∂p)_T = T (∂V/∂T)_p. Use standard thermodynamic notation, where subscripts on partial derivatives indicate the variable held constant.

A hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell produces electrical energy W by feeding O2 to one electrode and H2 to the other. The gases react reversibly, isothermally, and isobarically at p = 1 \unit{atm} and T = 298 \unit{K} to form liquid water. The internal resistance of the cell is negligible. Calculate the cell e.m.f. \varepsilon for the formation of 1 \unit{mol} of water. Definitions and data: q is the total electric charge transferred through the external circuit, F is the Faraday constant with F = 96500 \unit{C/mol}, h_O, h_H, h_W are the molar enthalpies at 1 \unit{atm} and 298 \unit{K} for O2, H2, and H2O, respectively, with h_O = 17200 \unit{J/mol}, h_H = 8100 \unit{J/mol}, h_W = -269300 \unit{J/mol}. S_O, S_H, S_W are the corresponding molar entropies with S_O = 201 \unit{J/(mol.K)}, S_H = 128 \unit{J/(mol.K)}, S_W = 66.7 \unit{J/(mol.K)}. \Delta G denotes the Gibbs free energy change of the reaction.

A certain magnetic salt obeys Curie's law and has a heat capacity per unit volume at constant magnetic field inversely proportional to the square of the absolute temperature. Equivalently, its magnetic susceptibility and total heat capacity at constant field are specified by the relations χ = b/T and c_H = α V/T^2 with α = b + a H^2, where a and b are constants, V is the sample volume, and H is the magnitude of the applied uniform magnetic field. Let μ_0 denote the vacuum permeability. A sample initially at temperature T_i is placed in a magnetic field of strength H and then adiabatically and quasistatically demagnetized to zero field. Use M = χ H for the magnetization M, S for the entropy, and G(T,H) for the Gibbs free energy. Find the final temperature τ.

The atmosphere can be in a convective steady state at constant entropy (adiabatic), not at constant temperature. In such an equilibrium, for each gas parcel the adiabatic invariant p V^{\gamma} is independent of altitude z, where \gamma=C_p/C_v is the ratio of specific heats. Here p is the pressure, V is the specific volume per molecule defined by V=1/n with n the molecular number density, T is the temperature, z is the altitude, g is the magnitude of the uniform gravitational acceleration, m is the molecular mass of a representative molecule, and k is Boltzmann's constant. Using the condition of hydrostatic equilibrium in a uniform gravitational field, find an expression for dT/dz, the vertical temperature gradient (lapse rate).

Consider a small parcel of air near the ground that rises slowly, reversibly, and adiabatically, remaining in mechanical equilibrium with the surrounding atmosphere at each height. For small height z above the ground, assume linear profiles p(z)=p_0(1-α z) and T(z)=T_0(1-β z), where z is measured upward from the ground, p(z) is the atmospheric pressure, T(z) is the atmospheric temperature, p_0=p(0), T_0=T(0), and α>0, β>0 are the lapse-rate coefficients to be determined. Let ρ(z) be the atmospheric mass density and ρ_0=ρ(0). Let g be the gravitational acceleration near Earth’s surface, R the universal gas constant, and M the molar mass of air. Assume the air behaves as an ideal gas. The adiabatic index is γ=C_p/C_v, where C_p and C_v are the molar heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively. For a reversible adiabatic process the parcel obeys T^γ p^{1−γ}=K_ad, where K_ad is a constant of the adiabatic path. Suppose the air is a mixture of 4/5 N2 and 1/5 O2 by mole. Assume T_0 is low enough that vibrational modes are frozen but high enough that rotational modes are fully excited classically. Find α and β as functions of T_0, g, and M, and evaluate M for the given composition.

Choose the correct melting point of tungsten from the options: 350\unit{^\circ C}, 3500\unit{^\circ C}, 35000\unit{^\circ C}, or 350000\unit{^\circ C}. Let T_m denote the melting point of tungsten. Provide your final answer in the form T_m = ... with the appropriate unit

At temperature T=300\unit{K}, the energy required to liberate one molecule from the surface of a certain liquid is E_m=\frac{1}{20}\unit{eV}. Use Avogadro's number N_A=6.023\times10^{23}\unit{mol^{-1}} and the conversion 1\unit{eV}=1.6\times10^{-12}\unit{erg}. Find the molar heat of vaporization L_{vap} in \unit{erg/mol}.

Twenty grams of ice at 0 \unit{^\circ C} are dropped into a beaker containing 120 grams of water initially at 70 \unit{^\circ C}. Neglect the heat capacity of the beaker. The heat of fusion of ice is 80 \unit{cal/g}. Use the following symbols and values, all in consistent units of \unit{g}, \unit{cal}, and \unit{^\circ C}: M_1 = 20 \unit{g} (mass of ice), M_2 = 120 \unit{g} (mass of water), T_0 = 70 \unit{^\circ C} (initial temperature of the water), L_{fusion} = 80 \unit{cal/g} (heat of fusion of ice), C_{p,water} = 1 \unit{cal/(g. ^\circ C)} (specific heat capacity of liquid water). Let T be the final equilibrium temperature of the mixture in \unit{^\circ C}. Find T.

Find the smallest possible time τ necessary to freeze m=2 kg of liquid water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C using a motor of power P=50 W, when the outside air (hot reservoir) is at 27 °C. Assume the process is carried out by an ideal reversible (Carnot) refrigerator. Definitions and data: T1 is the absolute temperature of the hot reservoir, T2 is the absolute temperature of the cold reservoir, L_f is the latent heat of fusion of water, Q2 is the heat extracted from the cold reservoir at T2, ε is the coefficient of performance of a Carnot refrigerator, W_min is the minimum work input, and τ is the minimum time required. Use T1=300 K, T2=273 K, and L_f≈3.34×10^5 J/kg.

Compute the theoretical minimum mechanical work W_min required to freeze M=1 kilogram of liquid water when both the water and the only available large heat reservoir (the surroundings) are initially at T_0=25 \unit{C}. The process ends with the water at T_f=0 \unit{C}. Use the latent heat of fusion of ice L=80 \unit{cal/g} and the specific heat at constant pressure of liquid water C_p=1 \unit{cal/(g C)}. In all thermodynamic integrals, T denotes the absolute temperature in Kelvin. Definitions for the solution: W_1 is the minimum work to reversibly cool the water from T_0 to T_f, W_2 is the minimum work to reversibly freeze the water at T_f, and W_min=W_1+W_2. The mass symbol M denotes the mass of the water.

A mass M of liquid water is cooled and frozen by contact with a thermal reservoir, all at constant pressure. Given: M=10\unit{kg}; initial water temperature T_i=293\unit{K} (20\unit{\degree C}); melting temperature T_m=273\unit{K} (0\unit{\degree C}); final ice temperature T_f=263\unit{K} (-10\unit{\degree C}); reservoir temperature T_R=263\unit{K} (-10\unit{\degree C}); heat capacities at constant pressure C_{p,w}=4180\unit{J/(kg K)} for water and C_{p,i}=2090\unit{J/(kg K)} for ice; latent heat of fusion L_f=3.34\times 10^5\unit{J/kg}. Compute the total entropy change of the universe, defined as \Delta S_{univ}=\Delta S_1+\Delta S_2+\Delta S_3+\Delta S_R, where \Delta S_1 is the entropy change of water cooling from T_i to T_m, \Delta S_2 is the entropy change of freezing at T_m, \Delta S_3 is the entropy change of ice cooling from T_m to T_f, and \Delta S_R is the entropy change of the reservoir that absorbs all the released heat at temperature T_R.

Estimate the surface tension sigma of a liquid whose heat of vaporization per unit mass is Q = 10^{10} erg/g (equivalently 250 cal/g). Assume the surface tension equals the free energy of the surface per unit area, modeled by sigma = Q r rho, where r is the effective thickness of the surface layer and rho is the liquid density. Use r = 10^{-8} cm and rho = 1 g/cm^3.

In a closed container at the triple point of water, one gram each of ice, liquid water, and water vapor are initially in equilibrium. The pressure is 4.58\unit{mmHg} and the temperature is 1/100\unit{^\circ C}. Then heat Q=60\unit{cal} is added to the system while the total volume of the container is kept constant. Assume ideal-gas behavior for the vapor. Let x, y, z denote, respectively, the final masses of ice, liquid water, and water vapor in grams. Calculate, to within 2\%, the values of x, y, and z. Justify your answer. Use the following data at 1/100\unit{^\circ C}: latent heat of fusion L_{fus}=80\unit{cal/g}, latent heat of vaporization L_{vap}=596\unit{cal/g}, latent heat of sublimation L_{sub}=676\unit{cal/g}, water and ice densities \rho_{water}=\rho_{ice}=1\unit{g/cm^3}, molar mass of water \mu=18\unit{g/mol}, ideal-gas constant R=8.2\times 10^8\unit{m^3 atm/(mol K)}, absolute temperature T=273.16\unit{K}, and pressure p=4.58\unit{mmHg}. You may also note that the volume of the vapor is much larger than the volume of the water or the volume of the ice.

Determine the ratio r_c = p_c V_c / (R T_c) at the critical point for a gas that obeys the Dieterici equation of state p (V - b) = R T exp(-a / (R T V)). Here p is the pressure, V is the molar volume, T is the temperature, R is the universal gas constant, and a and b are positive constants characteristic of the gas. The critical point (p_c, V_c, T_c) is defined by the conditions (∂p/∂V)_T = 0 and (∂^2 p/∂V^2)_T = 0 evaluated at T = T_c and V = V_c. Report the numerical value of r_c accurately to two significant figures.

Consider a charged, thin conducting soap bubble of equilibrium radius r carrying total charge q. Let the electric potential at the bubble surface be φ. The air inside the bubble is phase α with pressure p^α, the air outside is phase β with pressure p^β, and the soap film is phase γ. Let F^α, F^β, and F^γ denote the Helmholtz free energies of phases α, β, and γ, respectively. Define the excess of ambient pressure over internal pressure as Δp = p^β - p^α. Assume the temperature is constant and surface tension is neglected. The bubble is a spherical conductor in Gaussian units, so φ = q/r. Use the minimum free energy condition to find the relation among r, φ, and Δp. You may use V^α = 4π r^3/3, δV^α = 4π r^2 δr, and δV^β = - δV^α.

Derive the vapor-pressure equation (Clausius-Clapeyron equation) for liquid-vapor equilibrium along the coexistence curve. Let p be the pressure and T the temperature. For each phase i in {1 (vapor), 2 (liquid)}, let mu_i be the chemical potential per mole (molar Gibbs free energy), S_i the molar entropy, and V_i the molar volume. Let L be the latent heat of vaporization per mole, with L = T(S_1 - S_2) at equilibrium. Find dp/dT along the coexistence curve.

Data and goal: The specific latent heat of vaporization of water is L_s = 2.44×10^6\unit{J/kg}. The saturated water vapor density at the boiling point is ρ_2 = 0.598\unit{kg/m^3} at 100\unit{^\circ C}. The density of liquid water is ρ_1 = 1000\unit{kg/m^3}. Assume an isothermal atmosphere with air temperature T_0 = 300\unit{K}. The density of air at 0\unit{^\circ C} and 1\unit{atm} is ρ = 1.29\unit{kg/m^3}. Let z be altitude, p(z) the atmospheric pressure at altitude z, and p(0) the sea-level pressure. Let m be the mean mass of an air molecule, k the Boltzmann constant, and g the gravitational acceleration with g = 9.8\unit{m/s^2}. Let T be the boiling temperature of water at pressure p, and let T(0) denote the boiling temperature at sea level with T(0) = 100\unit{^\circ C}. Define the constant α by α = L_s ρ_1 ρ_2/(ρ_1 − ρ_2). Using these definitions and the relations below, find the sea-level slope S = dT/dz near z = 0, and report S in \unit{^\circ C/km}.

A long vertical cylindrical column is filled with a single substance and placed in a uniform gravitational field of magnitude g. The system is at absolute temperature T. At equilibrium, the part of the column below a certain horizontal level is solid and the part above that level is liquid. Let the solid have density ρ_s and specific volume V_s=1/ρ_s, and let the liquid have density ρ_1 and specific volume V_1=1/ρ_1. When the temperature is lowered by ΔT, with ΔT/T ≪ 1, the solid–liquid interface is observed to move upward by a distance l. Neglect the thermal expansion of the solid and take L to be the latent heat per unit mass for the solid–liquid phase transition. Let p denote pressure. Find an expression for the liquid density ρ_1 in terms of ρ_s, L, g, T, and ΔT.

Liquid helium-4 has a normal boiling point at T_0=4.2\unit{K} under a saturation pressure p_0=1\unit{atm}=760\unit{mmHg}. At a reduced pressure p=1\unit{mmHg}, it boils at T=1.2\unit{K}. Estimate the average molar latent heat of vaporization L of helium-4 over this temperature range. You may take the saturated vapor to behave as an ideal gas, neglect the liquid molar volume compared to the vapor molar volume, and treat L as approximately constant over the interval. Use R for the universal gas constant, V_g for the molar volume of the saturated vapor, and V_l for the molar volume of the saturated liquid.

A certain substance is superconducting in the absence of external magnetic fields for temperatures satisfying the following condition. $$T<T_0$$ In the presence of a uniform magnetic field, the system can exist in two thermodynamic phases for temperatures satisfying the following condition. $$T<T_0'$$ Here T is the temperature, T_0 is the zero-field critical temperature, and T_0' is the highest temperature at which two phases can coexist in a magnetic field with T_0'\leq T_0. The two phases are specified as follows. $$B<B_c(T)$$ corresponds to the superconducting phase. $$B>B_c(T)$$ corresponds to the normal phase. Along the coexistence curve in the B–T plane we have the following condition. $$B=B_c(T)$$ The entropies per unit volume of the normal and superconducting phases along the coexistence curve are denoted by S_N(T) and S_s(T). The entropy discontinuity is defined by $$\Delta S=S_N(T)-S_s(T)$$ The critical field is given by $$B_c(T)=B_0\left(1-\frac{T^2}{T_0^2}\right)$$ We adopt Gaussian cgs conventions on the coexistence curve, with magnetizations per unit volume given by $$M_N=0$$ $$M_s=-\frac{B}{4\pi}$$ so that $$\Delta M=M_N-M_s=\frac{B}{4\pi}$$ Define the dimensionless temperature $$\tau=\frac{T}{T_0}$$ For reference in the solution, we will use the Clausius–Clapeyron relation of a p–V system, where p denotes the pressure and V the volume. Compute the entropy jump per unit volume across the coexistence curve as a function of \tau and the other parameters.

In granite, day–night temperature variations are significantly damped at a depth of about 10 cm. Let the temperature at depth Δx = 10 cm be constant at T0 (in degrees Celsius). At the time of the daily maximum surface temperature, take the surface temperature to be T1 ≈ T0 + 10 °C. The incident solar radiation intensity on the ground is Q = 1400 W/m^2, which is approximately Q ≈ 3.3 × 10^−2 cal/(s·cm^2). Assume this radiation is completely absorbed within the top Δx = 10 cm of the ground. Using Fourier’s law in one dimension and a linear temperature gradient, estimate the thermal conductivity K of granite and then choose the best option for K from the set {5 × 10^−3, 5 × 10^1, 5 × 10^2, 5 × 10^5} cal/(s·cm·°C). Define ΔT = T1 − T0, and define K_choice as the selected option for K.

Convection to and from a vertical surface (e.g., a window pane) in still air follows the empirical heat-flux law q = (2/5)×10^{-4} (Δt)^{5/4} in units of cal/s·cm^2, where Δt is the temperature difference between the surface and the adjacent air in °C. The indoor air temperature is 25 °C and the outdoor air temperature is −15 °C. A glass pane of thickness d = 2 mm has thermal conductivity k = 2×10^{-3} cal/(s·cm·°C). Neglect radiation and assume one-dimensional steady conduction through the pane. Define t_1 as the inner surface temperature in °C and t_2 as the outer surface temperature in °C. Let q be the steady heat flux per unit area in cal/s·cm^2. You may analyze a unit area A = 1 cm^2. Find the inner surface temperature t_1.

The water at the surface of a lake and the air above it are initially in thermal equilibrium at the freezing temperature of water. At time t=0, the air temperature suddenly drops by ΔT. Find the thickness of the ice on the lake as a function of time h(t), expressed in terms of the latent heat per unit volume L/V and the thermal conductivity Λ of ice. Assume ΔT is small enough that the specific heat of ice may be neglected and that heat transfer through the ice is by conduction across a linear temperature gradient. Use the following definitions and SI units: h(t) is the ice thickness in \unit{m}, t is time in \unit{s}, ΔT is the air temperature drop relative to the freezing temperature in \unit{K}, Λ is the thermal conductivity of ice in \unit{W m^{-1} K^{-1}}, L is latent heat in \unit{J}, V is volume in \unit{m^3}, so L/V is the latent heat per unit volume in \unit{J m^{-3}}. Let ΔS denote an arbitrary area element in \unit{m^2}.

A spherical, black asteroid made of rock has been ejected far from the Sun, so solar radiation is negligible. Heat is produced uniformly inside the asteroid by radioactive elements at a rate per unit mass of \(\dot q\). The asteroid has density \(\rho\), thermal conductivity \(k\), and radius \(R\). Assume a steady state and that the asteroid radiates as a blackbody with Stefan–Boltzmann constant \(\sigma\). Let \(T(r)\) be the temperature at radial coordinate \(r\), \(T_s=T(R)\) be the surface temperature, \(T_c=T(0)\) be the central temperature, and \(Q\) be the total power generated and radiated in steady state. Given: \(\dot q=3\times 10^{-14}\unit{cal/(g s)}\), \(\rho=3.5\unit{g/cm^3}\), \(k=5\times 10^{-3}\unit{cal/(cm s K)}\), \(R=100\unit{km}\). Determine \(T_c\) and \(T_s\).

When there is heat flow in a heat-conducting material, entropy increases. Consider a homogeneous heat conductor with thermal conductivity k (SI units \unit{W/(m K)}). Let T(x,t) be the temperature field, u(x,t) the internal energy per unit volume, S(x,t) the entropy per unit volume, and q(x,t) the heat flux vector. Neglect volume expansion so that the local thermodynamic relation du = T dS holds. Denote the local irreversible entropy generation rate per unit volume by \dot S (SI units \unit{W/(m^3 K)}). Given k and the temperature gradient \nabla T, find \dot S in terms of T and \nabla T.

A classical one-dimensional harmonic oscillator with mass m and spring constant k has a fixed total energy E. Let x be the instantaneous displacement from equilibrium, \dot x the velocity, l the oscillation amplitude, and T the period. The initial phase (starting time) is completely unknown, i.e., the observation time is uniformly distributed over one period. Find the probability density function p(x), where p(x) dx is the probability that the mass is found in the interval dx at position x.

A solid contains N magnetic atoms with spin 1/2. At sufficiently high temperature the spins are completely randomly oriented, and at sufficiently low temperature all spins align ferromagnetically along the same direction. Let C(T) denote the heat capacity of the solid at temperature T (measured in \unit{K}). Assume C(T) is approximated by a piecewise-linear form with a single-peak value c_1 attained at the upper end of its support. The model is specified by the following two conditions for a given temperature scale T_1 (in \unit{K}):

$$C(T)=c_1\left(\frac{2T}{T_1}-1\right)$$

for temperatures in the interval

$$\frac{T_1}{2}<T<T_1$$

and

$$C(T)=0$$

otherwise. Here k is the Boltzmann constant (in \unit{J/K}), S(T) is the entropy (in \unit{J/K}), and N is the number of spins. Using entropy considerations, find the maximum value c_1 of the heat capacity.

Consider a three-dimensional, nonrelativistic ideal gas in the nondegenerate limit λ << 1. The fugacity is defined by λ = exp(μ/kT), where μ is the chemical potential, T is the absolute temperature, k is Boltzmann’s constant, m is the particle mass, h is Planck’s constant, and n is the particle number density. The single-particle energy is denoted by ε, the single-particle occupation function by f(ε), and the density of states per unit volume (excluding spin degeneracy) by D(ε). The quantum volume is defined by V_Q = (h^2/2π m k T)^{3/2}. Show that λ = n V_Q.

Energy probability. Find and make a careful sketch of the probability density, \rho(E), for the kinetic energy E of a single atom in a classical, non-interacting, monatomic gas in thermal equilibrium at absolute temperature T. Let m be the mass of an atom, p the magnitude of its momentum vector, and k the Boltzmann constant. The kinetic energy is related to momentum by E = p^2/(2m). The function \rho(E) is defined so that \rho(E) dE is the probability that the energy lies in [E, E + dE]. In the derivation, introduce a normalization constant A for the momentum-space probability density, and use d^3 p for the volume element in momentum space. Let dP denote the probability that the atom’s momentum lies within a volume element d^3 p about p in momentum space.

Consider two distinct lattice sites, each occupied by a noninteracting atom with spin s=1. For each atom, the spin orientation yields a single-site energy epsilon that can take the values epsilon in {1, 0, -1}. Work in the canonical ensemble at inverse temperature beta = 1/(k_B T), so that a single-site level of energy epsilon has Boltzmann weight proportional to e^{- beta epsilon}. Denote by epsilon_1 and epsilon_2 the single-site energies for the two atoms. The total energy of the system, assumed to be that of the spins only, is U = epsilon_1 + epsilon_2. For any quantity X, write overline X for its ensemble average at fixed beta. Compute overline U and overline U^2.
problem_answer: $$\overline{U^2} = \frac{2 \left( e^{2\beta} + e^{-2\beta} + e^{\beta} + e^{-\beta} \right)}{\left( 1 + e^{\beta} + e^{-\beta} \right)^2}$$

problem_description: Consider a hydrogen molecule modeled by the empirical interatomic potential V between its two atoms. Let r be the internuclear separation, D, a, and r_0 be potential parameters, and m_H be the mass of a hydrogen atom. The potential is given by the expression $$V=D e^{-2 a (r-r_0)}-2 D e^{-a (r-r_0)}$$ Use the following numerical data: $$m_H=1.672\times 10^{-24}\unit{g}$$ $$D=7\times 10^{-12}\unit{erg}$$ $$a=2\times 10^{8}\unit{cm^{-1}}$$ $$r_0=8\times 10^{-9}\unit{cm}$$ Define the reduced mass by $$\mu=\frac{m_H}{2}$$ and the equilibrium separation by d=r_0. Let K denote the force constant of the potential at equilibrium, K=\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial r^2}\big|_{r=d}. Let \omega be the angular frequency of vibration of the two atoms, \hbar the reduced Planck constant, k_B the Boltzmann constant, and R the ideal gas constant. Define the characteristic rotational and vibrational temperatures by k_B\theta_R=\hbar^2/(2 I) with I=\mu d^2, and k_B\theta_V=\hbar\omega, respectively. Let T_R and T_V be the temperatures at which rotation and vibration begin to contribute appreciably to the molar specific heat, and set T_R=\theta_R and T_V=\theta_V. Let C_v and C_p denote the molar specific heats at constant volume and constant pressure, respectively. Neglect ionization and dissociation. Estimate T_R and T_V, and give approximate values of C_v and C_p at the following temperatures: $$T_1=25\unit{K}$$ $$T_2=250\unit{K}$$ $$T_3=2500\unit{K}$$ $$T_4=10000\unit{K}$$

Derive an expression for the molar vibrational specific heat at constant volume, C_{vib}(T), of a diatomic gas as a function of temperature. Start from the vibrational partition function Z_{vib}, evaluate it, then use the result to calculate C_{vib}. Finally, describe the high- and low-temperature limits. Use the following definitions and conventions: The vibrational energy levels are ε_v = ℏ ω_0 (v + 1/2) with v = 0, 1, 2, ..., where ℏ is the reduced Planck constant and ω_0 is the angular frequency of the vibrational mode. The absolute temperature is T, Boltzmann’s constant is k, and the vibrational temperature is θ defined by θ = ℏ ω_0 / k. Let β = 1/(k T). For one mole, N_A is Avogadro’s number and R = N_A k is the gas constant. The Helmholtz free energy for one mole is F, the internal energy (molar) is U, and the molar vibrational specific heat at constant volume is C_{vib}. In your final answers for the limiting behaviors, present them in the form C_{vib} = ...

A gas of molecular hydrogen H2 is initially in equilibrium at temperature T_i=1000 K. It is rapidly cooled to T_f=20 K so quickly that the nuclear spin states (parahydrogen vs orthohydrogen) do not change, although the translational and rotational degrees of freedom re-equilibrate through collisions. At high temperature the spin-statistics ratio is parahydrogen:orthohydrogen = 1:3 and remains fixed after the quench. Report all energies in temperature units K, i.e. E denotes E/k_B with unit K. The rotational energy levels in these units are E_{l,rot} = A l(l+1), where l is the rotational quantum number and A ≈ 90 K for H2. Vibrational motion is neglected. Parahydrogen corresponds to even l with ground state l=0, while orthohydrogen corresponds to odd l with ground state l=1. Define f_p and f_o as the fixed para and ortho fractions, respectively. Define E_{r,p} and E_{r,o} as the mean rotational energies within the para and ortho subensembles at T_f, and let E_r be the mean rotational energy per molecule. Let E_t be the mean translational energy per molecule. The total internal energy per molecule is E=E_t+E_r, reported in temperature units K. Define β=1/T as the inverse temperature. Find the approximate value of E at T_f, and report the final answer as E=... K.

A monatomic gas consists of atoms with two internal energy levels: a ground state of degeneracy g_1 and a low-lying excited state of degeneracy g_2 at an energy E above the ground state. Let k be the Boltzmann constant and T the absolute temperature. Let epsilon be the average internal energy per atom and let c_v be the specific heat at constant volume per atom. Let E_0 denote a T-independent reference energy of the ground state (e.g., a dissociation energy); choosing the ground state as the zero of energy sets E_0=0, and in any case E_0 does not affect c_v. Find c_v.

Consider a system with two single-particle orbitals, both of the same energy ε. When both orbitals are unoccupied, the system energy is 0. When exactly one of the two orbitals is occupied by one particle, the system energy is ε. Configurations with both orbitals occupied are forbidden and assigned infinite energy. Work in the grand-canonical ensemble with chemical potential μ and absolute temperature T. Let k_B be the Boltzmann constant and define τ = k_B T. Let N denote the number of particles in this degenerate level, so N ∈ {0,1}. Show that the ensemble-average occupancy ⟨N⟩ is
$$
\langle N \rangle = \frac{2}{2 + e^{(\varepsilon - \mu)/\tau}}
$$

Two classical point dipoles with dipole moment vectors M_1 (magnitude M_1) and M_2 (magnitude M_2) are held at a fixed center-to-center separation of magnitude R. Only the orientations of the dipole moments are free to fluctuate. The system is in thermal equilibrium with an environment at absolute temperature T. Let k be the Boltzmann constant and define β=1/(kT). The goal is to compute the mean force F between the dipoles in the high temperature limit M_1 M_2/(k T R^3) ≪ 1. The potential energy between two dipoles separated by displacement vector R (of magnitude R) is given by the classical expression φ=[3(M_1·R)(M_2·R)−(M_1·M_2)R^2]/R^5, where dots denote vector dot products. In the calculation below, the z-axis is chosen along the line connecting the dipoles. The spherical polar angles of M_1 and M_2 relative to that axis are θ_1, φ_1 and θ_2, φ_2, respectively. The solid-angle elements for the two dipoles are dΩ_1 and dΩ_2. The partition function for the orientational degrees of freedom is denoted by z. The mean potential energy is denoted by u, and the mean force magnitude along the line of centers is F, defined by F=−∂u/∂R.

A perfect gas consists of identical rigid diatomic molecules. Each molecule is made of two atoms of mass m, fixed at separation distance d, carrying charges +q and -q, so that the permanent dipole moment has magnitude p_0 = q d and is directed along the intermolecular axis. The gas is placed in a uniform external electric field vector epsilon of magnitude epsilon. Let theta be the angle between the dipole moment and the field. Let k be the Boltzmann constant, T the absolute temperature, and hbar the reduced Planck constant. Define the orientational energy of one molecule as E = -p_0 epsilon cos theta. Define the mean polarization per molecule along the field as p_bar, the orientational mean energy per molecule as E_bar, the per-molecule susceptibility at fixed T as chi = partial p_bar / partial epsilon, and the orientational specific heat per molecule at fixed epsilon as c = partial E_bar / partial T. The solid-angle element is dOmega = sin theta dtheta dphi. Neglect quantum effects unless otherwise stated. Find p_bar as a function of epsilon and T, find chi and c, and state the condition under which the classical (non-quantum) treatment of molecular rotation is valid.

Consider a gas of non-interacting, non-relativistic, identical bosons in either two dimensions or one dimension. In two dimensions the system occupies area A, and in one dimension the system occupies length L. Let N be the total number of particles, m the particle mass, h the Planck constant, k the Boltzmann constant, T the absolute temperature, mu the chemical potential, epsilon the single-particle energy, and l a positive integer index used in series expansions. Use the standard ideal-gas criterion that Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) corresponds to the limiting case mu=0 for the excited-state population at fixed T when the thermodynamic limit is taken. The thermodynamic limit means A→infinity with N/A fixed in two dimensions and L→infinity with N/L fixed in one dimension. Define ans_{2D}=1 if BEC occurs in two dimensions and ans_{2D}=0 otherwise, and define ans_{1D}=1 if BEC occurs in one dimension and ans_{1D}=0 otherwise. Provide the final answers using ans_{2D} and ans_{1D}. Explain whether and why the Bose-Einstein condensation effect that applies to a three-dimensional gas applies also to a two-dimensional gas and to a one-dimensional gas.

Electromagnetic radiation in thermal equilibrium fills a cavity and follows the Planck distribution. Let V denote the instantaneous cavity volume, T the temperature, U the internal energy of the photon gas, p its pressure, and S its entropy. Initially the volume is V_i and the temperature is T_i; the initial spectral energy density per unit angular frequency is u_i(omega), which attains its maximum at angular frequency omega_i. The cavity is then expanded quasistatically and adiabatically to a final volume V_f=2 V_i, reaching a final temperature T_f; the final spectral energy density is u_f(omega) that peaks at omega_f. The photon density of states per angular frequency is D(omega) d omega = a omega^2 d omega with a>0 a constant. The peak of the spectral energy density satisfies omega = gamma T with gamma>0 a constant independent of T. For a photon gas U = 3 p V and p = b T^4 with b>0 a constant. An adiabatic quasistatic process satisfies dS = 0. Define the Planck factor P(omega,T) = 1/[exp(hbar omega/(k T)) - 1], where k is the Boltzmann constant and hbar is the reduced Planck constant. The symbols C_1 and C_2 denote positive constants independent of state. Find omega_f in terms of omega_i for V_f=2 V_i.

A He–Ne laser produces a quasi-monochromatic beam at wavelength λ = 6328\unit{Å}. The beam has output power W = 1\unit{mW}, divergence half-angle dθ = 10^{-4}\unit{rad}, and spectral linewidth dλ = \frac{1}{100}\unit{Å}. If a blackbody with area A = 1\unit{cm^2} is filtered to transmit only this angular and spectral range, determine approximately the temperature T the blackbody must have to generate such a beam. Definitions: c is the speed of light, h is Planck's constant, k is Boltzmann's constant, ε is the photon energy, and dΩ is the solid angle. For a narrow beam with half-angle dθ, the solid angle is dΩ = π(dθ)^2.

Graphite has a layered crystal structure in which the coupling between carbon atoms in different layers is much weaker than the coupling within the same layer. This makes the low-energy phonon spectrum effectively two dimensional. Experimentally, at low absolute temperature T (denoted by τ in some statements; here we will use T), the lattice specific heat C_v scales as a power of T. Using the Debye theory adapted to this quasi-two-dimensional situation, show that C_v ∝ T^2 and determine the proportionality constant. Assumptions and notation to use: consider acoustic phonons with linear dispersion ω = c k for wavevector magnitude k within a layer; let L be the in-plane linear size of the crystal so that the in-plane area is L^2; let c_{\parallel} be the in-plane longitudinal sound speed and c_{\perp} the in-plane transverse sound speed, with two degenerate transverse branches; let g(ω) be the phonon density of states per unit angular frequency; let ω_D be an in-plane Debye cutoff angular frequency; let k_B be Boltzmann's constant and \hbar the reduced Planck constant; and let C_v denote the lattice specific heat at constant volume.

One-Dimensional Debye Solid. Consider a one-dimensional lattice of N identical point particles of mass m, separated by lattice spacing a. Neighboring particles interact via spring-like forces with spring constant m \omega^2. The normal-mode angular frequencies are given by
$$\omega_k = \omega \sqrt{2(1 - \cos ka)}$$
The allowed wave numbers are
$$k = \frac{2\pi n}{aN}$$
where n is an integer in the range from -N/2 to +N/2 and N \gg 1. Using the Debye approximation in one dimension, derive an expression for the quantum-mechanical specific heat at constant length, C_V(T), of this lattice as a function of temperature T. In particular, determine the leading non-zero terms of C_V(T) in the limits T \to \infty and T \to 0. You may introduce and must explicitly define any additional parameters you use, such as the Debye angular frequency \omega_D, the Debye temperature \Theta_D, and the constants \hbar and k_B. For a unified answer format, define C_V^{low}(T) as the leading non-zero asymptotic term of C_V(T) as T \to 0 and C_V^{high}(T) as the leading non-zero asymptotic term of C_V(T) as T \to \infty, and report
$$C_V^{low}(T) = ans_{low}(T)$$
$$C_V^{high}(T) = ans_{high}(T)$$

Calculate the average energy per particle at zero temperature for a three-dimensional ideal Fermi gas. Let T be the temperature, with T=0, and let \varepsilon_F be the Fermi energy. Define the average energy per particle to be \varepsilon_{avg}. Use the following standard definitions: p is the particle momentum, m is the rest mass, c is the speed of light, and D(\varepsilon) is the density of states as a function of the single-particle energy \varepsilon. At T=0, all single-particle states are filled up to \varepsilon_F. Treat separately: (a) the non-relativistic regime p \ll mc with \varepsilon = p^2/(2m) and D(\varepsilon) = A \varepsilon^{1/2}, and (b) the ultra-relativistic regime p \gg mc with \varepsilon = pc and D(\varepsilon) = B \varepsilon^{2}, where A and B are positive constants that cancel in ratios. (UC Berkeley)

Consider a one-dimensional free electron gas confined to a line segment of length L with periodic boundary conditions. Let m be the electron mass, p the electron momentum, h the Planck constant, and ε the single-particle kinetic energy. Electrons are spin-1/2, so each momentum state has a spin degeneracy factor of 2. Define the density of states D(ε) so that D(ε) dε gives the number of single-particle states with energies in the interval [ε, ε + dε], including spin degeneracy. Define the Fermi energy ε_F at zero temperature as the energy up to which all single-particle states are filled for a system containing N electrons. Derive D(ε) as a function of ε and then compute ε_F at zero temperature for N electrons.

Consider a three-dimensional free-electron gas at zero temperature. Let N be the electron number density in units of per volume, m be the electron mass, h be Planck's constant, epsilon be the single-particle energy, and mu_0 be the chemical potential at T = 0 K (the Fermi energy). Let D(epsilon) be the density of states per unit volume including spin degeneracy. Derive mu_0 in terms of N, m, and h at T = 0 K. Then evaluate mu_0 for conduction electrons in a metal with N = 10^{22} electrons per cubic centimeter at T = 0 K. You may also use N = 10^{28} per cubic meter for the same case.

The free-electron model of the conduction electrons in metals, modeled as a three-dimensional non-interacting spin-1/2 Fermi gas, often gives a good account of the compressibility at zero temperature. You are given the number density n and the Fermi energy ε_F of a non-interacting Fermi gas at zero absolute temperature T=0 \unit{K}. Find the isothermal compressibility κ. Definitions and notation: V is the volume, p is the pressure, E is the total energy, F is the Helmholtz free energy, S is the entropy, N is the total particle number, m is the particle mass, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, k is the wavevector magnitude, k_F is the Fermi wavevector, and ε(k) is the single-particle energy. The spin degeneracy factor is 2. Use the identity
$$
p V=\frac{2}{3}E
$$
The isothermal compressibility is defined by
$$
\kappa=-\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial p}\right)_T
$$

Fermi gas. Consider an ideal Fermi gas of neutral atoms with atomic mass m = 5 × 10^{-24} \unit{g}, nuclear spin I = \frac{1}{2}, and nuclear magnetic moment μ = 2 × 10^{-24} \unit{erg/gauss}. The gas is at temperature T = 0 \unit{K} and is placed in a uniform external magnetic field of magnitude H = 10^{5} \unit{gauss}. Assume the electronic magnetic moment is zero. Let n denote the particle number density and ε_F the Fermi energy of a fully spin-polarized gas. Let ħ be the reduced Planck constant and π the circle constant. Find the largest number density n_max for which the gas can be completely polarized by the field. Give your final answer in the form n_max = ... with units of \unit{atoms/cm^3}.

Consider a Fermi gas model of a heavy nucleus. Apart from the Pauli principle, the nucleons are assumed to move independently in a sphere of volume V and form a completely degenerate Fermi gas. Let A be the total number of nucleons with A = N + z, where N is the number of neutrons and z is the number of protons. Assume symmetric nuclear matter N = z. Let m be the nucleon mass, p_F the Fermi momentum, h the Planck constant, and ħ = h/(2π). The nuclear volume is V = (4π/3) R_0^3 A with R_0 ≈ 1.4×10^{-13} \unit{cm}. Use the spin-isospin degeneracy factor 4 for nucleons in symmetric matter, and let E_kin denote the total kinetic energy of all nucleons. Compute the kinetic energy per nucleon E_kin/A with this model and give the result in \unit{MeV}.

Determine the minimum electron number density n at which a uniform, nonrelativistic, fully degenerate gas of free electrons at T=0 \unit{K} has a Fermi energy large enough to permit the reaction proton + electron + 0.8 \unit{MeV} -> neutron to proceed from left to right. Definitions: n is the electron number density, \varepsilon_F is the electron Fermi energy, m is the electron mass, h is Planck's constant, n_{min} is the minimum electron number density that meets the reaction threshold, m_n is the neutron mass, and \rho_{min} is the estimated minimum mass density of a neutron star. Assume one nucleon per electron so that \rho_{min}=m_n n_{min}.

Assume that a neutron star is a highly degenerate, non-relativistic Fermi gas of neutrons in a spherically symmetric equilibrium configuration. The gas is held together by the gravitational pull of a heavy object with mass M and radius r_0 at the center of the star. Neglect all interactions among the neutrons. Let r be the radial distance from the center with r>r_0. Let rho(r) denote the neutron mass density, p(r) the pressure, mu the chemical potential, G the Newtonian gravitational constant, and a>0 the constant in the polytropic equation of state p=a rho^{5/3}. Let C denote the integration constant that appears when integrating the hydrostatic equilibrium equation and that will be fixed by the boundary condition rho(r)→0 as r→∞. Calculate the neutron density rho(r) for r>r_0.

Heat capacity at constant volume. Consider a system in the canonical ensemble with fixed particle number N and volume V at absolute temperature T. Let k be the Boltzmann constant. The system has discrete microstates indexed by n with energies E_n. The canonical partition function is Z, and ensemble averages are denoted by angle brackets ⟨⋯⟩, so that the average energy is ⟨E⟩. The heat capacity at constant volume is defined by C_v=(∂⟨E⟩/∂T)_{N,V}. Using the canonical ensemble with probability weights proportional to exp(−E_n/(kT)), prove that the heat capacity is related to the mean-square fluctuation of the energy by C_v=(1/(k T^2))⟨(E−⟨E⟩)^2⟩. Here E denotes the microstate energy, and all sums run over the microstates n.

Consider an ideal classical (Boltzmann) gas of N indistinguishable particles confined in a volume V at absolute temperature T in the extreme relativistic limit. Each particle has energy epsilon related to the magnitude of its momentum p_mom by epsilon = c p_mom, where c is the speed of light. Let h be Planck's constant, k be Boltzmann's constant, and beta = 1/(k T). Find the pressure p, the entropy S, and the specific heat at constant volume C_V of the gas, expressing each as a function of V, T, and N. Define z as the single-particle partition function and Z as the canonical partition function of the gas.

Consider a dilute ideal gas of heteronuclear diatomic molecules. Each molecule consists of two non-identical atoms, and the moment of inertia about an axis through the molecular center of mass perpendicular to the bond is I. The absolute temperature is T (the original symbol τ is identified with T). The Boltzmann constant is k, the Planck constant is h, and the reduced Planck constant is ħ with ħ = h/(2π). The rotational quantum number is J = 0, 1, 2, ... with degeneracy 2J+1 and energy levels ε_J = (ħ^2/(2I)) J(J+1). For one mole of gas, let N denote the number of molecules in the sample (Avogadro's number). Define the single-molecule rotational partition function z_R, the total rotational partition function for one mole z_R^T, the rotational energy per mole E_R, the rotational constant-volume specific heat per mole C_R, and the rotational entropy per mole S_R. You may use β = 1/(k T). Calculate the rotational contributions C_R and S_R in the two limiting cases k T ≫ ħ^2/I and k T ≪ ħ^2/I, keeping the lowest non-zero order terms in each limit. Express all final answers in terms of I, k, T, h, ħ, and N.

Consider a single electromagnetic mode in a perfectly conducting rectangular cavity. The mode has angular frequency ω and is in thermal equilibrium with a heat bath at temperature T. Let n ∈ {0,1,2,...} denote the photon occupation number of this mode. Angle brackets ⟨⋅⟩ denote thermal averages over the canonical ensemble for this single mode (Bose-Einstein statistics with zero chemical potential). Define the fluctuation Δn = n − ⟨n⟩. Introduce the dimensionless parameter λ = ħ ω / (k T), where ħ is the reduced Planck constant and k is the Boltzmann constant. Let z(λ) be the single-mode partition sum. Find the root-mean-square (rms) fluctuation √⟨(Δn)^2⟩ and determine whether it is smaller than the average photon number ⟨n⟩.

Consider a classical canonical ensemble of N identical point particles, each of mass m, confined to a volume V at absolute temperature T. The position of particle i is r_i in R^3, and r_{ij} = |r_i - r_j| is the distance between particles i and j. The particles interact via the pairwise central potential phi(r_{ij}) = A r_{ij}^{-n} with A > 0 and n > 0. This potential is homogeneous: phi(gamma r) = gamma^{-n} phi(r) for any gamma > 0. Let U denote the total internal energy and p the pressure. Let k be the Boltzmann constant and h the Planck constant. Let Z(T,V) denote the canonical partition function and F(T,V) the Helmholtz free energy. Using scaling arguments applied to the partition function, show that U can be expressed in the form U = a p V + b N k T, where the constants a and b depend only on n. Determine a(n) and b(n).

Consider an ideal helium gas contained in a cube at standard temperature and pressure (STP). The cube has side length L, so the area of one wall is S. Let N denote the number of molecular collisions per second on a single wall. Use the following conventions and definitions: L is the cube side length, S is the wall area with S=L^2, p is the gas pressure at STP, T is the gas temperature at STP, k is the Boltzmann constant, m is the mass of one helium atom, n is the number density given by the ideal-gas relation, and \bar v is the mean molecular speed. Take STP here as p≈1×10^5 Pa and T≈300 K. The cube side length is L=10 cm. Estimate, to order of magnitude, N.

Estimate the mean free path l of a cosmic ray proton in the atmosphere at sea level. Let n be the number density of air molecules with n=N/V=p/(kT), where N is the number of molecules in volume V, p is the atmospheric pressure at sea level, T is the temperature, and k is Boltzmann's constant. Let sigma be the effective nuclear scattering cross section; take sigma=10^{-26} \unit{cm^2}. Report l in centimeters.

Even though there is a high density of electrons in a metal, the mean inter-electron separation r is roughly 1–3 \unit{Å}, yet electron-electron mean free paths are long, with \lambda_{ee} roughly 10^{4} \unit{Å} at room temperature. State reasons for the long electron-electron collision mean free path and give a qualitative argument for its temperature dependence.
